title: Refine migrated Beads graph so triage recommends concrete work
description: Adjust the migrated roadmap graph so bv/br recommend concrete next work instead of umbrella epics.
close_reason: Graph refined so triage recommends concrete work

title: yzx update <any existing package> for standalone updates
description: Imported GitHub issue #459.
close_reason: Not planned: generic imperative single-package updates do not fit Yazelix's current declarative runtime model. The supported update surfaces remain repo/devenv/nix and maintainer-scoped input updates rather than arbitrary package mutation.

title: Bump the Nushell version Yazelix uses
description: Upgrade the Nushell version Yazelix pins and ships, then update the affected install/runtime/bootstrap surfaces and verification so the newer Nushell version is consistently used across flake installs, runtime-owned tools, and maintainer flows.
design: Treat this as a managed-runtime version bump, not just a single pin edit. Update the authoritative Nushell source Yazelix ships and boots through, then verify the newer version is used consistently across the flake/runtime package, bootstrap wrappers, managed shell entrypoints, doctor/version reporting, and maintainer flows. The change should also make any real bootstrap floor or compatibility caveats explicit instead of leaving them implicit in scattered scripts.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The pinned/shipped Nushell version is intentionally bumped in the authoritative runtime source. 2. Flake installs, runtime-owned wrappers, and managed shell entrypoints all resolve to the new Nushell version coherently. 3. Doctor/version reporting and maintainer update flows remain accurate after the bump. 4. Any new bootstrap caveat or minimum-version expectation is documented explicitly instead of surfacing as a surprise failure.

title: Harden the flake install surface
description:
Problem:
The top-level flake install surface exists, but it is still easy to regress because the repo does not yet defend the full install path, some docs remain contradictory, and desktop-entry behavior is not yet an explicit flake-install responsibility.

Design:
Track the remaining flake-specific hardening work as a small focused subtree: smoke validation, docs reconciliation, and explicit desktop-entry installation policy.

Acceptance Criteria:
- The flake install surface has maintained regression coverage.
- User-facing docs describe the flake install path consistently.
- Desktop-entry installation/responsibility is explicitly decided and tracked.
close_reason: Completed initial flake hardening follow-up subtree

title: Add a maintained flake install smoke check
description:
Problem:
The repo can ship flake install regressions without detection. Recent failures affected `nix run github:luccahuguet/yazelix#install` directly.

Design:
Add a focused smoke check that validates the flake install surface end-to-end enough to catch installer/runtime regressions without turning it into a heavy full-session integration suite.

Acceptance Criteria:
- There is a maintained smoke path for the flake install surface.
- The smoke check exercises the installer and verifies the installed `yzx` entrypoint at minimum.
- The check is wired into the appropriate maintainer/CI lane.
close_reason: Implemented flake install smoke validator and wired it into CI

title: Reconcile docs around the current flake install story
description:
Problem:
Some docs still describe flake as removed or otherwise contradict the new `nix run ...#install` front door.

Design:
Audit and fix the remaining contradictory docs so the current install story is internally consistent.

Acceptance Criteria:
- Primary docs no longer contradict the existence of the flake install surface.
- User-facing installation guidance is internally consistent.
close_reason: Reconciled README and troubleshooting docs with the current flake install story

title: Decide desktop entry installation responsibility for the flake installer
description:
Problem:
The flake installer materializes runtime state, but desktop-entry installation is not yet an explicit part of the contract.

Design:
Decide whether the installer should install/update desktop-entry assets directly or whether that remains a separate setup responsibility.

Acceptance Criteria:
- Desktop-entry responsibility is explicitly decided.
- The decision is reflected in docs or implementation follow-up scope.
close_reason: Decided desktop-entry installation stays explicit via yzx desktop install and updated specs/docs accordingly

title: Make Yazelix spec driven
description: Imported GitHub issue #451. Define a spec-driven workflow for new Yazelix features so user-visible behavior has an explicit contract before and during implementation.
close_reason: Completed: the spec-driven workflow epic work is done; the remaining Rust rewrite planning work now continues as standalone bead yazelix-kt5 with matching GitHub issue #461.

title: Pilot the spec-driven workflow on one real Yazelix feature
description: Pick one concrete user-visible feature or bugfix and run it through the proposed spec-driven flow end-to-end: bead, spec, implementation, and regression checks. The goal is to prove the workflow is small enough to use and strong enough to prevent drift before expanding it further.
close_reason: Piloted the spec-driven workflow on shell-opened editors with a concrete spec, user-facing docs, an implementation-side diagnostic clarification, and regression coverage.

title: Define the Yazelix spec template and repository conventions
description: Create the minimal spec format for user-visible Yazelix work. Define where specs live, which changes need one, what sections are required, and how acceptance criteria should be written so specs are precise enough to guide implementation without turning into bureaucracy.
close_reason: Defined the spec-driven workflow, spec location, required sections, acceptance-case rules, and reusable template under docs/spec_driven_workflow.md and docs/specs/template.md

title: Add traceability between specs, beads, and regression tests
description: Make spec-driven development enforceable instead of aspirational. Define a lightweight traceability path from a bead to its spec and from the spec acceptance cases to the tests or checks that defend them. Prefer a small validator or convention check over a heavyweight process engine.
close_reason: Added lightweight traceability between specs, beads, and regression checks through a Traceability section convention plus validate_specs.nu.

title: Tie the default test suite more explicitly to specs and contracts
description: Strengthen the connection between Yazelix's default automated regression suite and the specs/contracts it is supposed to defend. The goal is not heavy process or one-spec-per-test bureaucracy. The goal is to make the small default suite legible: maintainers should be able to see which supported behavior or contract each default-suite entrypoint exists to defend.
design: Keep this lightweight and default-suite-focused. Treat the public default suite as the strongest candidate for explicit spec/contract traceability. At minimum, map the default suite entrypoints to documented specs or explicitly justified regression-only contracts, add a small validator for that relationship, and avoid expanding the requirement to every helper test or validator in the repo. Prefer file-level traceability over per-function bureaucracy. Cheap validators and sweep/manual lanes can keep lighter ownership rules.
acceptance_criteria: The default automated suite entrypoints are explicitly tied to specs, documented contracts, or a small justified regression-only allowlist. Add a lightweight validator that checks the default-suite traceability relationship. Update the relevant specs and/or nearby comments so a maintainer can tell what the default suite is defending without reverse-engineering the test files. The solution should stay lightweight: no requirement for one spec per test function and no heavy metadata system for the whole repo.
close_reason: Completed: added a lightweight validator tying the default test suite to specs or a tiny justified regression-only allowlist, updated specs/comments to make default-suite ownership legible, and verified the default suite plus validators.

title: Make Yazelix config migrations land atomically across managed surfaces
description: Imported GitHub issue #520.
design: Treat this as config-transition atomicity, not merely filesystem atomicity. The goal is to make migrations across Yazelix-managed config surfaces land as one coherent state transition: stage the migrated result, validate it before commit, update related managed config files coherently, and preserve explicit backup/recovery paths. Scope this to Yazelix-owned managed paths such as user_configs and generated state that depends on migrated config, while preserving the existing boundary against taking ownership of external user config.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Yazelix has a clearer staged migration model across managed config surfaces. 2. A migration either preserves the previous valid managed state or lands the new valid managed state coherently, rather than leaving partial cross-surface updates behind. 3. yzx config migrate and startup migration preflight follow the same staged transition model. 4. Backups and failure recovery remain explicit and understandable.
notes: Planning refinement: this work is broad enough to deserve explicit child beads for (1) the staged migration contract, (2) staged apply semantics in yzx config migrate, and (3) reuse of that staged transition model in startup or entrypoint preflight. The goal is to keep the architecture coherent without collapsing design and implementation into one vague task.
close_reason: Completed: child beads 53zx.1, 53zx.2, and 53zx.3 landed the staged transaction contract, staged apply semantics, and entrypoint preflight reuse.

title: Define the staged migration transaction contract for managed config surfaces
description: Specify the staged transaction model for Yazelix-managed config migrations: which managed surfaces participate, where staged output lives, what gets validated before commit, what backup or rollback artifacts are created, and which failures must leave the previous valid state untouched.
design: Keep this contract-focused. Define the exact managed surfaces, staging boundary, validation boundary, backup semantics, and generated-state handling that together make a migration coherent instead of partially applied. Preserve the boundary against touching external non-Yazelix config.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The repo has an explicit staged migration transaction contract for managed config surfaces. 2. The contract names the participating surfaces, staging location or mechanism, validation-before-commit rules, and backup or rollback behavior. 3. The contract is specific enough that both yzx config migrate and startup preflight can follow the same model later without reinterpreting it differently.
close_reason: Completed: added the staged managed-config migration transaction contract and aligned the migration engine spec with it.

title: Implement staged apply semantics in yzx config migrate
description: Replace the direct-write backup/copy-back path in `apply_config_migration_plan` with a narrow managed-config transaction helper that stages rewritten TOML payloads and a JSON manifest under `user_configs`, validates the staged main/pack set, commits via same-directory rename, and rolls back cleanly on failure or stale journal recovery. Even though this bead is framed around `yzx config migrate --apply`, the helper is already shared by doctor and entrypoint call sites, so the implementation must preserve those callers while keeping the explicit migrate path as the main proving UX.
design:
Delete-first:
- do not invent a generic file-transaction framework
- do not broaden the surface beyond the canonical managed config pair

Implementation seam:
- extract a small helper, likely under `nushell/scripts/utils/`, that owns `prepare -> validate -> commit -> rollback` for the canonical managed surfaces
- have `apply_config_migration_plan` delegate to it instead of writing final targets directly

First implementation should:
- stage rewritten TOML payloads in the same `user_configs` filesystem as their final targets
- persist a hidden JSON manifest with `transaction_id`, `schema_version`, `caller`, `phase`, target, staged, backup, and `existed_before` metadata
- validate the staged final pair with the same config-surface loading rules before any canonical target swap
- preserve explicit backup files as the rollback source of truth
- clean up staged files and manifest on success
- on a subsequent explicit migrate apply, detect any stale manifest and roll back conservatively before starting a new transaction

Keep out of scope for this bead:
- startup/entrypoint recovery wiring beyond what already shares `apply_config_migration_plan`
- generic transaction support for runtime state, desktop entries, shell hooks, or external configs
- forward-completion heuristics for interrupted transactions
acceptance_criteria:
1. `apply_config_migration_plan` no longer writes canonical targets directly with `save --force` followed by copy-back rollback.
2. Main-only and main-plus-pack migrations are staged, validated, and committed coherently through the managed-config transaction helper.
3. Safe migrations that create a new `yazelix_packs.toml` sidecar record that the file did not exist before and remove it again on rollback.
4. `yzx config migrate --apply` detects and rolls back an unfinished managed-config transaction before starting a new apply attempt.
5. Successful apply leaves backups in place but removes staged files and manifest artifacts.
6. High-signal regression coverage exists for at least one interrupted or failing apply path, and the existing migrate/doctor fix behaviors continue to pass.
close_reason: Completed: staged yzx config migrate apply through a managed-config transaction helper with stale-journal recovery and regression coverage.

title: Finish reusing the staged migration transaction model in entrypoint preflight and legacy-root relocation
description: 53zx.2 moved deterministic rewrites onto the shared staged transaction engine, but the startup and entrypoint family still has one important ad hoc path left: legacy root-level config relocation is still performed before the transaction boundary. This bead should finish that integration so entrypoint migration preflight uses one coherent prepare, recover, and apply model for both deterministic rewrites and any managed-surface relocation it owns, instead of mixing staged writes with separate pre-transaction moves.
design:
Keep this bead narrow and entrypoint-focused. Start from nushell/scripts/utils/entrypoint_config_migrations.nu, nushell/scripts/core/start_yazelix.nu, nushell/scripts/core/launch_yazelix.nu, nushell/scripts/yzx/launch.nu, and nushell/scripts/yzx/env.nu.

Delete-first boundary:
- do not build a second transaction engine for startup
- do not reopen yzx config migrate apply or doctor semantics beyond shared helper reuse
- do not broaden into generic config-surface ownership work already covered elsewhere

The real remaining seam is that build_preflight_context currently performs legacy-root relocation through reconcile_primary_config_surfaces before the staged transaction lifecycle begins. That means entrypoint preflight already reuses staged apply for deterministic TOML rewrites, but not yet for the full managed-surface transition it is responsible for.

The implementation should either:
1. move legacy-root relocation under the same staged prepare, recover, and apply boundary as entrypoint migration preflight, or
2. introduce one small shared helper that truthfully wraps relocation plus staged apply as one managed-surface transition contract for entrypoints.

Keep the caller distinction explicit:
- entrypoint_preflight may commit safe deterministic changes and relocation
- it may still block launch afterward if manual-only items remain
- interrupted transaction recovery should remain conservative and rollback-first

High-signal code seams to review:
- nushell/scripts/utils/entrypoint_config_migrations.nu
- nushell/scripts/utils/config_migration_transactions.nu
- nushell/scripts/utils/config_migrations.nu
- nushell/scripts/utils/config_surfaces.nu
- existing entrypoint regressions in nushell/scripts/dev/test_yzx_core_commands.nu
acceptance_criteria:
1. Entry-point migration preflight no longer relies on an ad hoc legacy-root relocation step outside the shared managed-config transition model.
2. Safe deterministic rewrites and managed legacy-root relocation for the entrypoint family use one coherent prepare, recover, and apply lifecycle, or one clearly shared wrapper around that lifecycle.
3. Startup and entrypoint code does not maintain a second incompatible migration-commit model beside yzx config migrate apply.
4. Entry-point behavior remains explicit: safe deterministic changes may commit, but remaining manual-only items still block continuation afterward.
5. There is at least one regression that defends the remaining seam this bead closes, not just the already-covered happy path.
notes: Current state after 53zx.2: entrypoint preflight already reuses staged apply for deterministic rewrites and already recovers interrupted staged transactions. The main remaining mismatch with the transaction spec is legacy-root relocation happening early in build_preflight_context via reconcile_primary_config_surfaces, before the staged transaction boundary. Existing tests already cover auto-apply, manual-follow-up blocking, and basic relocation; this bead should focus on the still-unified transition boundary rather than re-testing generic migration behavior.
close_reason: Implemented entrypoint relocation through the shared managed-config transaction lifecycle and added regressions for stale recovery ordering and pack-only relocation reporting.

title: Add optional yazelix_packs.toml sidecar config
description: Add an optional ~/.config/yazelix/yazelix_packs.toml sidecar so dense pack configuration can move out of yazelix.toml without removing any existing pack capability or turning packs into a folder forest.
design: Treat this as a configuration-clarity feature, not a pack redesign. Keep yazelix.toml as the front door and add yazelix_packs.toml as an optional structured sidecar for denser pack enablement and pack-specific settings. Preserve all current pack features; the sidecar must not remove any capability currently expressible in yazelix.toml. Define one sharp ownership rule so users are never left wondering which file wins: either the sidecar owns dense pack config while yazelix.toml keeps only simple top-level references, or Yazelix fails fast when the same pack keys are declared in both places. Prefer explicit duplicate-source errors over silent precedence surprises. Keep this limited to packs; do not turn it into a generic many-file config explosion. Because packs are supported through both direct config and Home Manager today, the sidecar design must preserve a coherent story for both paths.
acceptance_criteria: Yazelix supports an optional yazelix_packs.toml without regressing existing pack behavior from yazelix.toml. The final design clearly documents what pack data belongs in each file, how duplicate or conflicting definitions are handled, and how the feature works for both direct config users and Home Manager users. Add focused unit tests for sidecar discovery, parse or merge behavior, duplicate-source errors, and pack-resolution behavior. Add end-to-end tests with detailed logs for main-file-only pack config, sidecar-only pack config, a conflicting dual-definition case that fails clearly, a direct-config path, a Home Manager path, and a representative generated-config run showing the resolved pack set and emitted tooling or config output.
close_reason: Completed

title: Define Yazelix Nushell tooling strategy
description: Umbrella for deciding how Yazelix should package and govern Nushell-specific maintainer tooling. Scope includes a dedicated Nushell pack, nufmt formatting policy, and nu-lint lint policy. Keep tool availability separate from enforcement: shipping tools in an opt-in pack must not by itself decide canonical formatter status, maintainer workflows, or CI gates.
design: Reason from first principles: Yazelix needs a coherent Nushell-tooling story, not an ad hoc accumulation of commands. There are three separate questions that should not be collapsed into one: 1. what Nushell-specific tools belong in a first-party opt-in pack; 2. whether nufmt becomes the canonical formatter and what rollout it should have; and 3. whether nu-lint is repo-compatible enough to justify any maintained command surface. The parent bead should stay an umbrella for those planning decisions rather than directly implementing a single tool addition.
acceptance_criteria: The subtree under this bead cleanly separates pack composition, formatting policy, and lint policy; each child bead has an explicit boundary and success criteria; and maintainers can make progress on one area without accidentally forcing the others. The parent can close once the dedicated pack direction and the nufmt and nu-lint policy decisions are each resolved.
close_reason: Completed: all three strategy decisions resolved — Nushell pack shipped, nufmt rejected, nu-lint adopted as maintainer command.

title: Add a dedicated Nushell tooling pack
description: Create a dedicated Nushell pack instead of overloading the generic maintainer pack. Initial scope should include nufmt and should consider nu-lint based on the outcome of the repo-fit evaluation. The pack bead is about package availability and ergonomics, not about forcing formatter or linter enforcement by itself.
design: Keep pack composition separate from policy enforcement. A first-party opt-in Nushell pack is valuable even if nu-lint remains experimental or nufmt adoption is staged. The pack should stay small and purposeful: prefer clear Nushell authoring and hygiene tools over a grab-bag of loosely related utilities. Any change here must preserve the normal Yazelix config model by keeping the pack available through both the pack sidecar/default config path and the Home Manager module, without creating a second-class configuration path.
acceptance_criteria: A dedicated Nushell pack exists in both Yazelix config surfaces, with justified initial contents and no ambiguity about what the pack is for. The pack can be enabled through direct config and Home Manager. Its contents do not silently imply CI enforcement or canonical formatter/linter status beyond what the separate policy beads decide.
close_reason: Completed

title: Evaluate nufmt and define Yazelix Nushell formatting policy
description: Decide whether nufmt should be the canonical formatter for Yazelix Nushell code, define the formatting policy and command surface, and determine how adoption should happen: repo-wide reformat, touched-files-only rollout, or rejection. This bead owns formatter policy, migration story, and enforcement decisions, not just tool installation.
design: The important question is not merely whether nufmt exists, but whether Yazelix wants one authoritative Nushell formatting policy. Resolve formatter ambiguity explicitly: if nufmt is adopted, decide whether it becomes the canonical formatter, where it is invoked from, and whether maintainers are expected to reformat the whole repo or only touched files. The rollout should preserve review quality and avoid accidental churn. If nufmt is rejected or deferred, capture the reasons so the repo does not drift into an undocumented quasi-policy.
acceptance_criteria: There is an explicit maintainers-facing decision on nufmt: adopt, defer, or reject. If adopted, the bead records the canonical invocation path, the enforcement lane if any, and the migration strategy for existing files. If deferred or rejected, the reasons and blocking concerns are captured clearly enough to prevent accidental partial adoption.
notes: Decision: do not adopt nufmt for Yazelix right now because the current formatter output was observed to rewrite at least one real Yazelix Nushell file into invalid syntax.
close_reason: Closed: current nufmt repo-fit evaluation found unsafe formatter output on real Yazelix Nushell files, so Yazelix is not adopting a Nushell formatting policy around nufmt right now

title: Evaluate nu-lint and define Yazelix Nushell lint policy
description: Evaluate nu-lint against the Yazelix codebase, identify incompatible or noisy rules, and decide what role it deserves: pack-only availability, editor/LSP assistance, explicit maintainer command, pre-commit lane, CI lane, or rejection. This bead owns repo-fit evaluation and lint policy, not just checking that the tool exists.
design: Judge nu-lint by repo fit, but optimize for extracting real value rather than defaulting to a timid pack-only posture. Current evidence says the upstream default rule set is too noisy for blind enforcement: on real Yazelix files it reports parse errors for normal relative `use` imports, and several prominent rules conflict with repo conventions or priorities, especially `kebab_case_commands`, `add_doc_comment_exported_fn`, `missing_output_type`, and broad style rules like `string_may_be_bare`. Pack inclusion is already solved; this bead should now determine the highest-value maintained role nu-lint can play in Yazelix. That may be editor/LSP use, an explicit maintainer command, a tuned pre-commit lane, or a narrower CI lane, but only with a repo-specific config that disables or scopes noisy rules. Do not assume upstream defaults are acceptable, but do aggressively explore whether a constrained rule set yields strong signal.
acceptance_criteria: There is an explicit decision on nu-lints role in Yazelix that aims for maximum useful signal, not minimum ambition. The bead records the concrete repo-fit blockers already found: relative-import parse noise, rule conflicts with underscore-oriented naming, and high-churn style/documentation warnings. If adoption is chosen beyond pack availability, the bead defines the exact lane, the repo-specific config or disabled-rule policy, and why that scoped rule set is worth the maintenance cost. If broader adoption is rejected, the bead explains why no high-value narrowed configuration was good enough.
notes: Evaluation complete. Raw nu-lint produces 8565 violations (dominated by noise: string_may_be_bare 2466, kebab_case_commands 845, missing_output_type 784, nu_parse_error 433 from relative imports). With a curated .nu-lint.toml disabling 10 groups and ~10 noisy individual rules, output drops to ~170 findings of genuine signal: unsafe_dynamic_record_access (49), do_not_compare_booleans (23), unused_parameter (19), check_typed_flag_before_use (18), unchecked_get_index (11), non_final_failure_check (11), unused_helper_functions (10), unused_variable (9), assign_then_return (6), fragile_last_exit_code (4), unchecked_cell_path_index (2). Decision: adopt nu-lint as an explicit maintainer command (yzx dev lint_nu) with a repo-specific .nu-lint.toml. Not CI-gated because relative import parse errors are a structural upstream false-positive blocker. Not pre-commit because maintainer-triggered is the right ergonomic fit. redundant_nu_subprocess (39) disabled because all hits are intentional process-isolation patterns in test harnesses, menu dispatch, and script runners. Deliverables: .nu-lint.toml at repo root, yzx dev lint_nu command.
close_reason: Completed: nu-lint adopted as maintainer command (yzx dev lint_nu) with repo-specific .nu-lint.toml. Policy decision documented.

title: Revert Yazi to default theme
close_reason: Fixed on main in ba937e4 (Yazi default theme restored to upstream default)

title: Define a supply-chain hardening policy for Yazelix tool surfaces
description: Define a lightweight supply-chain hardening policy for Yazelix so default-installed tool surfaces stay low-risk, optional higher-risk packs stay explicitly scoped, and future incidents have a repeatable triage and communication workflow.
design: Keep this lightweight and policy-first. The goal is not exhaustive auditing of every upstream tool; it is a practical safety model for Yazelix's maintained surfaces. Define the risk tiers for default-installed tools versus opt-in packs, prefer hermetic Nix-managed runtime paths over host package managers, require a small review checklist before enabling new default tools, and define a repeatable incident-response workflow for future supply-chain advisories. The result should help maintainers decide when a tool can be default-enabled, when it must remain opt-in, and how to communicate impact quickly when a dependency incident happens.
acceptance_criteria: Yazelix has a short documented policy describing: 1. what package/tool surfaces Yazelix treats as default, opt-in, or higher-risk; 2. review criteria for enabling new default tools; 3. preferred runtime/update paths and discouraged package-manager paths; and 4. a repeatable incident triage workflow for future supply-chain advisories. The policy should give maintainers enough guidance to classify a new tool addition or a future supply-chain incident without ad-hoc reasoning each time.

title: Add Yazelix-managed per-shell user hook surfaces
description: Add explicit Yazelix-owned user hook files for managed shell startup instead of sourcing personal dotfiles in managed panes. Scope includes bash, zsh, fish, and nushell.
design: Preferred design: add optional per-shell hook files under ~/.config/yazelix/user_configs/shells/ (bash.sh, zsh.zsh, fish.fish, nu.nu) and source them at the end of each Yazelix-managed shell startup path. Managed panes and Yazelix env sessions stay self-contained by default; user hooks are an explicit extension surface inside Yazelix ownership, not personal dotfile sourcing. Do not auto-copy or auto-source ~/.bashrc, ~/.zshrc, ~/.config/fish/config.fish, or ~/.config/nushell/* for managed panes. Keep the order strict: runtime env, Yazelix initializers, managed shell config, then optional Yazelix user hook. Nushell should use a managed user hook file in the same spirit as the new managed pane wrapper, not the personal env.nu/config.nu path. Prefer one shared resolver/helper for shell hook file lookup rather than duplicated per-shell path logic.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Managed Yazelix shell startup for bash, zsh, fish, and nu can source an optional Yazelix-owned user hook file from ~/.config/yazelix/user_configs/shells/. 2. Managed panes do not source personal shell dotfiles by default. 3. The feature does not reintroduce the pane-latency problem by importing large personal shell setups implicitly. 4. Missing user hook files are a normal no-op, not an error. 5. Tests defend at least one generated/runtime path and one startup path for the new shell hook contract.
notes: UX reasoning: users still need a place for aliases/functions/prompt tweaks inside Yazelix, but personal shell startup is too broad, slow, and surprising for managed panes. The explicit Yazelix-owned hook surface preserves self-containment while giving advanced users a clear extension point.
close_reason: Completed: managed per-shell user hook surfaces now exist for bash, zsh, fish, and nushell; the remaining stub-scaffolding follow-up stays in yazelix-5yg1.1.

title: Scaffold visible stubs for Yazelix-managed user config surfaces
description: Create lightweight empty/commented stubs for relevant Yazelix-managed user config surfaces so users can discover where customizations belong without reading docs first.
design: Scope this to lightweight, self-explanatory scaffolding for Yazelix-owned user config surfaces under ~/.config/yazelix/user_configs/. Prefer creating stubs automatically when a surface becomes relevant or is first managed, not a giant first-run dump of empty files. For shells, likely create ~/.config/yazelix/user_configs/shells/ plus small commented stubs. For other managed surfaces, favor one-line comment stubs or empty directories only when they materially improve discoverability. These are user-owned runtime files under ~/.config/yazelix, not tracked repo files and not personal dotfiles outside Yazelix ownership.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Relevant Yazelix-managed user config surfaces are auto-created under ~/.config/yazelix/user_configs/ when they become relevant. 2. Scaffolding stays lightweight and does not create a noisy pile of unused files by default. 3. Stub files clearly say they are Yazelix-managed extension points. 4. No personal config files outside ~/.config/yazelix/ are created or modified by this feature. 5. The implementation does not add tracked repo-state pollution for these user-owned stubs.

title: Create repo-specific .nu-lint.toml config
description: Ship a .nu-lint.toml at repo root that disables 10 noisy groups and ~10 individual rules, keeping ~15 high-signal rules for dead code, runtime safety, and correctness.
close_reason: Shipped .nu-lint.toml with curated config: 11 groups disabled, 13 noisy rules suppressed, ~15 high-signal rules retained. Verified: 205 findings, all genuine signal.

title: Document Alt+m cwd regression spiral after refactor
description: Record the regression sequence where Alt+m pane creation was refactored away from the known-good orchestrator path and reintroduced the Zellij cwd bug, leading to panes opening in HOME, stale sidebar-state confusion, and crashing helper-pane experiments.
design: Keep this as a postmortem-style regression bead linked conceptually to yazelix-2ex.1.7.5. The key invariant is that Yazelix must resolve the live sidebar cwd in Nushell and delegate pane creation to the pane orchestrator via open_terminal_in_cwd. Refactors must not replace that path with zellij new-pane/run --cwd helpers, explicit shell launches, or command panes. Preserve the later good fixes from this incident: immediate sidebar-state cache refresh after open-from-Yazi sync, pruning stale sidebar session files, and avoiding launcher cwd poisoning from ~/.config/yazelix.
acceptance_criteria: The bead notes capture the failure chain, the known-good contract, and the forbidden fallback paths. Future work touching Alt+m or pane cwd should be able to use this bead to avoid repeating the regression.
notes: Regression chain on 2026-03-15: a refactor replaced the orchestrator-owned Alt+m pane-open flow from a7b5716/yazelix-2ex.1.7.5 with helper-driven zellij new-pane --cwd and later zellij run --cwd shell launches. Symptoms included panes opening in /home/lucca or ~/.config/yazelix despite correct target resolution, stale sidebar-state files masking the real issue, helper crashes, and a wider session cwd spiral after layout churn. Confirmed root cause remained the upstream Zellij cwd bug already documented in zellij-org/zellij#3068. Final recovery: restore open_workspace_terminal.nu to call run_pane_orchestrator_command_raw "open_terminal_in_cwd" with the resolved sidebar/workspace cwd, keep sidebar-state freshness fixes, and treat the orchestrator path as the only supported Alt+m implementation.
close_reason: Documented the Alt+m regression spiral, the repeated Zellij cwd bug trigger, and the required orchestrator-owned recovery path.

title: Define the Yazelix backend contract before evaluating alternative runtime backends
description:
Define the Yazelix backend capability contract before doing deeper backend experiments. The point is to stop using “backend” as a vague synonym for “env manager” and instead write down the concrete capabilities Yazelix currently needs from its runtime/environment layer.

This contract should classify at least these capability families:
- runtime materialization of the shipped/default Yazelix tool stack
- stable install/update ownership and user-facing entrypoint identity
- env/run/shell entrypoints for commands like `yzx env`, `yzx run`, and launch re-entry
- cached profile or reusable activation semantics, including refresh/invalidation rules
- runtime-owned binary and asset resolution, not just generic PATH lookup
- generated runtime/state ownership and how it relates to the backend
- platform/package availability expectations and which current conveniences are actually optional

The goal is not to bless a new backend yet. The goal is to produce a contract concrete enough to evaluate current full Yazelix and a future Nix-only `Yazelix Core` flake candidate without redefining the criteria midstream. Later optional backend experiments should be able to reuse the same contract instead of shaping it.
design:
Treat this as contract/spec-first work, not implementation work. Build directly on `docs/architecture_map.md` plus the existing runtime/install/config contracts: `runtime_root_contract.md`, `flake_interface_contract.md`, `one_command_install_ux.md`, and `config_surface_and_launch_profile_contract.md`.

Ground the contract in the real code seams that currently depend on the backend layer:
- `nushell/scripts/utils/devenv_cli.nu`
- `nushell/scripts/utils/environment_bootstrap.nu`
- `nushell/scripts/utils/launch_state.nu`
- `nushell/scripts/utils/config_state.nu`
- `nushell/scripts/yzx/env.nu`
- `nushell/scripts/yzx/run.nu`
- `nushell/scripts/yzx/launch.nu`
- `nushell/scripts/yzx/refresh.nu`

The written contract should produce:
1. a capability matrix with at least these columns:
   - full Yazelix today
   - possible Nix-only Core backend
2. a required vs optional vs explicitly-out-of-scope classification for each capability bucket
3. a clear boundary between backend capability requirements and launch-time dependency/preflight checks
4. a clear boundary between backend capability requirements and the eventual product/support boundary for `Yazelix Core`

Optional later backend experiments, such as the Pixi lane, should be evaluated against this contract after it exists instead of influencing the contract definition itself.

Important constraint: this bead is not where launch-time “is Helix/Zellij/Ghostty installed?” checks are defined. That belongs to `yazelix-j4qv`. This bead is about what a backend must make possible for Yazelix as a product at all.
acceptance_criteria:
There is a written backend contract for Yazelix that groups the real backend surface into explicit capability buckets instead of an abstract “backend” label. Each bucket is marked as required, optional, or out of scope for backend evaluation.

The contract is concrete enough to evaluate at least:
- current `devenv`-backed full Yazelix
- a future Nix-only, no-`devenv`, opinionated flake candidate for `Yazelix Core`

The contract explicitly covers the current hard seams visible in code today:
- runtime-owned CLI/tool resolution
- install/update ownership and stable entrypoint identity
- env/run/rebuild/re-entry entrypoints
- cached profile reuse and invalidation semantics
- relationship between backend materialization and generated runtime/state artifacts

The contract also explicitly states what it does *not* own:
- user-facing launch preflight / dependency checker behavior (`yazelix-j4qv`)
- the final `Yazelix Core` product boundary (`yazelix-qv8c`)
- implementation of a new backend

A later bead should be able to use this contract to evaluate candidate backends without moving the goalposts or rediscovering requirements from scattered code.
notes:
Evidence already points to a broader backend surface than “can spawn a shell”:
- `devenv_cli.nu` and `environment_bootstrap.nu` show that Yazelix currently expects preferred runtime-owned CLI resolution plus rebuild/re-entry control.
- `config_state.nu` and `launch_state.nu` show that cached profile reuse and invalidation are part of the backend story, not just a UX optimization.
- `yzx env`, `yzx run`, `yzx launch`, and `yzx refresh` show that the backend currently owns both activation and rebuild semantics.
- `flake_interface_contract.md`, `one_command_install_ux.md`, and `flake.nix` show that install/update identity and stable runtime pointers are part of the backend boundary too.

The likely delete-first outcome is that some things people casually bundle into “backend” will turn out to be:
- launch preflight concerns
- product-boundary concerns
- or optional conveniences rather than hard backend requirements.
close_reason: Backend capability contract written, linked into architecture docs, and aligned with issue #538.

title: Move user-owned config under user_configs with terminal subdir
description: Move user-owned Yazelix config into a dedicated user_configs boundary. End-state: all Yazelix-managed user inputs live under ~/.config/yazelix/user_configs/ (including yazelix.toml, yazelix_packs.toml, terminal/, yazi/, and zellij/), while generated runtime state lives under ~/.local/share/yazelix/. Keep tracked defaults at the repo/config root. Managed mode should not implicitly read external native tool config from unrelated XDG paths; native tool config should only participate as an explicit import/migration source or escape hatch. Keep the system self-contained, coherent for users, and friendly to a future first-party Yazelix flake. Do not auto-scaffold optional user-owned files by default. Add only one minimal guide file at user_configs/README.md rather than a README in every subfolder.
close_reason: Completed

title: Add a self-validating desktop-entry freshness contract
description: Define a Yazelix desktop-entry contract that carries enough metadata to detect when the installed desktop file is stale relative to the current installed runtime, instead of relying only on indirect path drift checks.
design: Prefer explicit desktop-entry metadata over heuristic-only staleness checks. Store a runtime-target identifier in the desktop file, such as the runtime/current target path or a derived install-generation token. Keep the contract readable and stable enough for both yzx doctor and the desktop launcher to consume.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The desktop entry format includes a Yazelix-owned freshness field. 2. The chosen field is strong enough to distinguish a current runtime from a stale one without relying only on version strings. 3. The contract is documented clearly enough for both doctor and launcher-side validation.

title: Refactor the stable CLI into a leaf-command dispatcher for latency-sensitive commands
description: Refactor the stable Yazelix command-entrypoint path so latency-sensitive leaf commands do not pay the full `core/yazelix.nu` import cost by default. Recent performance work already exposed the pattern twice: popup and reveal both became much faster once their hot path stopped importing the monolithic core command suite. This bead turns that from ad hoc fast paths into a cleaner command-dispatch boundary for small interactive commands.
design: Treat this as a narrow pre-Rust architecture cleanup, not a giant CLI rewrite. Keep `core/yazelix.nu` as an aggregator and rich command-development surface, but stop treating it as the default hot path for every small interactive command. The immediate work is to identify which leaf commands are genuinely latency-sensitive, route them through direct module entrypoints by default, and leave slower/heavier commands on the core path until there is evidence they need more. Current measured evidence already justifies this for `reveal`, `popup`, and likely `menu`: full core import is about 2.05s locally, while `yzx/popup.nu` imports around 0.09s, `yzx/menu.nu` around 0.11s, and `integrations/reveal_in_yazi.nu` around 0.06s. The right outcome is a small dispatcher boundary around the stable CLI and runtime wrappers, not a speculative generated registry system yet. This should make the later Rust rewrite cleaner by clarifying command-entrypoint ownership first instead of porting the current monolithic hot path as-is.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The stable CLI and relevant runtime wrappers have an explicit fast path for the small interactive commands that are proven latency-sensitive. 2. At least one remaining hot candidate beyond the already-fixed popup/reveal path is migrated through that cleaner dispatcher boundary and measured before/after. 3. The repo has one authoritative place to decide whether a command uses a leaf entrypoint or the full core command suite, instead of growing ad hoc wrapper exceptions forever. 4. Add focused wrapper-behavior regressions for the new dispatcher paths without adding weak implementation-string tests.
notes: Current local evidence: `use nushell/scripts/core/yazelix.nu *` imports in about 2.05s, `use nushell/scripts/yzx/menu.nu *` in about 0.11s, and the lightweight reveal wrapper import in about 0.06s. `configs/zellij/scripts/yzx_menu_popup.nu` is the clearest remaining hot-path candidate because it still imports `core/yazelix.nu` just to run `yzx menu`.
close_reason: Completed

title: Speed up desktop-entry launch by bypassing monolithic CLI and core launch imports
description: The desktop launcher path is still paying the full stable-CLI and core-launch bootstrap when opened from the generated desktop entry. Current measurements show `~/.local/bin/yzx desktop launch --help` takes about 2.07s, while importing `nushell/scripts/yzx/desktop.nu` directly takes about 0.025s and `nushell/scripts/core/desktop_launcher.nu` about 0.057s. The generated desktop file currently executes `~/.local/bin/yzx desktop launch`, which falls through the full `core/yazelix.nu` path, and `desktop_launcher.nu` then shells back into `use .../core/yazelix.nu *; yzx launch --home` instead of calling the leaf launch module directly.
design: Treat this as a narrow child of the leaf-dispatcher architecture bead, not a separate launcher subsystem rewrite. The desktop file should keep using the stable `yzx` binary, but the stable CLI should leaf-dispatch the `desktop` command family directly to `nushell/scripts/yzx/desktop.nu`. Inside that family, `yzx desktop launch` should keep using the installed runtime and desktop-launcher entrypoint, but `desktop_launcher.nu` should call `nushell/scripts/yzx/launch.nu` directly instead of re-importing `core/yazelix.nu`. Keep the shared launch semantics, preflight, and terminal dispatch owned by `yzx/launch.nu`; only remove redundant aggregator overhead.
acceptance_criteria:
1. `~/.local/bin/yzx desktop launch --help` no longer pays the full `core/yazelix.nu` import path by default.
2. `desktop_launcher.nu` launches through `yzx/launch.nu` directly rather than routing through `core/yazelix.nu`.
3. The installed desktop entry still works and preserves the current installed-runtime contract.
4. Add at least one focused regression for the lightweight desktop dispatcher path without adding weak implementation-string tests.
5. Measure before/after and keep the local evidence in the bead notes or commit context.
notes: Local evidence before starting: `~/.local/bin/yzx desktop launch --help` ~2.07s, `use nushell/scripts/yzx/desktop.nu *; help 'yzx desktop launch'` ~0.025s, `use nushell/scripts/core/desktop_launcher.nu *; help main` ~0.057s, and `use nushell/scripts/yzx/launch.nu *; help 'yzx launch'` ~0.424s.
close_reason: Completed

title: Document and validate Zellij session sharing with Yazelix
description: Investigate how Zellij session sharing works in practice for Yazelix users, including whether Yazelix makes the process easier, harder, or just different compared with vanilla Zellij. The main sharing paths to verify are Zellij's built-in web client and ordinary attach/share flows for existing sessions.
design: Start as a docs-and-validation task grounded in the official Zellij sharing model. Zellij now has a built-in web server and web client that can be started with `zellij web` or through the share plugin, and authenticated with one-time login tokens. The default local URL scheme and token flow should be verified in a Yazelix session. Also verify ordinary multi-client session attach flows and session resurrection/share behavior. Then check how Yazelix interacts with these flows: shared Yazelix environments, shared devenv profiles, plugin/runtime expectations, persistent session naming, and whether the current keybinding model makes the share plugin discoverable enough. If Yazelix creates friction, consider small helper behavior or targeted docs from the Yazelix side to smooth it out.
acceptance_criteria: There is accurate documentation for sharing Zellij sessions with Yazelix, grounded in verified behavior rather than guesswork. Any Yazelix-specific helper or caveat is documented clearly. Leave behind a repeatable validation script or checklist with detailed logs covering the tested sharing path, authentication or web-client steps, and any plugin or session caveats that users need to know.
notes: Official Zellij sources to anchor this work: Web client tutorial, web client user guide, and the 0.43.0 release notes about browser sharing. The docs note that the web client is useful for remote access, demos, pair programming, and collaborative debugging. They also recommend HTTPS for anything beyond local-only use.

title: Add desktop-entry staleness detection to yzx doctor
description: Teach yzx doctor to detect when an installed Yazelix desktop entry exists but points at a missing runtime or a runtime different from ~/.local/share/yazelix/runtime/current, and recommend the exact repair command.

title: Audit large files and identify worthwhile refactors
description: Inspect Yazelix's largest or most complex files and decide which ones deserve real refactoring versus which ones are large for legitimate reasons. The goal is to create a justified shortlist, not to split files reflexively.
design: Use delete-first reasoning rather than assuming every large file is bad. Consider file size, complexity, churn, ownership boundaries, and whether the file is hard to understand or hard to test. Prefer identifying a small number of high-value refactors with concrete boundaries over creating a blanket mandate to break up large files everywhere.
acceptance_criteria: There is a ranked shortlist of large files that are worth refactoring, with concrete reasons and suggested seams. Files that are large but acceptable are explicitly called out as such. Follow-up implementation beads are narrower than this audit umbrella.
notes: Promote this audit. Current repo direction is to reduce LOC and complexity before packaging. Large-file review should identify a small number of genuinely high-value refactors and explicitly reject reflexive file splitting where the current size is justified.

title: Split oversized ownership-heavy modules by subsystem contracts
description: Turn the large-file pain in devenv.nix, home_manager/module.nix, zellij_config_merger.nu, and integrations/zellij.nu into a contract-first decomposition plan. The goal is not to reduce line count cosmetically; it is to stop single files from carrying several subsystem owners at once.
design: Start from ownership boundaries, not helper extraction. Group code by stable subsystem contracts such as config-contract consumers, runtime launch state, terminal wrapper policy, plugin protocol client, and Zellij render model. Prefer deleting mixed-responsibility paths and moving responsibilities behind clearer modules over creating a wider helper jungle. The decomposition plan should identify which slices are real subsystem boundaries and which apparent splits would just shuffle complexity around.
acceptance_criteria: 1. There is a concrete split plan for the worst mixed-responsibility files. 2. The plan groups future slices by subsystem ownership rather than arbitrary helper extraction. 3. The plan identifies deletions/simplifications as well as extractions. 4. The result narrows future refactor beads into explicit contract-owned slices instead of generic 'break up big file' work.

title: General movement should skip the managed sidebar
description: Adjust general movement/navigation so the managed sidebar is skipped by default. Users should reach the sidebar explicitly through sidebar-focused commands like Ctrl+y, not by incidental workspace movement.
design: After sidebar focus behavior is made correct, consider making general movement treat the sidebar as skippable by default, but only if it falls out naturally from the current Zellij/plugin model. The sidebar should still be reachable through explicit sidebar commands, but ordinary pane movement should prioritize the main workspace panes. If this requires brittle special cases, layout-specific hacks, or substantial new focus state, it is not worth the complexity.
acceptance_criteria: General movement no longer lands on the managed sidebar unless the user uses an explicit sidebar-targeting action, but only if the implementation is straightforward and low-complexity. Add focused tests for the affected movement bindings if implemented; otherwise keep the current explicit-access model and close the issue as not worth the added complexity.
close_reason: Alt+h and Alt+l now use pane-orchestrator walking that skips the managed sidebar when it is closed.

title: Fix Yazelix launch hanging after Zellij config generation
description: Yazelix sometimes hangs during startup after the welcome prompt and after successfully regenerating the Zellij config. The visible stall point is after the final 'Config will auto-regenerate when source files change' line, before the Zellij UI takes over. This should be tracked separately from the stale zjstatus layout-label bug.
design: Treat this as a startup handoff bug, not a status-bar naming bug. The key boundary is the transition from Yazelix's launch/setup flow into the actual Zellij UI. Investigate whether the hang is in the included terminal path, start_yazelix_inner handoff, wrapper interaction, plugin permission state, or Zellij startup state after config generation. Avoid bundling unrelated fixes. Keep the bug scoped to restoring reliable startup first; layout-label correctness is a separate issue.
acceptance_criteria: Yazelix no longer hangs after printing that Zellij configuration was generated successfully. From a normal launch, the UI reliably transitions into the Zellij session after that point. Add regression coverage or a defended diagnostic contract around the launch handoff, and document the exact failure boundary that was fixed.
notes: March 27 fresh-state evidence: after clearing /tmp/zellij-$UID, /run/user/$UID/zellij, ~/.cache/zellij, ~/.local/share/zellij, and the permission cache, a new launch still recreated the bad state. Zellij restored only the cached zjstatus permission block; neither yazelix_pane_orchestrator.wasm nor yazelix_popup_runner.wasm reached the permission-prompt stage. The launch also spawned two separate Zellij 0.44.0 servers from a clean slate, each with its own launch_sidebar_yazi/set_workspace_root path. This means the missing Yazelix plugin permission popup is a downstream symptom, not the root cause. Current strongest boundary: duplicate server/session creation or earlier Zellij 0.44 startup corruption before Yazelix plugins can finish loading.
close_reason: Completed by the March 27 startup-handoff fixes. Commit 5043aeb (Harden Zellij startup handoff) removed the startup-time sidebar launch path that was implicated in the duplicate-server / pre-UI stall boundary, and commit 142e4c8 added an explicit permission recovery path for already-corrupted Zellij plugin state. The observed hang after successful Zellij config generation is no longer the active failure mode; any future recurrence should come back as a fresh regression with new evidence.
