title: make the popup receive a size from 1 to 100 (percentage of width, height)
description: Imported GitHub issue #464.
close_reason: Completed: added validated zellij.popup_width_percent and zellij.popup_height_percent config in the 1..100 range, wired yzx popup to use them, synced Home Manager/default config, and added popup regression tests

title: README: refresh the project front door after recent architecture and packaging changes
description:
Update the main README so it matches the current Yazelix product shape instead of older assumptions. The front page should reflect the current architecture, runtime model, supported entrypoints, and maintainer/user mental model after the recent packaging, spec, and workflow cleanup.

This bead also owns the README-side release-surface cleanup: the README should stop acting like a second changelog or partial history page.
design:
Treat this as a front-door documentation pass, not a changelog dump. The README should explain what Yazelix is today, point to the right deeper docs, and avoid stale or misleading surfaces. It should align with the architecture map, the package-ready runtime model, and the current public CLI surface. In particular, make sure the README does not imply repo-shaped runtime assumptions, does not overexpose maintainer-only workflow details on the front page, and presents the two main product layers clearly: the workspace experience and the runtime/environment layer behind it.

Explicitly clean up the current release-surface duplication. The README should not keep versioned "Improvements in v13.x" sections or other rolling changelog content when that material already belongs in CHANGELOG.md and docs/history.md. Prefer one stable README front door plus clear links outward: CHANGELOG.md for concise upgrade-facing notes, docs/history.md for long-form project history, and docs/architecture_map.md or installation/customization docs for deeper product explanation.
acceptance_criteria: The README gives a coherent, current explanation of Yazelix for a new user, points to the right deeper docs, and does not advertise removed or de-emphasized command surfaces. It reflects the current architecture/runtime model, keeps version markers correct, and no longer acts like a duplicate changelog or version-history surface. Versioned improvement blocks and other release-note duplication are either removed or reduced to a minimal stable pointer to CHANGELOG.md and docs/history.md. Leave behind at least one focused validation step or checklist showing that the main entrypoints and referenced docs were re-checked.

title: Add an optional modern_js pack for Bun, Deno, and future Vite+ workflows
description: Add a dedicated optional modern_js pack instead of widening the default ts pack. The pack owns JS/TS runtime and workflow tooling, while ts stays focused on editor-facing language tooling. Vite+ remains a follow-up once it is packaged in nixpkgs.
design: Keep the boundary explicit: ts is for language servers, linting, and formatting; modern_js is for runtime and workflow tools. Implement modern_js with tools that already exist in nixpkgs and keep the pack small enough to remain an optional add-on. Document the Vite+ gap directly instead of pretending the package exists.
acceptance_criteria: Yazelix ships a documented optional modern_js pack in both yazelix_default.toml and home_manager/module.nix, the ts pack no longer owns runtime tooling that belongs in the new pack, docs explain the split, and tests defend parity across both configuration paths. If Vite+ is not yet packaged in nixpkgs, the docs explicitly say so and the bead still closes with that defended boundary.
notes: Implemented as the optional modern_js pack with bun and deno. bun was removed from the default ts pack so ts stays editor-focused, docs now explain the split, and parity tests cover both ts and modern_js. Vite+ is intentionally deferred until it is packaged in nixpkgs.
close_reason: Completed

title: Add local startup profile comparison and baseline tooling
description:
The new structured startup profiler makes local evidence possible, but there is still no first-class way to compare two runs, summarize phase deltas, or keep stable local baselines without relying on noisy hosted CI timing. That limits the practical value of the new reports for ongoing performance work.

Design:
Build on the existing structured JSONL report format rather than adding a separate metrics store. Provide a local-only surface that can diff two startup profile reports or compare a fresh run against a named baseline, highlighting phase deltas and major regressions. Keep this explicitly local and deterministic; do not turn it into a flaky GitHub Actions wall-clock gate.

Acceptance Criteria:
1. There is a user-facing local command or helper to compare two startup profile reports. 2. The comparison output highlights total wall-time delta plus per-step or per-phase deltas. 3. The tooling can work from saved reports under the Yazelix state dir without rerunning startup. 4. Docs explain the intended local workflow and explicitly avoid promising a noisy hosted-CI timing gate.

title: Investigate touchpad three-finger gestures for Zellij pane navigation
description: Investigate whether Yazelix can support touchpad three-finger gestures or similar gesture-driven input to move between Zellij panes without compromising portability or making the product depend on one desktop stack.
design: Treat this as an input-stack feasibility question spanning terminal, OS gesture system, desktop environment, and Zellij. First determine what signal, if any, can reach Yazelix reliably on the supported platforms. If gesture support requires desktop-specific helpers or fragile remapping layers, capture that explicitly instead of pretending it is a clean cross-platform feature.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear feasibility decision on touchpad gesture support for pane navigation, including which platforms or terminal stacks could support it and what extra components would be required. If the idea is viable, leave behind a narrower implementation bead with explicit platform scope.

title: Optimize Yazi config generation on warm startup
description:
The structured startup profiler now shows warm current-terminal startup is dominated by Yazi config generation after the Zellij warm-state reuse fix. This should be the next delete-first warm-start optimization slice.

Design:
Keep this delete-first. First identify which parts of managed Yazi config generation are unnecessarily redone on every warm startup and which generated artifacts can be safely reused without weakening config correctness. Prefer deleting redundant regeneration or overly broad invalidation before micro-optimizing string processing. Preserve fail-fast behavior and managed-config correctness.

Acceptance Criteria:
1. The current bottleneck inside inner.generate_yazi_config is broken down enough to show which substeps dominate.
2. Warm startup avoids clearly redundant Yazi config work when inputs have not changed.
3. The change does not make generated Yazi state stale across real config/runtime changes.
4. A focused regression or profiling-oriented check defends the intended reuse or invalidation boundary.
notes:
Attempted two delete-first optimization shapes and reverted both.

Findings:
- Full-surface generation reuse with metadata/fingerprints reduced warm generate_merged_yazi_config by roughly 140ms, but added substantial invalidation complexity around config surfaces, bundled-vs-user plugins, and random-theme behavior.
- A smaller boundary that only tried to avoid recopying unchanged bundled Yazi assets was simpler, but did not produce a meaningful end-to-end win and still added nontrivial logic.
- The structured profiler showed Yazi is a warm-path chunk, but not large enough to justify the attempted caching machinery in its current form.

Current decision:
- Optimization code was reverted.
- Bead stays open because warm Yazi generation is still one of the larger warm-start steps, but the acceptable solution bar is now higher: materially smaller state surface, clearer correctness story, or a meaningfully larger win.

Measured context from this failed pass:
- Baseline warm inner.generate_yazi_config is roughly 300ms on maintainer hardware.
- The reverted full-surface cache brought warm direct generation down by about 140ms, but at too high a complexity cost.

Next likely directions to evaluate:
- Push more work into Yazi runtime/plugin behavior so startup does not need to rewrite as much managed state.
- Identify one narrower generated artifact with a naturally safe idempotence boundary instead of caching the whole surface.
- Prefer bigger warm-start wins elsewhere before revisiting this lane.
close_reason: Completed

title: add a ruby pack
description: Imported GitHub issue #537.

title: Replace string-built Zellij runtime wrapper commands with direct runtime script execution
description: The Zellij wrapper layer under configs/zellij/scripts currently relies on dynamic Nushell source strings plus `nu -c` to enter runtime-owned logic. This works, but it is stringly typed, harder to test, and duplicates wrapper boilerplate.
design: Prefer a delete-first refactor. Add a shared helper that executes runtime-owned Nushell scripts directly by relative runtime path, move wrapper logic into real runtime leaf scripts, and keep tracked/generated Zellij wrapper files thin. Scope this narrowly to wrappers under configs/zellij/scripts that currently build command strings through run_runtime_nu_command. Do not widen into unrelated command-surface changes.
acceptance_criteria: 1. At least one current string-built Zellij wrapper path is replaced with direct runtime script execution through a shared helper. 2. The new pattern is capable of covering wrappers that currently build `use ...; ...` strings for runtime entry. 3. The converted wrapper surface is materially thinner or fewer lines overall for the touched paths. 4. Existing high-signal wrapper regressions still pass, and no live plugin/runtime behavior regresses.

title: zjstatus: allow a small user-configurable text slot
description: Give users a small piece of personal space in the Yazelix zjstatus bar where they can define a short custom string.
design: Keep this intentionally narrow. The likely placement is to the left of the `YAZELIX` segment. This should not become a full templating or arbitrary string-programming system for the entire status bar. A simple configurable text field in `yazelix.toml` is enough if the result feels stable and readable.
acceptance_criteria: Users can set a short custom string for zjstatus, and it appears in a defined place near the YAZELIX branding without destabilizing the rest of the status bar design. Add unit tests for config parsing and rendering helpers, width-budget or rendering checks, and end-to-end validation that logs the configured value, rendered placement, and truncation or hiding behavior on narrow layouts.
close_reason: Completed

title: Strengthen test governance against weak AI-generated tests
design: Replace the default-suite canonical test-count budget with structural governance that better resists low-value AI-generated tests. Enforce small explicit lane declarations on all test_*.nu files, require nearby justification markers for every canonical default-lane test entry, and forbid new generic _extended overflow files unless they are explicitly grandfathered. Keep the runtime budget as the real speed guard. Update AGENTS and the test governance spec so the repo, not prompt discipline, carries the enforcement.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The canonical test-count budget is removed from the default-suite validator and from the governance spec. 2. CI rejects test files without an allowed # Test lane declaration. 3. CI rejects default-lane canonical tests that lack a nearby # Defends:, # Regression:, or # Invariant: marker. 4. CI rejects new generic _extended overflow files unless they are explicitly grandfathered. 5. The runtime budget validator remains the speed guard for the default suite.
close_reason: Completed: removed the canonical test-count budget, added enforced test-lane declarations and canonical-test justification markers, and blocked new generic _extended overflow files while keeping the runtime budget as the real CI speed guard.

title: Extend yzx edit to Yazi keymap and init.lua surfaces
description: Follow-up to the picker-based yzx edit surface. Add explicit support for the managed Yazi sub-surfaces beyond yazi.toml: keymap.toml and init.lua under ~/.config/yazelix/user_configs/yazi/. Keep the same canonical managed-surface policy and avoid exposing host-owned files.
design: Treat this as a narrow continuation of the current yzx edit surface, not as a generic file browser. Keep the supported targets limited to Yazelix-owned Yazi surfaces that are already part of the managed config contract. Reuse the existing target-selection machinery instead of adding one-off path logic, and keep the user-facing names explicit enough that scripting and printed-path flows remain predictable.
acceptance_criteria: yzx edit can target the managed Yazi keymap and init.lua surfaces in the same way it already targets config, packs, helix, zellij, and yazi.toml. Printed-path and interactive selection flows remain coherent, and the command still avoids exposing host-owned files outside Yazelix-managed paths.

title: investigate adding more 2 layouts to the game of life welcome screen
description:
Imported GitHub issue #544.

the random pool would then have 3 options

title: Audit nondefault test lanes file-by-file and remove dead weight
close_reason: Completed

title: Evaluate reusable fuzzy target selection across the yzx CLI
description: Analyze the new picker/query pattern introduced for yzx edit and decide where else it should be reused across the CLI. Scope: inventory candidate commands, compare command-surface clarity vs subcommand sprawl, identify shared implementation opportunities, and recommend where fuzzy disambiguation improves UX without making scripting or discoverability worse.
design: Treat this as a command-surface audit, not as a blanket mandate to add fuzzy selection everywhere. The point is to identify where a small target catalog meaningfully simplifies the CLI and where it would instead add ambiguity or make automation worse. The output should include candidate commands, a recommendation on whether interactive selection, fuzzy query matching, exact aliases, or plain subcommands fit best, and whether the current yzx edit implementation should be extracted into a reusable helper at all.
acceptance_criteria: There is a concrete recommendation for which yzx commands should reuse the new target-selection pattern, which should not, and why. Any proposed reuse points are grounded in command-surface clarity and scripting impact rather than novelty. If a shared implementation seam exists, it is identified explicitly; if not, the bead says so instead of forcing abstraction.

title: Sidebar focus should be explicit, not latent
description: Make the managed sidebar behave like an explicit auxiliary surface rather than a normal focus target. A closed sidebar should not remain focusable, closing it while focused should move focus back to the main workspace, and opening it should not steal focus unless the action explicitly targets the sidebar.
design: Use the managed sidebar as an explicit auxiliary surface, but only if the behavior can be implemented cleanly within the current pane-orchestrator model. Closing it should clear any latent focus on it and return focus to the main workspace; opening it should preserve current focus unless the user explicitly targeted the sidebar. If this requires a large amount of extra focus bookkeeping or layout-specific complexity, it is not worth doing.
acceptance_criteria: When the sidebar is closed it is no longer focusable, and if it was focused the active focus returns to the main workspace/editor pane. Reopening the sidebar does not steal focus by default. Add focused tests for close-while-focused and reopen-without-focus-steal behavior only if the implementation stays small and coherent; otherwise leave the current explicit-focus model in place.
close_reason: Sidebar visibility and focus are now explicit; closed sidebars are not reachable through explicit or incidental movement.

title: Add yzx keys command for Yazelix bindings and tool discoverability
description: Add a small `yzx keys` surface that shows Yazelix-owned keybindings by default and offers tool-specific discoverability helpers like `yzx keys yazi` and `yzx keys hx`. Keep it intentionally narrow: the default output should focus on the Yazelix layer, while per-tool subcommands explain how to view upstream keybindings inside Yazi or Helix instead of duplicating their full keymaps.
close_reason: Implemented yzx keys with Yazelix defaults plus yazi and hx discoverability helpers

title: Define a first-class one-command install UX for Yazelix
description:
Users should have a first impression closer to modern tools like uv: one obvious copy-paste install path, not a repo-bootstrap explanation. Evaluate concrete install surfaces such as `curl | sh`, `nix run`, or another thin first-party bootstrap that installs the real runtime cleanly without reviving the old clone-under-~/.config/yazelix assumption.

Design:
Treat this as install-product design, not as a docs polish task. Compare the trust, portability, maintenance cost, update story, rollback story, and failure modes of each candidate. Be explicit about what the command installs, where runtime assets live, how user config is initialized, and how the installed `yzx` command and desktop entry are produced. Prefer a surface that feels copy-paste simple for users while still preserving Yazelix's real runtime model and Nix/devenv constraints.

Acceptance Criteria:
A single recommended install UX is chosen and justified against the main alternatives. The bead records the exact user-facing command shape, ownership of runtime/config/state paths, and the minimum supporting implementation work needed to ship it. The result should make the install guide shorter and more copy-paste friendly, not just more carefully worded.
notes:
Decision recorded in docs/specs/one_command_install_ux.md.

Recommendation: Yazelix should ship a first-class one-command install UX built around a thin first-party Nix installer app, not a hosted shell installer.

Canonical front door once implemented:
  nix run github:luccahuguet/yazelix#install

Important boundary: the GitHub flake source path used during `nix run` is bootstrap-only. The installer must materialize a persistent runtime tree, initialize user config if missing, install the stable `yzx` executable, and optionally install the desktop entry. User config remains under `~/.config/yazelix/user_configs/`; generated state remains under `~/.local/share/yazelix`.
close_reason: Install UX decision recorded and follow-on implementation identified

title: Install UX: evaluate a first-party nix run bootstrap surface
description:
Evaluate whether the cleanest one-command install UX for Yazelix is a first-party `nix run` surface that bootstraps the real runtime without requiring users to understand the repo layout.

Design:
Assess how thin the required flake interface can stay, what it would install or initialize, whether it can produce `yzx`, desktop entry assets, and runtime placement cleanly, and how it would behave on Linux vs macOS. Be explicit about whether `nix run` is just a bootstrap helper or the long-term user-facing install command.

Acceptance Criteria:
The bead records whether `nix run` is a viable first-class install entrypoint, the exact user-facing command shape if yes, and the concrete implementation prerequisites if Yazelix chooses this path.
notes:
Outcome: viable and preferred.

The cleanest one-command install surface for Yazelix is a thin first-party installer app exposed through a top-level flake:
  nix run github:luccahuguet/yazelix#install

Why it wins:
- matches Yazelix's real Nix/devenv architecture
- gives users a copy-paste command without inventing a second bootstrap ecosystem
- keeps trust and auditability better than a hosted shell installer
- can stay thin if it only bootstraps a persistent installed runtime and then hands off to the stable `yzx` entrypoint

Critical constraint: the flake source path itself must not become the runtime root. The installer must install or materialize a persistent runtime tree and point `yzx` and desktop assets at that installed runtime.

Implementation follow-on belongs to `yazelix-2ex.1.4.2`.
close_reason: Evaluation complete; first-party nix run installer app chosen as viable

title: Install UX: evaluate a hosted curl-pipe bootstrap installer
description:
Evaluate whether Yazelix should ship a hosted bootstrap command in the style of modern tools: a short curl-pipe installer that fetches or installs the real runtime cleanly without teaching users the repo layout first.

Design:
Compare trust, auditability, update behavior, rollback story, macOS/Linux portability, and how the installer would hand off to Nix, devenv, runtime assets, yzx wrapper creation, and desktop integration. Be explicit about what must stay transparent to avoid a sketchy installer story.

Acceptance Criteria:
The bead records whether a hosted installer is a viable first-class install surface, the exact command shape if yes, and the minimum infrastructure or release discipline Yazelix would need before shipping it.
close_reason: Duplicate of yazelix-aa24.3

title: Install UX: evaluate a hosted curl-pipe bootstrap installer
description:
Evaluate whether Yazelix should ship a hosted bootstrap command in the style of modern tools: a short curl-pipe installer that fetches or installs the real runtime cleanly without teaching users the repo layout first.

Design:
Compare trust, auditability, update behavior, rollback story, macOS/Linux portability, and how the installer would hand off to Nix, devenv, runtime assets, yzx wrapper creation, and desktop integration. Be explicit about what must stay transparent to avoid a sketchy installer story.

Acceptance Criteria:
The bead records whether a hosted installer is a viable first-class install surface, the exact command shape if yes, and the minimum infrastructure or release discipline Yazelix would need before shipping it.
notes:
Outcome: not the preferred first move.

A hosted `curl | sh` installer could eventually wrap a mature packaged install path, but it should not be Yazelix's first-class front door yet.

Why it loses now:
- adds a second trust surface immediately
- adds release-hosting and cross-platform bootstrap burden
- still has to hand off to Nix and the pinned `devenv` story anyway
- risks looking simpler than it really is while hiding important runtime constraints

Reconsider only after the packaged runtime and first-party installer app are solid.
close_reason: Evaluation complete; hosted curl installer not chosen as first-class front door

title: Install UX: choose the canonical one-command Yazelix install story
description:
Choose the single install story Yazelix should optimize for after comparing the leading options. This is where the project stops debating surfaces and commits to one first-class command users can copy and paste.

Design:
Use the comparison work to choose one canonical path and demote the others to secondary or maintainer-oriented options. Record the user-facing command, supported platforms, runtime/config/state ownership, how yzx becomes available, and what documentation or packaging changes are required.

Acceptance Criteria:
One install path is clearly recommended as the front-door UX. The bead records the chosen command, why it beat the alternatives, and the concrete follow-on implementation work needed to ship it.
notes:
Decision: optimize for a first-party Nix installer app as the canonical install front door.

Chosen command shape:
  nix run github:luccahuguet/yazelix#install

Why it beat the alternatives:
- honest about Yazelix being a Nix-first product
- short enough to feel like a real copy-paste install command
- aligns with runtime-root and package-ready work already underway
- avoids creating a separate hosted-installer product before the packaged path is stable

Minimum follow-on work:
- add a thin top-level flake interface
- expose an `install` app
- install or materialize a persistent runtime tree
- install the stable `yzx` executable and optional desktop assets
- make update behavior work without a live Git clone
- rewrite installation docs around this front door
close_reason: Canonical one-command install story chosen

title: Keep Zellij pane cwd aligned with restart directory
description: Imported GitHub issue #500. Restart currently boots the sidebar from the requested directory but was rewriting Zellij's own default cwd to HOME, so native new panes ignored the restart directory.
close_reason: Closed on GitHub

title: Make new tabs inherit the active workspace root
description: New tabs currently inherit a stale bootstrap root, so Alt+m can open panes in the original launch directory even when the current tab visually starts at HOME. Tighten the workspace model so new tabs inherit the active tab's workspace root and tab names follow that root immediately, including HOME-backed tabs.
close_reason: Completed: fresh sidebar tabs now bootstrap the tab workspace root from the actual Yazi launch directory, so Alt+m follows the tab start directory instead of stale launch cwd state

title: yzx wallpaper / screensaver
description: Explore whether Yazelix should ship a branded wallpaper and/or screensaver path instead of leaving the idea as a vague aesthetic request.
design: Keep this explicitly low-priority and opt-in. First decide what problem this would solve: branding, delight, showcase visuals, or an idle-state experience tied to Yazelix sessions. Distinguish between static wallpapers, lightweight animated assets, and an actual screensaver workflow, and avoid introducing platform-specific complexity unless the user value is real.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear decision on whether Yazelix should ship wallpapers, a screensaver path, both, or neither. If something is implemented, it is lightweight, optional, and documented with the intended use case and platform caveats. Leave behind validated assets or a documented rejection, plus any generation or preview commands needed to reproduce the result.

title: Evaluate replacing the popup-runner plugin with Zellij 0.44 CLI pane control
description:
Post-Zellij-0.44 follow-up: evaluate whether Yazelix can replace the custom popup-runner plugin with pure Zellij CLI control for popup lifecycle management.

Why this exists:
- Zellij 0.44 expanded CLI automation with `zellij action list-panes` (including JSON output) and pane IDs returned from pane/tab creation commands
- Yazelix currently uses a dedicated popup-runner plugin plus a shell toggle wrapper to discover, focus, and close the managed popup pane
- If the new CLI surface is sufficient, Yazelix may be able to remove one Rust/WASM plugin and simplify popup lifecycle logic
design: Treat this as a simplification evaluation first, not an automatic rewrite. Start from the current popup contract: one managed popup identity, toggle=open/focus/close semantics, no duplicates, and active-tab scoping. Validate whether Zellij 0.44 CLI surfaces can cover that contract reliably without plugin state: list panes in the active/current session, identify the managed popup pane by stable metadata, and focus/close it without racing the open path. Preserve the current shell-side `yzx popup` launcher if it remains the most reliable creation path. Success can be either a real plugin removal plan or a documented rejection if active-tab targeting or popup identity still requires plugin state.
acceptance_criteria: The evaluation ends with a concrete decision grounded in verified Zellij 0.44 behavior. Either: 1. Yazelix adopts or prototypes a CLI-only popup lifecycle path that preserves active-tab scoping, toggle semantics, and no-duplicate guarantees, with regression coverage at the popup seam; or 2. the bead records a clear rejection explaining which popup invariants still require the custom plugin. In either case, leave behind a short implementation plan or rejection note rather than vague future intent.

title: Split managed config contract tests by subsystem ownership
close_reason: Completed

title: evaluate making the welcome screen skip on any key press
description: Imported GitHub issue #483.
close_reason: GitHub issue #483 is closed; tracker synced.

title: Specify canonical config surface and launch-profile contract
description: Define the canonical config surfaces and launch-profile contract for Yazelix so downstream work can stop guessing which configuration path owns what behavior and how launch entrypoints are supposed to compose.
design: This is a high-leverage contract bead. Clarify the relationship between direct config, Home Manager, generated runtime state, launch profiles, and any environment-specific entrypoints. The goal is not just documentation; it is a stable contract that downstream beads can depend on when reducing duplication or adding validators. Prefer sharp ownership rules and explicit derivation paths over fuzzy precedence.
acceptance_criteria: There is a documented canonical contract for config surfaces and launch profiles that downstream work can reference. The contract states what each surface owns, how launch profiles compose with configuration, and where parity or validation is required. Dependent beads such as duplication reduction and parity validation can proceed without redefining the model.
close_reason: Completed: added the canonical config-surface and launch-profile contract spec and linked it from the architecture map.

title: Add launch-profile freshness diagnostics to yzx doctor
description: Teach `yzx doctor` to report whether the current cached Yazelix launch profile is valid, stale, or missing for the active rebuild-relevant config and tracked devenv inputs so users get actionable guidance before launch/reuse confusion sets in.
design: Keep this user-facing and runtime-local. Reuse compute_config_state and the existing refresh-reason logic instead of reimplementing launch-profile validity in doctor. The result should distinguish at least: healthy profile, stale profile because rebuild-relevant config changed, stale profile because tracked devenv inputs changed, and missing verified launch profile. It should suggest the right recovery path such as `yzx refresh` or rerunning without `--reuse`. Do not turn `yzx doctor` into a repo-maintainer parity validator; that stays in the cheap validator lane.
acceptance_criteria: Running `yzx doctor` includes a clear launch-profile health result. When the cached profile matches the current rebuild-relevant config and tracked inputs, doctor reports it as healthy. When the profile is stale or missing, doctor reports the reason using the existing config-state logic and points the user at the right next command. The check relies on the canonical launch-profile contract rather than duplicating its rules ad hoc.
notes: Promote this canonical-contract follow-up. Current cleanup direction is to keep centralizing runtime/config semantics onto the maintained contract so launch/profile state stops depending on fuzzy or duplicated ownership rules. This is part of the pre-packaging hardening lane.

title: Use Yazi as a floating picker from Helix
description: Add a Yazelix-native flow where Helix can open Yazi as a floating picker pane, select a target, and return the chosen path back to the editor. Keep the first version tightly scoped: it should feel fast and integrated inside the existing Zellij workspace rather than spawning an unrelated external file picker.
design: Keep the first slice tightly scoped around a fast file-selection loop from Helix using a floating Yazi surface inside the existing Zellij workspace. Define invocation, selected-path return, cancellation, and what happens when the persistent sidebar is disabled. Avoid letting this turn into a generic command-palette replacement or a second unrelated file-tree system.
acceptance_criteria: From Helix, a user can open a Yazi-based floating picker, choose a path, and return that path cleanly to the editor without breaking the existing workspace flow. The first slice is explicitly scoped and remains compatible with the narrower sidebar-disabled variant tracked separately.

title: Split CI into fast and integration lanes with path-gated heavy install validation
description: Restructure GitHub Actions so strong always-on branch protection remains in place while the current single serial CI job stops paying for heavy install/runtime validation on every push; the main target is to stop validate_flake_install.nu from dominating Beads/docs and other unrelated changes without losing real install/runtime/packaging/Home Manager coverage.
design: Current state: .github/workflows/ci.yml is one serial validate job; cheap validators finish quickly, validate_default_test_budget is about 60s, Check Home Manager flake is about 15-20s, and validate_flake_install.nu dominates runtime with roughly 4m cold-success paths and >10m hangs/cancels. Recommended architecture: keep an always-on fast required lane for cheap branch-protection checks; move heavy install/runtime coverage into a separate integration lane gated internally by changed-file detection rather than workflow-level paths filters; keep nix flake check ./home_manager --no-build in fast unless later timing proves otherwise; preserve visibility so skipped integration work concludes cleanly as not-needed instead of disappearing into pending required-check state. Likely heavy-lane file patterns: flake.nix, shells/posix/install_yazelix.sh.in, yazelix_runtime_package.nix, mk_runtime_tree.nix, locked_devenv_package.nix, home_manager/**, nushell/scripts/setup/**, nushell/scripts/core/**, nushell/scripts/utils/**, and configs/** when they affect installed/generated runtime content.
acceptance_criteria: 1. CI no longer consists of one fully serial validate job that always runs the heavy install smoke on every push. 2. There is an always-on fast lane preserving current cheap branch-protection checks. 3. Heavy flake-install/runtime integration validation runs only when relevant install/runtime/packaging/Home Manager files change, or under another explicitly documented narrow trigger. 4. The design avoids the GitHub Actions trap where skipped required workflows remain pending and block merges. 5. Heavy-lane skip decisions are explicit and observable in CI output. 6. Workflow comments or nearby docs explain which checks belong to fast vs integration and why. 7. Routine CI runtime and Actions spend are materially reduced for non-runtime changes.
notes: Relevant repo surfaces: .github/workflows/ci.yml, nushell/scripts/dev/validate_flake_install.nu, nushell/scripts/dev/validate_default_test_budget.nu, home_manager/flake.nix. External references informing the design: GitHub Docs on skipped-but-required checks staying pending and on passing job outputs between jobs, which supports a detector-job to gated-integration-job design. Observed timings from recent runs: default-suite runtime budget about 60s, Home Manager flake about 15-20s, flake install smoke 4m timeout-triggered failure on cold runner after the fail-fast patch and >10m when previously hanging/cancelled.
close_reason: Implemented: split CI into fast and integration lanes with explicit internal change gating for flake install smoke

title: Add a maintainer refresh path for vendored Yazi plugins
description: Yazelix vendors Yazi plugin code from upstream repositories, but it currently lacks a clear maintainer refresh path comparable to the vendored zjstatus update flow. Add a deterministic maintainer update path so vendored plugin drift is explicit and reproducible.
design: Treat vendored Yazi plugin refresh as a source-management problem, not a user runtime feature. Inventory which shipped plugins come from which upstreams: git.yazi is from the yazi-rs/plugins monorepo, while starship.yazi and lazygit.yazi are separate upstream repositories. Add an explicit source map that records the upstream repo, pinned revision, source path, and target vendored path for each intentionally shipped plugin. Then add one maintainer refresh command that pulls those sources into configs/yazi/plugins/, preserving any clearly-marked Yazelix-local patches instead of relying on manual copy steps or user-side ya pkg resolution. Keep the runtime deterministic: the refresh happens in the repo, maintainers review the vendored diff, and end users just consume the committed plugin code.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The repo has an explicit source-of-truth mapping for each vendored upstream Yazi plugin Yazelix ships. 2. Maintainers can run one documented command to refresh vendored Yazi plugins from those pinned upstream sources. 3. The refresh path makes it explicit which plugins come from yazi-rs/plugins versus separate upstream repositories. 4. The resulting vendored files under configs/yazi/plugins/ are reproducible and do not depend on user-side ya pkg resolution or network access. 5. Focused maintainer validation or regression coverage defends the source-map/update seam so vendored plugin drift is easier to detect.
close_reason: Completed: added a pinned source map for vendored Yazi plugin runtime files, a refresh helper with overlay patch support and dirty-worktree protection, integrated it into yzx dev update, and added a maintainer regression. The first slice intentionally manages runtime files (main.lua) rather than full README synchronization.

title: When opening many tabs, the tabs bar stops working correctly
description: Imported GitHub issue #549. With several tabs open and long repo-based tab names, the zjstatus tab bar can scroll the rendered tabs out of view instead of keeping the active tab visible. In practice the bar can end up showing only truncated tail fragments or an effectively empty tab area, leaving the user guessing which tab is focused.

title: Fill missing yzx subcommand help descriptions
description: Audit the yzx command surface and add explicit descriptions for user-facing subcommands that currently show blank help entries in Nushell help output. Treat the command surface as a product contract: help text should be present, concise, and useful for every supported subcommand instead of relying on users to guess from the name.
design: Treat this as command-surface contract cleanup, not help-text polishing for its own sake. Audit the real user-facing yzx subcommands that still show blank descriptions, fill them with concise task-level descriptions, and prefer one clear source of truth so help metadata does not drift from the command tree. Avoid verbose marketing copy and avoid testing help-text trivia unless a check defends the maintained command-metadata contract itself.
acceptance_criteria: 1. User-facing yzx subcommands no longer show blank built-in descriptions in Nushell help output. 2. Descriptions stay concise, specific, and aligned with the actual supported command behavior. 3. The implementation does not introduce a second hand-maintained command tree just to improve help text.

title: Proper agent command center IDE for teams of running agents
description: Create a real agent command-center surface for coordinating multiple running agents across one or more monitors, with fast visibility into agent state and the ability to pop open related tools.
design: Design this as a first-class operations surface rather than a pile of widgets. The core use cases are seeing which agents exist, whether they are idle or active, showing or hiding their panes, opening related terminals or tools, and exposing lightweight usage or health stats. Prefer a coherent workspace dashboard that can scale across monitors and degrade gracefully when some agent integrations are unavailable.
acceptance_criteria: Yazelix has a credible agent command-center direction with a concrete first slice that helps manage multiple running agents. The chosen surface can reveal agent state, toggle or focus the relevant workspace surfaces, and open related tools without feeling bolted on. Add unit tests for state and action routing plus end-to-end validation that logs the visible agents, derived status, and invoked workspace actions.

title: Decide mission-control tab vs tab-local agent sidebar
description: Decide whether Yazelix agent management should first ship as a dedicated mission-control tab or a tab-local sidebar surface, with current bias toward mission control as the higher-value and lower-hanging first slice.
design: Reason from scope first. A mission-control tab is probably the better first slice because it has more room for agent state, actions, and future orchestration, while avoiding premature sidebar overloading. Treat a tab-local agent sidebar as a possible later adjunct, not the default first target. This decision work should define which concerns are tab-local versus session-global, whether agents belong in a narrow side surface, and how a future sidebar would relate to the broader command-center story. Keep session-management sidebar ideas out of the first decision unless they materially change the surface choice.
acceptance_criteria: There is a documented decision on whether the first real agent-management surface should be a mission-control tab, a tab-local agent sidebar, or both in staged order. The decision explicitly classifies which agent operations are tab-local versus global, explains why the chosen first surface is the better product slice, and leaves a clear follow-up path for any deferred sidebar idea without hardcoding the current sidebar system around Yazi forever.

title: Create a GitHub social preview image for Yazelix
description: Create and configure a repository social preview image that represents Yazelix clearly when the repo is shared on GitHub and social platforms.
design: Treat this as a branding and front-door asset, not just a generic screenshot upload. The image should reflect Yazelix's terminal-native identity, remain readable at social-card sizes, and avoid overloading the card with tiny UI details. Decide whether the right asset is an illustrated brand card, a polished product screenshot, or a hybrid, then configure the repo to use the chosen image.
acceptance_criteria: There is a final social preview image asset sized appropriately for GitHub, it matches Yazelix branding well enough for public sharing, and the repository is configured to use it. The bead records the chosen direction so future branding work knows why this asset exists.

title: Surface desktop-entry rebuild progress after terminal switches
description: If the user edits yazelix.toml to switch to a terminal that is not present in the currently active Yazelix environment, launching from the desktop entry can spend noticeable time rebuilding the environment before the terminal becomes available. Today that happens silently, so it looks like the desktop entry is broken while Yazelix is actually doing work.
design: Treat this as a launch UX problem, not a terminal-packaging bug. Reproduce a terminal switch that requires a fresh environment (for example Ghostty to WezTerm or Kitty), then decide how the desktop-entry path should surface that work. The user should get immediate visible feedback that Yazelix is rebuilding or preparing the environment, rather than waiting with no signal and assuming launch failed.
acceptance_criteria: When a desktop-entry launch needs to rebuild or re-enter the environment because the configured terminal is not available in the current activated profile, the user gets immediate, clear feedback that Yazelix is preparing the environment. The launch still completes normally, and the UX no longer feels like a dead desktop entry.

title: Evaluate whether Yazelix should drop Foot support because it is not on macOS
description: Evaluate whether Yazelix should stop treating Foot as a meaningful supported terminal option because it is not available on macOS, unlike the other first-class terminal candidates under consideration.
design: Reason from the actual support matrix and product goals rather than from terminal popularity alone. Compare Foot against the terminals Yazelix most plausibly wants to support across platforms, especially macOS-relevant options like WezTerm and Alacritty. The key decision is whether Foot still earns documentation, config generation, testing effort, and maintainer attention despite being Linux-only. If Foot remains supported, the bead should justify that cost explicitly. If not, it should define the de-support path cleanly.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear decision to keep, demote, or drop Foot support. The decision records the platform and maintenance tradeoffs, how Foot compares to WezTerm and Alacritty for Yazelix's goals, and what documentation, config, or support-matrix changes would follow from the chosen direction.
close_reason: Closed on GitHub

title: Keep runtime-project shell artifacts aligned with recorded launch state
description:
Problem:
The runtime-owned profile resolver now correctly prefers recorded launch state over the runtime project shell artifact, but ~/.local/share/yazelix/runtime/project/.devenv/gc/shell can still lag behind the latest built profile after refresh. That means user-facing behavior is fixed by precedence, while one materialized artifact remains stale and misleading.

Design:
Decide the clean contract for the runtime-project shell artifact instead of continuing to tolerate drift. Either keep the runtime-project shell artifact aligned with the latest built profile whenever refresh or rebuild succeeds, or explicitly demote that artifact to bootstrap-only/internal status and remove remaining resolver or helper dependence on it where possible. Prefer delete-first reasoning: if the stale artifact does not need to participate in runtime-owned truth, stop treating it as meaningful state rather than layering more precedence hacks on top.

Acceptance Criteria:
1. The repo has an explicit contract for how ~/.local/share/yazelix/runtime/project/.devenv/gc/shell relates to the latest recorded launch profile after refresh/build flows.
2. Refresh/build flows either keep that artifact aligned with the fresh built profile, or the code stops depending on it as runtime-owned truth outside the narrow place that truly needs it.
3. Runtime-owned profile resolution, refresh, and desktop launch no longer rely on stale runtime-project shell artifacts to behave correctly.
4. Regression coverage or validators defend the chosen contract so this drift does not silently return.
close_reason: Investigation showed runtime-project .devenv profile artifacts are optional helper state, not canonical launch truth. Spec and maintainer regression now defend build-shell output plus recorded launch state as the authoritative reusable-profile contract.

title: Define desktop launch behavior when terminal choice changes across rebuilds
description: Desktop launch now has a real contract question: if the user changes terminal.terminals in yazelix.toml and the newly preferred terminal only becomes launchable after a rebuild, the launcher cannot simultaneously guarantee fast first paint, one-window behavior, and exact-terminal correctness. Capture and decide the intended product behavior instead of letting desktop fast paths guess.
design: Reason from first principles. The competing goals are: fast first paint, one click equals one real session/window, exact adherence to the newly configured terminal, and a maintainable state model. Evaluate at least these options: 1. correctness-first hidden wait, then launch the exact new terminal; 2. one-window visible bootstrap in the last known launchable terminal, with the new terminal taking effect on next launch; 3. two-window/bootstrap-then-final handoff that launches an old or host terminal immediately, rebuilds visibly, then spawns the newly configured terminal and disposes of the bootstrap window. Prefer the simplest contract that is honest, predictable, and maintainable. The bead should end with an explicit decision and follow-on implementation path, not just a list of options.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The bead records an explicit desktop-launch contract for the case where terminal selection changes and the new terminal only becomes available after rebuild. 2. The chosen behavior states whether Yazelix prioritizes fast first paint, exact new-terminal correctness, or a two-stage handoff in this edge case. 3. The decision includes failure behavior and restart or next-launch semantics. 4. The result is specific enough to guide a concrete implementation without re-arguing the product tradeoff.
close_reason: Defined and implemented the desktop launch contract: use a visible one-window bootstrap path when a host-launchable terminal exists, let newly rebuilt managed terminals take effect on the next launch via runtime-owned wrapper reuse, and fall back to the hidden-wait desktop path only when no visible bootstrap terminal is available.

title: Restore Nushell yzx completion via generated extern bridge
description: Generate a lightweight Nushell extern bridge for the external yzx CLI from the real Yazelix command tree during setup/refresh. Managed Nushell should source the generated bridge from Yazelix state so subcommand and flag completion return without re-importing the full command implementation or hand-maintaining a duplicate signature file.
close_reason: Generated Nushell extern bridge from the real yzx command tree, sourced it from Yazelix state, and verified completion/signature metadata in the installed runtime.

title: Add a shared migration engine and schema-aware yzx config migrate
description: Add the shared migration engine that knows about supported config removals, renames, moves, and value replacements, and expose it through yzx config migrate. This should preserve user intent whenever the mapping is deterministic, stay separate from blunt reset and replace flows, and always back up the original config before writing.
design: Treat this as the foundation for the whole upgrade UX rather than as a one off command. The migration engine should own structured rules with version and date metadata, path and value matchers, rationale, manual fix text, and whether a change is safe to rewrite automatically. Prefer one canonical Nushell-friendly data shape and API for these rules so startup diagnostics, yzx doctor, yzx config migrate, and the first-run upgrade summary all consume the same metadata. The rule schema should be rich enough for validators to prove that breaking config changes are either covered by a migration rule or explicitly marked as manual only in the upgrade-notes contract. yzx config migrate should default to a non destructive preview, support an explicit apply path with backup, preserve supported user settings, and leave ambiguous or lossy cases untouched but clearly explained. Prefer focused behavior tests over help text checks.
acceptance_criteria: A maintainer or user can run yzx config migrate against stale yazelix.toml files and get a detailed preview of safe rewrites, skipped ambiguous cases, and the exact backup path used on apply. Known migrations such as removed widget tray entries are expressed as explicit rules rather than hard coded one offs, and the same rule metadata can be consumed by diagnostics, validators, and upgrade-summary surfaces. Add comprehensive unit tests for rule matching, ordering, safe versus ambiguous classification, preview rendering, apply behavior, backup creation, and rule metadata completeness. Add end to end scripts with detailed logging that exercise dry run and apply flows in temp config roots, log before and after TOML plus command output, and cover at least one removed value case, one renamed or moved field case, and one ambiguous case that must stay manual.
notes:
Planning refinements:
- Create a focused spec such as docs/specs/config_migration_engine.md.
- Prefer a stable command surface like yzx config migrate [--apply] with an explicit preview-first default.
- Prefer a small migration helper module that exposes rule discovery, rule matching, preview rendering, and apply operations separately so tests can defend each boundary.
- Non-goals: silent startup rewrites, auto-deleting unsupported user settings without explanation, and conflating migrate with config reset.
- Add a cheap validator that checks migration-rule completeness and wire it into the same local and CI contract lane used by the upgrade-notes work.
- Verification plan: canonical unit coverage for rule matching and apply semantics, plus at least one high-signal end to end Nushell script that logs temp HOME setup, input TOML, detected migrations, preview output, backup path, output TOML, and manual-only skips.
close_reason: Completed: added the shared config migration engine, preview-first yzx config migrate command, migration-rule validator, spec, and comprehensive unit + e2e coverage for deterministic and manual-only config migrations.

title: Evaluate whether sesh adds meaningful value to Yazelix session workflows
description: Evaluate what sesh would actually add to Yazelix compared with the current Zellij and workspace flow, and decide whether it is worth integrating, documenting, or ignoring.
design: Compare sesh against the problems Yazelix already has or may soon have around session discovery, startup, switching, and saved workflows. Do not evaluate it as an abstract cool tool. The question is whether sesh solves a real user problem better than current Yazelix or Zellij surfaces, and if so whether integration should be first-party, optional, or purely documented.
acceptance_criteria: There is a go, no-go, or optional-integration decision on sesh, grounded in concrete session-workflow use cases. The bead records what sesh does better, what it duplicates, and what integration shape would make sense if it is worth adopting.

title: Fix stale config-root runtime env leaking into Zellij helper scripts
close_reason: Completed: runtime/Nushell resolution now recovers from stale config-root runtime env, and the flake install smoke defends the exact regression path from issue #526.

title: Inventory every test file with lane, contract, score, and verdict
close_reason: Completed

title: Reduce duplicate shellhook/setup chatter during yzx launch rebuilds
close_reason: Completed

title: Audit and classify yzx command families by backend coupling
description: Audit the exported `yzx` command surface from `core/yazelix.nu` and `yzx/*.nu` and classify each command family by backend coupling. The goal is not a flat "lite or not" list, but an honest map of which surfaces are backend-required control-plane commands, which are backend-agnostic workspace/config UX, which are runtime-owned distribution/install surfaces, and which are genuinely mixed seams that need refactoring before `Yazelix Core` decisions are honest. Cover canonical families such as env/run/refresh, launch/restart, doctor/status, desktop/update, menu/edit/config/import/open, popup/cwd/reveal, packs/gc, and the purely informational commands.
design:
Treat this as a command-family audit grounded in the actual exported defs, not in docs/help text. Use `core/yazelix.nu` plus `yzx/*.nu` as the source of truth, and fold aliases into canonical families instead of listing every leaf command separately. For each family, record: the canonical commands it covers, its primary owner bucket, why it belongs there, the current code evidence for that coupling, and whether a future `Yazelix Core` edition would plausibly keep it unchanged, narrow it, or drop it.

Do not force every command into a fake binary choice. The audit should allow a fourth subtype for backend-agnostic but runtime-owned/distribution surfaces so commands like `yzx desktop launch` or `yzx update runtime` do not get mislabeled as full environment-backend requirements. Be explicit about mixed seams instead of pretending the current architecture is already clean.

The code pass already suggests these likely families to verify:
- Backend-required control plane: `yzx env`, `yzx run`, `yzx refresh`, probably `yzx packs`, and probably `yzx gc`
- Backend-agnostic workspace/config UX: `yzx cwd`, `yzx reveal`, `yzx popup`, `yzx config`, `yzx edit`, `yzx import`, `yzx open`, `yzx keys`, `yzx tutor`, `yzx screen`, `yzx why`, `yzx sponsor`, `yzx whats_new`
- Runtime-owned distribution/install surfaces: `yzx desktop *`, `yzx update runtime`, possibly parts of `yzx update nix`
- Mixed/refactor-needed seams: `yzx launch`, `yzx restart`, `yzx status`, `yzx doctor`, and `yzx menu`

The output should leave behind a small ranked shortlist of the genuinely mixed command families that deserve refactoring next, rather than just a spreadsheet of labels.
acceptance_criteria: There is a concrete command-family matrix covering the exported `yzx` surface from `core/yazelix.nu` and `yzx/*.nu`, with aliases folded into canonical families. Each family is classified as one of: backend-required, backend-agnostic, runtime-owned/distribution, or mixed/refactor-needed, with a short reason grounded in the current code. The audit explicitly identifies the most important mixed seams to refactor next instead of pretending every command already fits cleanly. The result is precise enough to guide `yazelix-qv8c`, `yazelix-qow`, and later backend-adapter/Rust work without having to re-argue the command surface family by family.
notes:
Initial code-evidence pass:
- The exported surface is defined in `nushell/scripts/core/yazelix.nu` plus `nushell/scripts/yzx/*.nu`.
- The main mixed seam is not just `yzx launch`; it is the cluster of `launch`, `restart`, `status`, `doctor`, and `menu`, where backend state, runtime integrity, install/distribution surfaces, and workspace UX still overlap.
- `yzx desktop *` and `yzx update runtime` look different from `yzx env`/`run`/`refresh`: they are runtime/distribution ownership surfaces, not generic workspace UX, but they also do not need the full environment-control semantics of the current backend.
close_reason: Completed: added the command-family backend-coupling audit spec and linked it from the architecture map.

title: Canonicalize devenv CLI resolution and PATH ordering
description: Yazelix currently conflates the standalone devenv CLI, the project devenv input, and whatever devenv happens to be first on PATH. Fix the contract by introducing one shared host-tool resolver for devenv, using it for internal invocations and version reporting, and repairing Nushell PATH restoration so stale initial PATH entries do not outrank the current shell PATH.
close_reason: Canonicalized remaining maintainer/internal devenv invocations onto the shared resolver and made Nushell PATH restoration explicitly preserve current-shell precedence over stale saved entries.

title: Fix yzx cwd renaming the wrong Zellij tab
description: Sometimes tab renaming updates the wrong Zellij tab instead of the active tab. In the reported case on March 25, 2026, running yzx cwd in the current tab renamed the tab immediately to the left rather than the current tab. The same wrong-tab rename behavior was also observed when opening from Yazi into the editor. Treat this as a tab-targeting bug in the active-tab and cwd-sync flow: commands and editor-launch flows must resolve and rename only the current Zellij tab, never an adjacent tab or another tab in the session.
design: Start from first principles: yzx cwd should act on the active Zellij tab the user invoked it from, not on whichever tab state file or tab identifier was last touched elsewhere in the session. Verify how the command resolves the current tab identity, how that identity is translated into a tab rename target, and whether stale cross-tab state, pane-to-tab mapping, or tab-index math can redirect the rename one tab to the left. Prefer a fix that uses stable current-tab identifiers rather than inferred position or most-recently-updated state.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear reproduction or at least instrumentation for the wrong-tab rename bug in a multi-tab session. Running yzx cwd from the active tab renames only that active tab, and opening from Yazi into the editor also renames only that same active tab. Neither flow may rename the tab to the left or any other tab in the session. Add focused validation for active-tab resolution and a regression check covering at least two or three tabs with distinct names and cwd values, with logs showing the invoked tab, resolved tab identifier, triggering action, and final renamed tab.
close_reason: Cannot reproduce

title: Deduplicate Zellij popup wrapper bootstrap
description: The Zellij popup wrapper scripts still duplicate the same runtime-script resolution and wrapper bootstrap pattern. Replace that duplication with one shared runtime helper so popup/menu wrappers stay aligned with the canonical floating-wrapper contract.
close_reason: Completed

title: DRY shared runtime tree assembly across flake and nixpkgs package outputs
description:
The Yazelix packaging layer still duplicates the common runtime tree assembly across `yazelix_runtime_package.nix` and `mk_yazelix_package.nix`. Both build nearly the same runtime content: linked repo directories, top-level config/docs files, `bin/nu`, and the generated `bin/yzx` launcher, then diverge only in the final wrapper and package-shape details.

That duplication is now one of the clearest remaining packaging cleanup targets. It increases maintenance cost and makes authority changes, runtime-file additions, and install-path fixes easier to apply inconsistently.
design:
Keep the current top-level split: one flake/runtime artifact and one nixpkgs-style wrapped package. Do not try to collapse those two products into a single generic builder. Instead, extract one small shared helper for the common runtime tree assembly and let the final package files keep only the wrapper-specific behavior.

The shared helper should own the repeated linked directories/files, the common `bin/nu`, and the common `bin/yzx` launcher surface. Wrapper-specific PATH setup and any final package-shape differences should remain local to the callers. Prefer a narrow helper such as `mk_runtime_tree.nix` over a mini-framework.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The common runtime tree assembly no longer has to be edited in two places. 2. `yazelix_runtime_package.nix` and `mk_yazelix_package.nix` remain separate at the top level but share one maintained implementation for the repeated runtime tree contents. 3. Existing flake install, nixpkgs package, and nixpkgs submission validators still pass after the refactor. 4. The refactor does not reintroduce a second authority for the runtime-owned `devenv` path or weaken the current package/install contract.
close_reason: Completed: extracted shared runtime tree assembly into mk_runtime_tree.nix and kept only wrapper-specific behavior in the package callers.

title: Verify whether the LiteLLM PyPI compromise affected Yazelix
description: Audit whether the malicious LiteLLM PyPI releases affected Yazelix directly or through any shipped dependency or package surface.
notes: Assessment: no litellm or LiteLLM references were found in the repo, shipped config, or package declarations. Yazelix does not appear to ship or pin LiteLLM directly in its maintained runtime surface, so this issue is treated as not affected.
close_reason: Not affected: no litellm or LiteLLM references were found in the repo, shipped config, or maintained package declarations, so Yazelix does not appear to ship the compromised PyPI package in its supported runtime surface.

title: Add a misc pack and place tokei there
description: Imported GitHub issue #519.
close_reason: Completed: the misc pack with tokei is already in the catalog, default packs file, Home Manager module, and docs, and GitHub issue #519 is closed.

title: Add logo-forward welcome style
design: Add the branded baseline welcome style that should work as the strongest static final frame and as a short animated logo-forward reveal. It should adapt to narrow, medium, and wide layouts and define the overall visual language the other styles should respect. The implementation may use lightweight live frames or pre-rendered playback, but it must land on a clear final branded frame.
acceptance_criteria: Selecting welcome_style = logo produces a branded welcome sequence that fits narrow layouts, ends in a readable resting frame, and is defended by width-aware layout tests plus startup render checks.
close_reason: Completed

title: Enforce default suite shape and budget guards
description: Add validators that prevent default-suite test creep: no dead tests in default-lane files, canonical tests must stay spec-backed, canonical count budget, and default-suite time budget.
close_reason: Implemented default-suite shape/count and runtime-budget guards, wired into CI, and validated them against the current suite.

title: add a zellij plugin for fullscreen yzx screen
description:
Imported GitHub issue #530.

possibly add the feature for it to run after some minutes of inactivity

title: Investigate whether a Yazelix-specific zjstatus fork is needed for stable pane frames
description: Imported GitHub issue #521.
design: Treat this as a fork-worthiness investigation, not as an immediate implementation commitment. The purpose is to determine whether Yazelix can support real pane frames without severe flicker while keeping the current zjstatus-style top bar, or whether a Yazelix-specific zjstatus fork is the only honest path. Compare the realistic options: keep pane_frames false, prototype upstream zjframes, or fork zjstatus. The outcome should be a concrete recommendation with explicit reasoning and, if a fork is justified, a clearly scoped follow-up rather than a vague aspiration.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The repo has a concrete written recommendation on whether Yazelix should fork zjstatus for stable pane-frame support. 2. The recommendation explains why the non-fork alternatives are sufficient or insufficient. 3. If a fork is recommended, the issue leaves behind a clear next-step scope rather than a vague idea.
notes: Planning refinement: keep this investigation in three explicit phases: reproduce and classify the current flicker behavior, evaluate the upstream zjframes path under the same scenarios, then make a concrete fork-versus-nonfork recommendation. This should avoid a vague all-at-once investigation.
close_reason: Obsolete: the pane-frame fork investigation was superseded by the simpler direct pane_frames path without zjframes.

title: Reproduce and classify pane-frame flicker with the current zjstatus stack
description: Create a concrete behavior matrix for the current pane-frame problem so the fork decision is grounded in observed failure modes rather than memory or vague impressions.
design: Keep this as a focused reproduction and classification pass. Verify the current behavior across the scenarios that matter: pane_frames off or on, single or multiple panes, search or scroll modes, fullscreen, and any obvious transparency interactions. The result should identify which combinations actually flicker or redraw badly.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The current pane-frame behavior is reproduced in a small concrete scenario matrix. 2. The investigation records which combinations are stable, unstable, or visually unacceptable. 3. Later fork or non-fork decisions can point at this matrix instead of anecdotes.
close_reason: Obsolete: pane-frame flicker investigation was superseded by the simpler direct pane_frames path without zjframes or plugin-driven frame toggling.

title: Evaluate the upstream zjframes path against the same pane-frame matrix
description: Spike the upstream zjframes-based alternative under the same scenarios as the baseline reproduction so Yazelix can compare real behavior instead of guessing whether it helps.
design: Do not treat zjframes as automatically good or bad. Evaluate it under the same matrix as the baseline so the comparison is honest. The point is to learn whether the upstream non-fork path actually produces a stable usable pane-frame experience for Yazelix, not just whether it technically toggles frames.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The upstream zjframes path is tested against the same scenario matrix as the current baseline. 2. The repo records whether zjframes materially improves, fails to improve, or worsens the pane-frame experience. 3. The comparison is concrete enough to inform the final fork recommendation.
close_reason: Obsolete: the upstream zjframes evaluation is no longer relevant after adopting direct pane_frames without zjframes.

title: Decide whether a Yazelix-specific zjstatus fork is warranted for pane frames
description: Use the baseline reproduction and the upstream zjframes evaluation to make a concrete recommendation on whether Yazelix should keep pane frames off, rely on upstream alternatives, or pursue a fork.
design: This is the decision bead, not another open-ended investigation. Compare the baseline and zjframes results, explain the user-experience tradeoffs clearly, and recommend either keeping frames off, pursuing an upstream path, or creating a scoped fork follow-up. If a fork is recommended, define the first concrete scope instead of leaving a vague aspiration.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The repo has a concrete recommendation for pane-frame support based on observed evidence, not guesswork. 2. The recommendation explains why non-fork paths are sufficient or insufficient. 3. If a fork is recommended, the next implementation scope is named clearly.
close_reason: Obsolete: the fork-versus-zjframes decision is no longer the active problem after moving to direct pane_frames without zjframes.

title: Split demo GIFs into smaller pieces
description: Split the large demo GIF story into several smaller focused demos so users can understand specific Yazelix workflows without one heavyweight clip doing all the work.
design: The goal is not just smaller files. Each demo should communicate one idea clearly: onboarding, Yazi plus Helix flow, workspace switching, and so on. Coordinate with the preview-asset and git-history cleanup beads so the final asset set is coherent and lightweight.
acceptance_criteria: The docs and website use smaller focused demo assets instead of a single oversized catch-all GIF. Asset file sizes, placement, and narrative purpose are documented in a capture checklist or logged plan so the set remains maintainable.

title: Separate environment-backend concerns from workspace UX and prepare a future Yazelix Core edition
description: Drive the next modularity pass around a clearer separation between Yazelix's environment backend concerns and its workspace/runtime UX layer. The immediate goal is to improve main while keeping devenv: define backend-facing contracts, add a shared runtime preflight checker, reduce hardcoded backend assumptions in the command surface, and classify what would remain viable in a future no-package-management edition. The longer-term motivation is to make a future Yazelix Core edition possible without turning main into a confused multi-backend product.
design: Build on the already-closed modularity framing work in docs/architecture_map.md and docs/workspace_session_contract.md instead of reopening subsystem-boundary debate from scratch. This epic is about making main cleaner while still using devenv: clarify backend-facing contracts, reduce ad hoc backend coupling in the command/bootstrap layer, and make a future Core edition a consequence of better boundaries rather than a speculative fork. Adjacent existing beads such as yazelix-2ex.2.1, yazelix-b16x.1, and yazelix-uwz5 should be treated as parallel cleanup inputs, not displaced by this epic.
acceptance_criteria: There is a coherent bead sequence that sharpens the environment-backend boundary, introduces a shared runtime/preflight dependency story, classifies the current CLI surface by backend reliance, and leaves behind an evidence-based recommendation for or against a future Yazelix Core edition. Main gets cleaner without promising multi-backend support in place.

title: Define and reduce cross-language ownership boundaries in the runtime/workspace stack
description: Map the current hot-path behavior that crosses POSIX shell, Nushell, Rust plugins, Lua Yazi plugins, and Zellij CLI behavior, then define which language/runtime should own which category of logic going forward. Use that map to identify which seams deserve consolidation rather than letting correctness continue to span too many runtimes.
design: Treat this as an ownership-and-simplification bead, not a rewrite-everything proposal. The current stack is defensible but expensive to debug because too much user-visible behavior crosses language boundaries. Identify the long-lived stateful logic, the thin adapter logic, and the incidental bootstrap glue. Prefer moving hot-path correctness and session ownership into fewer layers while keeping the CLI UX and integration adapters intentionally thin.
acceptance_criteria: 1. There is an explicit language/runtime ownership map for CLI UX, runtime orchestration, live workspace/session state, and integration adapters. 2. The map identifies at least one high-value seam that should collapse into a stronger single owner. 3. Follow-up implementation beads become narrower and aligned with that ownership model. 4. The work informs the broader backend/Core discussion instead of competing with it.

title: Postmortem: session-global Yazi sidebar state caused cross-tab cwd leaks
description: Symptom: yzx cwd appeared to change the cwd of other tabs, and the same class of issue was suspected around Yazi-driven path changes. Initial suspicion was a pane-orchestrator tab-isolation leak. Actual cause: read_active_sidebar_state in nushell/scripts/integrations/yazi.nu selected the most recently modified Yazi state file for the whole session, so sidebar sync could target another tab Yazi pane. Because every tab starts with Yazi, this produced a real cross-tab leak in normal usage. Fix: resolve the current tab sidebar_pane_id via debug_editor_state, then read that exact sidebar state file. Verified in a disposable two-tab Yazelix session.
design: Keep this as a regression postmortem rather than implementation work. The value is preserving the causal chain: session-global sidebar state looked convenient, but it violated tab locality and produced real cross-tab cwd leaks. Capture the invariant that any sidebar or path-sync state must be keyed to the current tab or pane identity rather than global recency.
acceptance_criteria: The bead clearly documents the regression cause, the violated tab-locality invariant, the final fix, and the guardrail future work should preserve. A maintainer touching sidebar state later can use this bead to avoid reintroducing session-global leaks.

title: Fix Ghostty cursor effect shader path mapping
description: Ghostty cursor color changes still apply, but the cursor effect shaders do not render. Investigation shows the generated Ghostty config references effect shader paths like `./shaders/generated_effects/tail.glsl`, while the shader builder currently emits files named `cursor_trail.glsl`, `cursor_ripple_rectangle.glsl`, and similar. That mismatch leaves Ghostty with a flat colored cursor and no trail or mode-change effect.
close_reason: Completed: Ghostty cursor effect shaders were broken by a generated filename mismatch; the builder now emits the user-facing effect filenames that the generated Ghostty config references.

title: investigate how to integrate cool images
description: Imported GitHub issue #501.

title: Add bottom-terminal family to the no-side layout cycle
description: GitHub issue #491 asks for the no-sidebar layout family to include the same bottom-terminal variant that exists in the sidebar-aware family cycle. The change should add the missing bottom_terminal swap layout to yzx_no_side.swap.kdl and keep it in generated layouts.
close_reason: Added the missing bottom_terminal family to yzx_no_side.swap.kdl and verified generated no-side layouts keep it.

title: Replace local pi_agent_rust packaging with upstream flake
description:
Track the follow-on work for removing Yazelix's local `pi_agent_rust` derivation once upstream ships a real flake surface. Codex does not need a matching bead because `openai/codex` already exposes `flake.nix` and `flake.lock`.

Scope:
- monitor upstream `pi_agent_rust` flake support
- when a flake exists, replace the local `buildRustPackage` path in `devenv.nix`
- prefer consuming upstream flake outputs instead of carrying custom packaging in Yazelix
- keep the current `pi_rust` user-facing alias stable while changing only the packaging source under the hood

Success means Yazelix no longer needs to maintain a local Nix package definition for `pi_agent_rust`
close_reason: Obsolete after removing pi_rust support from Yazelix

title: teste issue
description: Imported GitHub issue #454.
close_reason: Closed on GitHub

title: Improve yzx run argument pass-through for external commands
description: Improve yzx run so argument pass-through to external commands is predictable and does not silently reshape argv in surprising ways.
design: Treat this as a command-surface contract issue. Clarify exactly what yzx run should pass through, where Yazelix-specific parsing should stop, and how quoting, separators, and variadic arguments should behave for external commands. Avoid compatibility hacks that preserve ambiguous behavior; the end state should be a clear argv contract that maintainers can test directly.
acceptance_criteria: There is a defined argv pass-through contract for yzx run, including representative examples of external command invocation. The bead leaves behind either an implementation path or a documented decision on what ambiguous legacy behavior will not be preserved.

title: slow yzx env
description:
Imported GitHub issue #509.

it takes a while to load

title: Create a bead-to-issue contract with no lifecycle drift
description: Imported GitHub issue #452. Define and implement the shared-subset contract between GitHub issues and Beads so the mapping and open/closed lifecycle cannot silently drift.
close_reason: Closed on GitHub

title: Add validator for config surface and launch-profile parity
description: Add a validator that enforces the canonical config-surface and launch-profile contract once that contract is defined, so drift is caught mechanically instead of by maintainer memory.
design: This bead should not invent the contract; it should defend the canonical config-surface and launch-profile ownership model from yazelix-b16x together with the machine-readable main-config contract introduced by yazelix-0oj1.1. Keep the validator high-signal: check the maintained parity and ownership rules that actually matter, and avoid noisy pseudo-invariants that mainly create churn. The right validator may be structural, config-based, or generation-based depending on what the canonical contract ultimately exposes.
acceptance_criteria: There is a validator for the canonical config-surface and launch-profile contract, and it checks the maintained source-of-truth invariants that the contract explicitly requires. The validator is justified by the contract rather than by general anxiety about drift.
close_reason: Completed: added config surface and launch-profile contract validator with CI and pre-commit integration
