title: Add data-driven Ghostty cursor builder
description: Add a constrained, data-driven Ghostty cursor builder that lets users choose a supported cursor family and supply 2-3 colors in ~/.config/yazelix/yazelix_cursors.toml while preserving all existing named presets. Generate runtime shader output from structured Yazelix-owned config rather than exposing raw GLSL or terminal-native shader editing.
design: Keep this builder deliberately narrow and schema-driven. The first slice should cover the families that already map cleanly to shared shader helpers, specifically simple_dual, orbit_dual, and axis_gradient, with exact color-count validation per family. Keep existing preset names working unchanged and add one custom or builder selection path that reads from yazelix_cursors.toml. Fail fast on invalid family names, wrong color counts, invalid hex colors, or incomplete config; do not silently fall back to a preset. Generate the custom shader into the normal runtime Ghostty shader location and keep the tracked source variants as the curated preset library. Avoid adding freeform GLSL, timing knobs, or a general shader DSL in this bead. Because this becomes a supported user configuration surface, define the direct-config and Home Manager story explicitly rather than leaving one path ambiguous.
acceptance_criteria: Users can select a custom Ghostty cursor family plus 2-3 colors through ~/.config/yazelix/yazelix_cursors.toml, and Yazelix generates a working runtime shader without regressing existing named presets. The feature works coherently for both direct config users and Home Manager users, with no silent divergence between the two paths. Add focused unit tests for sidecar parsing, schema validation, family-to-renderer routing, exact color-count enforcement, and invalid-config failures. Add end-to-end config-generation tests with detailed logs that show the input sidecar config, resolved family, normalized colors, selected output shader path, and generated shader identifiers/constants. Include at least one e2e case per supported family, one direct-config case, one Home Manager case, and one regression case proving named presets still work when no sidecar builder config is present.

title: new command: yzx open or yzx o (alias)
description: Imported GitHub issue #455.
close_reason: Not planned: yzx open is too ambiguous as a top-level command, and the closest likely intent already exists as yzx config open. Revisit only if it is rewritten as a narrowly scoped command contract.

title: add readme entries the user_configs/ subfolders
description: Imported GitHub issue #481.
close_reason: Completed

title: Consolidate Home Manager flake surface into the top-level flake
description: Reduce packaging-surface duplication by moving the Home Manager flake entrypoint to the top-level Yazelix flake. The goal is one canonical flake surface that exposes both the install/runtime package and `homeManagerModules.default`, with a deliberate migration path for the current `?dir=home_manager` input users.
design: Treat this as flake-surface consolidation, not a packaging side quest. The top-level flake should become the canonical entrypoint for both Yazelix install/runtime outputs and the Home Manager module, with a deliberate compatibility story for current users of the separate ?dir=home_manager path. Prefer deleting duplicate flake surface area over adding another forwarding layer unless a short migration bridge is genuinely necessary.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The top-level flake exposes the canonical Home Manager module surface alongside the existing install/runtime package surface. 2. The repo no longer relies on the separate home_manager flake entrypoint as the primary story. 3. Existing users of the current ?dir=home_manager path have a clear migration note or temporary compatibility path. 4. Docs and examples point to one canonical flake surface after the change.

title: Fix Yazelix-provided Ghostty runtime integration
description: The Yazelix-managed Ghostty path is still less reliable than it should be. Recent install and launch regressions showed that the packaged/default Ghostty flow can fail because of runtime-root propagation, wrapper assumptions, desktop-launch differences, or other Yazelix-managed integration bugs.
design: Treat this as the end-to-end Ghostty integration bug, not a single wrapper tweak. Audit the Yazelix-provided Ghostty path across install, yzx launch, restart, and desktop entry launch. Separate Ghostty-host problems from Yazelix-owned runtime/env/config bugs, then fix the Yazelix-owned path so the default managed Ghostty experience is reliable without local handholding.
acceptance_criteria: A normal installed Yazelix setup can launch through the managed Ghostty path from yzx launch and the desktop entry without clone-path assumptions or stale-wrapper failures. The remaining Ghostty-specific host limitations, if any, are explicit and documented rather than surfacing as silent Yazelix breakage.
notes: New evidence on 2026-04-01: plain nix-provided Ghostty also fails on the maintainer's hybrid Intel+NVIDIA Linux host outside the Yazelix wrapper path. Direct probe: devenv shell -- bash -lc 'timeout 8 ghostty --gtk-single-instance=false -e true' reproduced the OpenGL/EGL failure. Treat remaining Yazelix-owned wrapper/runtime issues separately from the possibility that nixpkgs Ghostty itself is not a safe default on some Linux hosts.
close_reason: Fixed by stabilizing the managed Ghostty launch path across install, yzx launch, and desktop launch, and removing stale-wrapper dependence from the installed runtime path.

title: Tighten the test contract further and cut LOC, especially Nushell and tests
description: Investigate how to make the test contract stricter while reducing overall LOC. Focus especially on Nushell code size and the test suite footprint, since a significant chunk of the LOC growth is test code.
notes:
Progress on 2026-03-29:
- Extracted shared managed-config fixture and e2e log helpers into nushell/scripts/dev/test_yzx_helpers.nu.
- Reduced duplicated plumbing across test_yzx_core_commands.nu, test_config_migrate_e2e.nu, test_stale_config_diagnostics_e2e.nu, and test_upgrade_summary_e2e.nu.
- Reduced duplicated doctor/config fixture setup across test_yzx_doctor_commands.nu and test_yzx_generated_configs.nu.
- Fixed stale test setup assumptions that still wrote managed config to legacy root paths instead of user_configs/.
- Landed in commits c62577c and 8f1383e.
Remaining work:
- Continue scanning for honest shared-fixture reductions in remaining high-duplication Nushell tests.
- Keep removing only setup/plumbing duplication; preserve strong behavior coverage and avoid weak existence tests.
close_reason: Tightened the default-suite contract to spec-backed entrypoints only, removed regression-only sidecars, and pruned low-signal tests to cut default-suite time and LOC.

title: Evaluate replacing yzx gc's du store-size probe with a modern Rust tool
description:
Investigate whether Yazelix should replace the current `du -sb /nix/store` size probe used by `yzx gc` with a modern Rust-based alternative such as `dust`, `dua`, or `gdu`.

Scope:
- identify which candidate tools are realistically available in the Yazelix environment or Nixpkgs
- verify whether any candidate provides a stable script-friendly single-path total size mode suitable for automation
- compare startup/runtime cost and output stability against `du -sb`
- avoid switching just for novelty; the replacement must be at least as robust for scripted GC summaries

Success means there is a clear decision: either adopt one concrete Rust tool for the probe with a clean contract, or document why `du -sb` should remain the implementation.
design: Judge replacement candidates by scriptability and robustness first, not by novelty or a Rust implementation. The current du probe may be ugly but acceptable if it is stable, available, and cheap enough. Any replacement must provide a clean single-path total-size contract, acceptable runtime, and predictable output that Yazelix can safely automate.
acceptance_criteria: There is a concrete keep or replace decision for the store-size probe. If replaced, one tool and invocation contract are chosen. If not, the bead records why the current du-based probe remains the better maintained option despite the appeal of modern tooling.

title: Audit remaining source-vs-installed runtime identity leaks after the launch-path cleanup
description: After the source-shell export cleanup lands, re-audit the remaining runtime identity surfaces. Check launch, doctor, generated shell hooks, popup/wrapper handoff, and maintainer probes for places where an installed runtime path can still shadow a source checkout or vice versa. Delete redundant aliases and document any remaining intentional split between inferred runtime and installed runtime reference.
notes:
2026-04-04 finding: desktop relaunch latency traced to runtime-identity hash churn, not desktop dispatch itself. The installed runtime package symlinks its nushell tree into a Nix -source path, so path-self inference inside common.nu could resolve the source tree even when YAZELIX_RUNTIME_DIR explicitly pointed at the installed runtime. Because compute_config_state hashes the runtime path, that heuristic made combined_hash oscillate between installed-runtime and source-tree identities and forced rebuilds on reopen. The fix direction is to trust an explicit valid YAZELIX_RUNTIME_DIR in installed flows, while keeping repo-shell DEVENV_ROOT as the user-facing source identity and using a separate setup-only runtime root for shellHook bookkeeping.

Deeper follow-on tightening from this audit is now split under yazelix-le0s: le0s.2 for fail-fast runtime root resolution, le0s.3 for explicit runtime-project materialization, le0s.4 for narrower profile resolvers, and le0s.6 for deprecating generic root aliases.

title: Remove the need to clone the Yazelix repo under ~/.config/yazelix
description: Eliminate any remaining requirement for end users to keep a full Yazelix git clone under ~/.config/yazelix in order to run the product normally.
design: Treat this as a runtime-boundary and packaging-quality issue. First identify which paths still assume a live repo checkout instead of a packaged or generated runtime layout. Then decide whether the correct fix is packaging changes, relocated runtime assets, generated config changes, or documentation cleanup. Avoid local-host workarounds that keep the clone requirement alive implicitly.
acceptance_criteria: Normal Yazelix usage no longer requires a git clone living under ~/.config/yazelix. Any remaining maintainer-only clone assumptions are explicit and documented. The final behavior works through the supported packaged or relocated runtime paths, not only from a source checkout.
close_reason: Runtime entrypoints and tracked terminal snapshots no longer require a repo clone under ~/.config/yazelix

title: Define the package-ready Yazelix runtime/config root contract
description: Make the config root versus runtime asset root split explicit for non-clone installs. Clarify which environment variables and helpers own user config under ~/.config/yazelix versus shipped/runtime assets under the installed runtime location.
close_reason: Superseded by completed child yazelix-hac.2

title: Define the package-ready Yazelix runtime/config root contract
description: Make the config root versus runtime asset root split explicit for non-clone installs. Clarify which environment variables and helpers own user config under ~/.config/yazelix versus shipped/runtime assets under the installed runtime location.
close_reason: Completed: added the runtime root contract spec and linked it from the architecture map.

title: Make shell, terminal, and desktop entrypoints resolve runtime assets without a repo clone
description: Remove launcher assumptions that Yazelix scripts live under ~/.config/yazelix. Terminal wrappers, desktop entry launchers, and startup entrypoints should resolve shipped runtime assets from the installed runtime root while keeping user config under the config root.
close_reason: Completed: POSIX launchers, devenv wrappers, and yzx launch now resolve shipped runtime assets through the runtime root instead of assuming ~/.config/yazelix is a repo checkout.

title: Remove bundled Yazi runtime path assumptions about ~/.config/yazelix
description: Fix shipped Yazi configs, plugins, and related command launchers so they invoke Yazelix integration scripts through the runtime root rather than assuming a source checkout under ~/.config/yazelix.

title: Update installation and user-facing docs for non-clone Yazelix runtime installs
description: Revise docs and help text so normal usage no longer assumes users cloned the repo into ~/.config/yazelix. Keep maintainer/source-checkout instructions explicit where they still apply.

title: Fix yzx update runtime output ordering
description: yzx update runtime currently prints captured stdout before stderr from nix run, which makes Nix's build/unpack lines appear after the install app says the runtime is already installed. Preserve the real event order or otherwise render the build phase clearly so the update flow does not look like it creates a second runtime after install already completed.
close_reason: Stream yzx update runtime output directly so build/install phases stay in order

title: Make Yazelix modular
description: Imported GitHub issue #450. Create a concrete modularity plan for Yazelix so major subsystems can evolve with clearer boundaries instead of being coupled through ad-hoc shell glue and shared assumptions.
close_reason: Closing the modularity epic: the core modularity framing work has landed, and the remaining follow-up work now continues as standalone beads instead of staying under this parent epic.

title: Define Yazelix subsystem ownership boundaries
description: Define a concrete ownership map for Yazelix subsystems so runtime/source, user config, generated state, workspace/session state, and Linux/desktop integrations stop bleeding into each other through implicit conventions. The output should be an explicit boundary document grounded in the current codebase, not a vague modularity aspiration.
close_reason: Documented the four-subsystem architecture map, ownership rules, and product-layer model in docs/architecture_map.md and linked it from the README

title: Define the workspace/session contract between Nushell and the pane orchestrator
description: Make the workspace/session boundary explicit between Nushell scripts and the Rust pane orchestrator. Define which side owns tab workspace roots, managed-pane identity, sidebar state, tab naming, and cross-tab isolation so future fixes stop rediscovering the same seam through bugs.
close_reason: Documented the current Nushell/plugin/Yazi workspace-session contract, ownership rules, command contract, invariants, and boundary gaps in docs/workspace_session_contract.md

title: Evaluate embedding a Beads viewer in the Yazelix website
description: Evaluate whether the Yazelix website should expose a Beads-based project viewer, and if so what form it should take: embedded live viewer, static snapshot, or simple external link.
design: Treat this as a product and maintenance question, not just a flashy website widget. Clarify the value to users and contributors, the hosting/update burden, and whether the public project graph should be live, periodically generated, or linked out to an existing viewer. Prefer the simplest surface that still gives meaningful roadmap visibility.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear decision on whether and how the Yazelix website should expose Beads data. If adopted, the bead leaves behind a concrete delivery shape such as embed, generated page, or external link. If rejected, it records why the maintenance cost or product value is not good enough.

title: Investigate why test_yzx_commands.nu takes so long
description: Investigate why test_yzx_commands.nu takes so long now that lightweight profiling exists, and separate genuinely slow contracts from unnecessary default-suite cost.
design: Use the profiling work from yazelix-q9mj rather than guessing. Break the runtime down by major test segments, identify whether the cost comes from real high-signal behavior checks or from avoidable setup and churn, and decide whether the right fix is optimization, lane reclassification, or test deletion. Keep the test-governance spec in mind: slower tests are acceptable only if they defend important behavior.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear explanation for where test_yzx_commands.nu time is going, including the dominant slow segments and whether they are justified. The bead leaves behind concrete next steps such as optimize, split into another lane, or keep as-is with explicit justification.
close_reason: Closed on GitHub

title: Implement a shared runtime contract checker and reuse it across launch and doctor
description: Implement a small shared runtime contract checker for the overlap between launch-preflight and `yzx doctor`, instead of scattering the same dependency reasoning across launch, startup, and doctor-specific helpers. The checker should stay lightweight enough for normal entrypoint use, return structured results, and give both launch and doctor one authoritative story for the checks they truly share. This is not a mega-doctor rewrite: the first slice should focus only on the runtime blockers and dependency conditions already called out by `yazelix-j4qv`.
design: Treat this as a narrow shared-evaluation layer plus separate presentation layers. The checker should probably live in a small runtime-validation module and return structured records with fields such as check id, status, severity, owner surface, and recovery hint. Ground the first slice directly in the current overlap seams instead of inventing a giant abstraction: `start_yazelix.nu` currently owns startup working-directory and generated-layout/runtime-script checks, `yzx/launch.nu` owns runtime launch-script checks before re-entry, `core/launch_yazelix.nu` owns new-window working-directory and terminal-availability checks, and `doctor.nu` owns richer rendering/reporting. The first implementation slice should cover only the checks that both launch and doctor can honestly share today: working-directory validity, required runtime entrypoint scripts, required generated layout presence, and configured terminal availability for new-window launch. Launch should consume only hard blockers from that result set and fail fast. `yzx doctor` should be able to render the same result set more fully, but doctor-only and install-only surfaces should remain separate for now. Do not try to absorb config-migration preflight, shell-hook freshness, desktop-entry freshness, version drift, Helix runtime conflicts, plugin/session-local health, or heavy install-smoke invariants into the first shared checker just because they are all “health” concerns. This bead should also standardize the result vocabulary enough that later Rust work (`yazelix-kt5.3`) has a clean target without forcing a daemon or giant abstraction now.
acceptance_criteria: Yazelix has one shared runtime contract checker used by both launch-facing code and `yzx doctor` for the overlapping runtime dependency checks covered by `yazelix-j4qv`. The checker returns structured results rather than only ad hoc error strings. The first shared slice covers at least: missing or invalid working directory, missing runtime entrypoint scripts, missing generated layout required for startup, and configured terminal availability for new-window launch. Startup and launch entrypoints consume the shared checker instead of keeping those checks split across `start_yazelix.nu`, `yzx/launch.nu`, and `core/launch_yazelix.nu`, while `yzx doctor` can surface the same shared results without reimplementing their core reasoning separately. Doctor-only diagnostics such as shell-hook freshness, desktop-entry freshness, version drift, Helix runtime conflicts, plugin health, and heavy install/package checks remain outside the first shared checker unless they clearly fit the same runtime contract result model. The implementation stays lightweight enough for normal launch use and does not regress startup into a slow audit path.
close_reason: Implemented shared runtime contract checker and reused it across launch and doctor

title: Investigate repo-root terminal_overrides regeneration
description: Investigate and fix why an untracked terminal_overrides/ directory is being recreated in the repository root during terminal config generation instead of staying inside the supported Yazelix config or runtime paths.
design: Treat this as a real path-resolution bug, not just local clutter. Identify the exact writer and the exact path-resolution mistake that creates repo-root terminal_overrides/. Then tighten the contract so override scaffolds and generated files can only land in supported locations such as ~/.config/yazelix/terminal_overrides or the runtime share tree. Avoid one-off local cleanup hacks; fix the path derivation at the source and document the intended destination. If tests or dev commands are responsible, make the fixture or runtime root explicit so they cannot accidentally write into the repository.
acceptance_criteria: No command or test path used by Yazelix recreates terminal_overrides/ in the repository root. The real source of the bug is identified and fixed, and the intended destination for override files is documented. Add focused unit or helper tests for the path-resolution function that was wrong. Add an end-to-end regression with detailed logging that runs the reproducing generation flow from the repo root and proves that only the supported config or runtime locations are written.

title: Fix config validation for optional TOML fields
description: Yazelix config validation currently infers the allowed schema from the parsed default TOML surface, which means optional fields that only exist as commented examples in yazelix_default.toml are treated as unknown. Comments should not affect validation in any way. Fix the validator so supported optional fields like helix.runtime_path are accepted based on a real schema/source-of-truth rather than whether the default TOML happens to materialize that key after parsing. The doctor and startup diagnostics should then report real config problems instead of false unknown-field errors for documented optional settings.
close_reason: GitHub issue #499 is closed; tracker synced.

title: yzx update devenv should also sync the devenv lock input
description:
## Problem

`yzx update devenv` only runs `nix profile install` to update the devenv CLI binary, but does not update the implicit `devenv` input in `devenv.lock`. This leaves the lock pinned to an older module revision, causing devenv to print a version-skew warning on every `devenv shell` / `yzx env`:

    ✨ devenv 2.0.7 is newer than devenv input (2.0.6) in devenv.lock. Run 'devenv update' to sync.

The devenv input (`github:cachix/devenv?dir=src/modules`) is mandatory — devenv injects it unconditionally in its generated flake. It cannot be removed. The maintainer-facing `yzx dev update devenv` already calls `devenv update devenv` to sync the lock, but the user-facing command does not.

## Proposed Fix

After `yzx update devenv` successfully installs/updates the CLI, also run `devenv update devenv` in the yazelix directory to re-lock the devenv modules input to match the new CLI version.

### Location

`nushell/scripts/core/yazelix.nu`, `yzx update devenv` command (lines ~399-476). After the `nix profile install` succeeds, add:

```nushell
# Sync the devenv lock input to match the new CLI
let yazelix_dir = (get_yazelix_runtime_dir)
cd $yazelix_dir
^(resolve_preferred_devenv_path) update devenv
```

### Alternatives considered

- `devenv.warnOnNewVersion = false` — suppresses the warning but hides legitimate drift. Not a real fix.
- Telling users to run `devenv update devenv` manually — bad UX, the whole point of `yzx update devenv` is a single command.
- Dropping the devenv input — impossible, devenv requires it for module evaluation.

## Acceptance Criteria

- After `yzx update devenv`, the devenv lock input matches the CLI version (no skew warning on next `yzx env`).
- If the lock sync fails, print a warning but don't fail the overall command (the CLI update already succeeded).
close_reason: Obsolete after the runtime-owned devenv cleanup removed yzx update devenv; the remaining version-skew architecture work is tracked in yazelix-15bx.

title: investigate session management
description: Imported GitHub issue #522.

title: Optimize Zellij config generation on warm startup
description:
The new structured startup profiler shows that warm current-terminal startup is dominated by Zellij config generation in start_yazelix_inner.nu, typically around 2.9s to 3.3s on the maintainer machine. That makes this the highest-value warm-start performance follow-up after yazelix-3n1.

Design:
Keep this delete-first. First identify which parts of merged Zellij config generation are unnecessarily redone on every warm startup and which artifacts can be reused safely without weakening config correctness. Prefer deleting redundant regeneration or broad invalidation before micro-optimizing string processing. Preserve fail-fast behavior and managed-config correctness.

Acceptance Criteria:
1. The current bottleneck inside inner.generate_zellij_config is broken down enough to show which substeps dominate. 2. Warm startup avoids clearly redundant Zellij config work when inputs have not changed. 3. The change does not make generated Zellij state stale across real config/runtime changes. 4. A focused regression or profiling-oriented check defends the intended reuse or invalidation boundary.

title: Apply test-prune deletions and simplify runner surfaces
close_reason: Completed

title: Manual-install to Home Manager takeover should not require manual cleanup of ~/.local/bin/yzx
description: A supported manual-install to Home Manager takeover still fails because Home Manager refuses to replace the existing ~/.local/bin/yzx launcher. During validation we had to move that file aside manually before home-manager switch could succeed. Track this as a real takeover bug, not just a docs gap.
design: Delete-first: do not normalize manual cleanup as the supported path. Either remove the launcher collision from the architecture, or give users an explicit ownership-safe takeover flow that handles installer-owned artifacts before Home Manager activation. The supported migration contract should make it obvious what Home Manager owns, what Yazelix owns, and when a preexisting manual launcher must be cleaned up automatically versus refused with a precise instruction.
acceptance_criteria: A user with an existing manual Yazelix install can switch to the documented Home Manager path without manually moving ~/.local/bin/yzx. The resulting ownership story is explicit and doctor/docs align with the supported takeover path.

title: Clean-room first-use Yazelix entries should not require pre-existing .bashrc and Nushell config
description: A real clean-room Home Manager activation can own runtime/current, ~/.local/bin/yzx, and the managed TOML surfaces correctly, but the first noninteractive entry paths still assume host shell config files already exist. In validation, `yzx run which uv` emitted a hard bash-config error until empty ~/.bashrc and ~/.config/nushell/config.nu files were seeded manually. This is not a Home Manager ownership problem; it is a first-use startup/materialization bug that still leaks host-dotfile assumptions into the user path.

title: Define the runtime dependency contract and launch preflight checker scope
description:
Specify the runtime dependency contract that normal user-facing entrypoints actually rely on, and define the scope of a preflight checker that should validate that contract before launch. Cover required versus optional dependencies, config-conditioned requirements, runtime assets versus host tools, and the boundary between lightweight launch-preflight checks and heavier doctor or install-smoke flows.

The contract should explain at least:
- always-required launch/startup prerequisites
- config-conditioned requirements such as terminal availability
- the difference between runtime-owned assets and host/external tools
- what launch should check quickly
- what doctor may check more deeply
- what only install/package validation should defend
design:
Treat this as a contract-first task, but not as the generic backend contract. Start from the current launch, startup, doctor, and install-smoke behavior and write down what the runtime layer is actually promising when a user runs Yazelix.

Ground it in the current code seams:
- `nushell/scripts/core/start_yazelix.nu`
- `nushell/scripts/core/start_yazelix_inner.nu`
- `nushell/scripts/core/launch_yazelix.nu`
- `nushell/scripts/utils/terminal_launcher.nu`
- `nushell/scripts/utils/doctor.nu`
- `nushell/scripts/utils/entrypoint_config_migrations.nu`
- `nushell/scripts/dev/validate_installed_runtime_contract.nu`
- `nushell/scripts/dev/validate_flake_install.nu`

Important boundary: config migration preflight remains adjacent but separate. Launch should still run it, but stale-config/migration semantics are not the same thing as runtime dependency checking.

This bead should complement the backend contract from `yazelix-6lkw`, not reopen it. The output should also stay honest for current full Yazelix while being reusable for a future Core discussion.
acceptance_criteria:
There is an explicit runtime dependency contract for user-facing entrypoints, with clear required, optional, and config-conditioned classifications.

The contract clearly separates:
- lightweight launch-preflight checks
- richer `yzx doctor` diagnostics
- heavier install/package validation

It is concrete enough to explain at least these current cases:
- missing or invalid working directory as an immediate launch blocker
- missing runtime script or generated layout as an immediate startup/runtime blocker
- unavailable configured terminal as a config-conditioned launch blocker for new-window launches
- stale shell hooks, desktop entries, install artifacts, version drift, and deeper runtime health as doctor-first or install-validation concerns rather than mandatory launch-preflight checks

A later bead should be able to implement one shared runtime checker from this contract without rediscovering the scope ad hoc.
close_reason: Runtime dependency/preflight contract written, issue #532 synced, launch preflight tightened for runtime scripts/layouts, and doctor-side desktop-entry freshness diagnostic defended.

title: Spec refresh, runtime update, and restart semantics across open Yazelix windows
description: Specify how shared runtime/materialized-state updates interact with already-open Yazelix windows.
design:
Yazelix now has clearer runtime/materialized-state ownership, but the user-visible meaning of `yzx refresh`, `yzx update runtime`, and `yzx restart` across multiple open windows still needs to be codified.

This task should define the durable-state versus live-session contract:
- `yzx refresh` rebuilds shared materialized state and launch-profile state; it should not silently hot-swap unrelated open windows
- `yzx update runtime` updates the installed runtime on disk; it should affect future launches, not mutate already-open windows in place
- `yzx restart` should be defined as an explicit live-session transition rather than a vague refresh alias

The spec should cover both the normal non-persistent case and the persistent-session exception, where restart scope may be session-wide because multiple windows are clients of one logical session.
acceptance_criteria:
1. The docs state that `yzx refresh` updates shared materialized state but does not hot-replace unrelated already-open windows.
2. The docs state that `yzx update runtime` updates the installed runtime for future launches and does not silently upgrade already-open live sessions in place.
3. The docs state that `yzx restart` is the explicit command that moves a live session onto newer durable state.
4. The docs state how the `yzx restart` contract differs between default non-persistent windows and persistent-session mode.
5. The resulting contract is specific enough to guide future fixes in launch, desktop launch, and session cleanup without re-deriving the UX each time.
notes:
This task is about cross-window semantics, not the internal launch-state resolver mechanics already covered by the `le0s` family.

Spec delivered:
- `docs/specs/open_window_update_transition_contract.md`

Current defense:
- `nu nushell/scripts/dev/validate_specs.nu`
- `nu nushell/scripts/dev/test_yzx_workspace_commands.nu`
close_reason: Completed: documented the open-window refresh, runtime update, and restart transition contract.

title: Replace text-based Zellij config merging with a typed render pipeline
description: Replace the current block-splitting and string-surgery approach in zellij_config_merger.nu with a typed intermediate model and deterministic renderer for Yazelix-owned Zellij output. The goal is to make plugin insertion, keybind merging, and managed overrides easier to reason about and less fragile.
design: Do not just extract more helper functions around the existing text manipulation. Define the owned concepts first: base config source, managed overrides, plugin/load_plugins entries, keybind fragments, and layout-family output. Then render Yazelix-owned output deterministically from that model. If partial parsing of user/native config is still needed, keep that boundary narrow and explicit instead of continuing broad top-level KDL text surgery.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Yazelix-owned Zellij output is rendered from an explicit intermediate model rather than ad hoc string/block splicing. 2. Plugin and load_plugins handling become deterministic and easier to validate. 3. Generated-config tests can defend semantic output with less fragile text trivia. 4. The resulting merger has a clearer ownership split between imported user/native input and Yazelix-owned rendered output.

title: Backfill upgrade notes for historical point releases
description: Retroactively add structured upgrade-note entries and concise changelog coverage for the historical point releases that shipped after Yazelix started doing point releases, so historical exact-version whats_new lookups can work from real note data instead of commit archaeology.
design: Build this on top of the existing upgrade-notes contract instead of inventing a second historical notes format. Prefer exact version entries in docs/upgrade_notes.toml for every supported historical point release, with matching concise sections in CHANGELOG.md. Keep the notes honest: if a release had no migration impact, say so; if migration or manual action cannot be reconstructed confidently, record the uncertainty conservatively rather than guessing. Define the practical historical floor for supported whats_new lookups so the CLI can fail clearly outside that window instead of pretending the archive is complete.
acceptance_criteria: docs/upgrade_notes.toml and CHANGELOG.md contain exact, validator-clean entries for the supported historical point releases in scope, with real dates, short headlines, and accurate upgrade-impact metadata. The historical entries are sufficient for a future exact-version yzx whats_new flow to render notes without scraping git history at runtime. Add focused verification for note integrity and at least one fixture-based or end-to-end check that historical exact-version lookup data can be loaded cleanly once version selection lands.
notes:
Planning refinements:
- Scope this to the era after Yazelix started shipping point releases; do not invent unsupported older history.
- Prefer reconstructing from real tags, commit ranges, release docs, and existing history pages.
- Keep the result narrow and high-signal: concise upgrade-facing notes, not full release essays.
- This bead should pair naturally with a future yzx whats_new <version> enhancement but should still be useful on its own as historical upgrade-note backfill.
close_reason: Completed: backfilled exact upgrade-note entries for the supported v12/v13 point-release floor, repaired the v13.7 structured-note baseline, generalized the upgrade-summary loader to accept explicit versions internally, and added default-suite plus detailed e2e verification for historical exact-version note loading.

title: Repo cleanup pass for legacy and unused artifacts
description: Do a deliberate cleanup pass across the repository to remove legacy, dead, or clearly unused files, commands, docs references, and compatibility paths that no longer serve the current Yazelix runtime model. Keep the pass conservative: prefer deleting things that are demonstrably stale over broad aesthetic churn.
design: Treat this as a conservative pruning pass, not an aesthetic refactor. Target legacy compatibility paths, stale docs references, dead commands, unused helper files, superseded repo-era assumptions, and external-tool artifacts that are no longer part of the intended Yazelix workflow. Every deletion should be justified by one of: no remaining callers, superseded by a newer path, no longer part of the supported public surface, or contradicted by the current architecture/runtime model. Prefer removal over replacement when behavior is no longer needed, and avoid broad style churn or speculative rewrites just to make the repo look cleaner.
acceptance_criteria: The repo leaves the pass with less unjustified surface area: at least one real stale, duplicated, superseded, or unused artifact is removed from the categories touched, any removed public surface is scrubbed from docs/help text, and the default test suite still passes. Leave behind a short inventory of what was removed versus intentionally kept. Net repo LOC should decrease unless a small amount of new code is explicitly justified as necessary to enable a larger cleanup or add a validator that supports future pruning.
notes: Partial progress already landed in 5253b00, 591edee, 7aef715, and 4857fcb, but the cleanup lane is not exhausted. Keep this bead open as an ongoing high-priority repo-hardening pass for deleting legacy, dead, redundant, or stale artifacts that are still discoverable.

title: Re-home package builder helper Nix files out of the repo root
description: Move root-level package-builder helper files that are implementation details rather than public entrypoints into packaging/, while keeping the top-level flake and the top-level package front doors stable.
design: Treat the helper builders as packaging internals. The public front doors can remain at the root: flake.nix, yazelix_package.nix, and yazelix_runtime_package.nix. The internal builder helpers such as locked_devenv_package.nix, mk_runtime_tree.nix, and mk_yazelix_package.nix do not need top-level discoverability and make the root harder to scan. Re-home them under packaging/ and update imports, validators, and packaging docs accordingly. Prefer a narrow move with no behavior change.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The internal package-builder helper Nix files no longer live at the repo root. 2. The top-level flake and package front doors still evaluate through the new locations. 3. Existing validators or packaging references that mention the old paths are updated. 4. The move reduces root-level clutter without changing the public install/package entrypoints. 5. Verification includes at least one package/validator check, not just path edits.
notes:
Completed as a narrow packaging-internal re-home.

Moved out of the repo root:
- locked_devenv_package.nix -> packaging/locked_devenv_package.nix
- mk_runtime_tree.nix -> packaging/mk_runtime_tree.nix
- mk_yazelix_package.nix -> packaging/mk_yazelix_package.nix

Kept at the repo root as public front doors:
- flake.nix
- yazelix_package.nix
- yazelix_runtime_package.nix

Updated callers and contract checks:
- flake imports
- top-level package front doors
- packaging/nixpkgs/yazelix_package.nix
- validate_installed_runtime_contract.nu

Verification:
- nix flake show --json >/dev/null
- nu nushell/scripts/dev/validate_installed_runtime_contract.nu
close_reason: Completed: package-builder helper Nix files now live under packaging/ while the public top-level flake and package front doors stay stable.

title: Add Mandelbrot welcome style
design: Add a Mandelbrot-inspired welcome style using precomputed frames or other very cheap deterministic playback rather than expensive live fractal rendering. The result should feel rich without turning startup into a renderer benchmark.
acceptance_criteria: Selecting welcome_style = mandelbrot renders the Mandelbrot sequence or frame set within the startup budget, preserves readability in narrow layouts, and is defended by tests for asset/frame selection and final-frame fit.

title: Add yzx dev bump for version and tag automation
description: Add a maintainer-facing yzx dev bump command that automates the release bump flow instead of doing version edits, commits, and tags by hand.
design: Implement a maintainer-only yzx dev bump command that computes or accepts the next version, updates nushell/scripts/utils/constants.nu so YAZELIX_VERSION matches the intended tag, creates a dedicated version bump commit, and creates the matching git tag. The command should fail fast on a dirty worktree, an existing conflicting tag, or a version mismatch between the file and the requested tag. The flow should be explicit and deterministic so maintainers do not need to remember the manual release steps.
acceptance_criteria: A maintainer can run yzx dev bump with an explicit version and the command updates nushell/scripts/utils/constants.nu to that version; the command creates a dedicated git commit for the version bump with a predictable message; the command creates the matching git tag after the commit succeeds; the command refuses to run when the git worktree is dirty; the command refuses to run when the requested tag already exists or conflicts with repository state; the command verifies that YAZELIX_VERSION matches the tag it creates; the command has automated tests covering the happy path and failure cases.

title: Include Nushell version in the pin/update workflow
description:
Problem:
The current pin/update flow tracks Yazelix and devenv version movement, but it does not treat the host/bootstrap Nushell version as an explicit pinned surface. Recent installer breakage on Nushell 0.108 showed that Nushell version drift matters during bootstrap and should be part of the maintained update story.

Design:
Define Nushell version as an explicit pinned tool surface in the same workflow that updates maintained pins, and extend the maintainer-facing update automation so `yzx dev update` (or the relevant subcommand in that surface) can refresh the Nushell pin and any corresponding lock/config references intentionally. The workflow should make it obvious when Yazelix runtime behavior depends on a host/bootstrap Nu floor versus the managed environment Nu version.

Acceptance Criteria:
- The maintained pin/update workflow explicitly includes Nushell version handling.
- `yzx dev update` (or the relevant maintainer update path) can update the Nushell pin without manual scattered edits.
- The implementation keeps Home Manager/default config/runtime pin metadata coherent where applicable.
- The command/docs make clear which Nushell version is bootstrap-sensitive versus runtime-managed.
- The workflow fails clearly if the Nushell pin cannot be updated consistently.

title: Move managed Zellij config into user_configs/zellij/config.kdl
description: Make Yazelix-managed Zellij config self-contained under ~/.config/yazelix/user_configs/zellij/config.kdl. In managed mode, stop implicitly reading ~/.config/zellij/config.kdl as the default input surface. Instead, treat native Zellij config as an explicit import/migration source or escape hatch. Keep the generated merged runtime config in ~/.local/share/yazelix/configs/zellij/config.kdl. Update config loading, migration/doctor flows, docs, and verification so the user mental model matches Yazi and the broader user_configs architecture. Include strong behavior tests for managed-path loading, native-config migration/import, and generated runtime output.
close_reason: Completed

title: Add configurable Ghostty cursor trail glow levels
description: Add a first-class config option for the glow around Ghostty cursor trails and related cursor effects. Use 'glow' as the canonical term rather than 'intensity' or 'halo'. The setting should control the spatial aura around the cursor/trail, not the underlying trail color itself. A value of 'none' should keep the trail color/effect but remove the surrounding glow. Proposed levels: none, low, medium, high. Proposed default: medium, matching the current feel closely enough that existing visuals still feel like Yazelix by default.
design: Treat this as a Ghostty shader/config contract first, not a vague visual-preference knob. The setting should only control the outer glow or aura around the cursor/trail, while preserving the underlying trail color/effect. Use 'glow' as the user-facing term. Start with a small discrete enum: none, low, medium, high. The current visual behavior should map roughly to medium so existing Yazelix visuals do not suddenly become more aggressive by default. The implementation should work across the supported Ghostty trail and mode effects, or clearly document any effect families that cannot share the same glow semantics without awkward one-off hacks.
acceptance_criteria: Yazelix exposes a documented Ghostty cursor-trail glow setting with values none, low, medium, high. Setting glow to none keeps the cursor/trail color effect while removing the surrounding spatial glow. Medium is the default and should preserve the current feel closely enough that the default user experience does not regress unexpectedly. Add focused validation for config parsing and generated Ghostty config or shader selection, and document which Ghostty effect families honor the glow setting if any exceptions remain.
close_reason: Completed

title: Fix pane-orchestrator permission limbo after plugin reload
description: Document and fix the failure mode where changing the pane-orchestrator plugin can trigger Zellij's permission popup inside an existing Yazelix window in a broken state that cannot be accepted. After that partial reload, launching Yazelix again can land in a blank-screen limbo because the session/plugin state is inconsistent.
design: Treat this as a plugin-lifecycle and recovery bug, not just a one-off UX glitch. Verify exactly when the permission dialog appears during pane-orchestrator reloads, whether the trigger is plugin hash changes, permission re-requests, stale plugin instances, or Zellij reload semantics, and why the popup becomes unusable inside the managed Yazelix window. The fix should prioritize clean recovery: avoid leaving the session in a blank-screen limbo, fail fast with a recoverable path when permissions are not granted, and make plugin reload/update flows deterministic instead of depending on manual session luck. If the root cause is a Zellij limitation, document the exact safe reload workflow and add guardrails in Yazelix rather than pretending the reload is seamless.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear reproduction path or documented trigger for the permission-limbo state, Yazelix no longer gets stuck in a blank-screen session after the pane-orchestrator plugin reload/update path fails, and there is an explicit recovery path for maintainers/users when plugin permissions are missing or stuck. Add focused validation for the detected failure mode, plus logs or diagnostics that show whether the plugin is loaded, permissioned, and responding after reload attempts.
close_reason: Handled by the restart/recovery workflow and current plugin update guidance; the permission-limbo issue is no longer actionable as a separate task.

title: Rust rewrite with spec-driven feature development
description: Create a long-term, explicitly post-v14 plan for moving the parts of Yazelix that most need stronger typing, safer state handling, and better performance toward a Rust core, while keeping spec-driven feature development as the behavior source of truth. The main goal is not rewriting for aesthetics; it is getting a more explicit, testable, and maintainable system with clear behavior contracts and a cleaner boundary between deterministic core logic and shell/Nix glue.
design: Treat this as a post-v14 architecture-and-process initiative, not just a language swap. The rewrite should define what belongs in a Rust core, what remains Nix/devenv-driven, and how feature behavior becomes spec-driven by default. Every user-visible feature should have a Markdown spec and tests that map to the spec's acceptance cases. The sequence should start with deterministic, reusable logic (config/migration/state and contract checking), not interactive shell glue. A key decision inside this epic is repository structure and interop: explicitly evaluate whether the rewrite should happen inside the current Yazelix repo or start in a new repo, and how Rust code should integrate incrementally with existing Nushell entrypoints before committing to deeper migration.
acceptance_criteria: There is a concrete post-v14 plan for a Rust-based Yazelix core, a defined spec template/workflow for feature development, and a documented decision on repository/interop strategy. The plan identifies the first migration slices, the surfaces that should remain Nushell or Nix, the parity strategy for each slice, and the support boundary between Rust, Nix/devenv, and any remaining shell glue. The rewrite can then start incrementally instead of as a vague big-bang idea.
notes:
Recommended sequencing after the 2026-04-05 architecture review:
- Do the seam and ownership clarifiers before any serious rewrite work: `yazelix-ewjz.1`, `yazelix-m5rk.1`, `yazelix-0hm3`, and `yazelix-uwz5.1`.
- Keep the first real Rust slice as the deterministic config core (`yazelix-kt5.2`), not the launch/bootstrap path.
- Do not treat Zellij generator pain or workspace/sidebar correctness bugs by themselves as evidence for a broad CLI rewrite; those areas still need product-boundary clarification first.

title: Define the Rust migration matrix and first-slice order after boundaries are proven
description: Produce an explicit post-v14 Rust migration matrix for the current Nushell codebase. Classify the major surfaces by Rust fitness, parity risk, expected payoff, and whether they should stay in Nushell, move behind a Rust shim, or move fully into a Rust core. Ground the matrix in the actual hot modules carrying product complexity today: config parsing/migrations/state, runtime contract checking, doctor/preflight logic, environment bootstrap, launch/bootstrap state, config generation, and the mixed yzx command surface.
design: This is the gate between 'Rust is safer/faster' sentiment and an implementation sequence that actually improves the repo. Build directly on the current architecture work: backend contract, runtime dependency/preflight contract, shared checker work, backend adapter extraction, and the command-surface audit. The output should prefer delete-first simplification before translation. Explicitly identify what should remain Nushell/Nix glue even in a Rust-heavier future, such as maintainer validators, shell hook generation, and flake/devenv wiring unless there is a stronger reason to move them.
acceptance_criteria: There is a written Rust migration matrix covering the main Nushell surfaces with a recommended keep/bridge/rewrite decision for each. The matrix identifies the first implementation slice, the slices that should explicitly wait, and the slices that should likely remain Nushell or Nix. The recommendation is grounded in current code hot spots and the active backend/runtime contract work rather than generic preference for Rust.
notes:
Current likely first-wave candidates from the codebase: config_parser.nu, config_contract.nu, config_schema.nu, config_migrations.nu, config_state.nu. Current likely later candidates: doctor.nu shared contract logic, then environment_bootstrap.nu and launch_state.nu after the backend seam is explicit. Current likely keep-in-Nushell surfaces: maintainer validators, shell hook generation, flake/devenv wiring, and most text-heavy config generators unless the matrix finds stronger reasons.

Sequencing note after the 2026-04-05 architecture review:
- Use `yazelix-ewjz.1` as a pre-matrix ownership input so the keep/bridge/rewrite decisions are grounded in an explicit language/runtime ownership map rather than sentiment.
- Keep `yazelix-0hm3` in view so unresolved workspace/sidebar ownership does not get misclassified as a generic rewrite target.

title: Define the Rust/Nushell bridge contract and incremental crate layout
description: Define how a Rust core will be integrated incrementally into the current Nushell-driven repo without forcing a big-bang rewrite. Cover the helper-binary versus library boundary, JSON/stdout-stderr contracts, error-classification handoff, repo/workspace crate layout, build/distribution implications, and how Nushell command surfaces should call into Rust while preserving current UX.
design: This is the missing bridge between planning a Rust rewrite and actually shipping one. The repo already contains Rust Zellij plugins, but those wasm crates do not answer how a future Rust core should coexist with Nushell commands, Nix packaging, and generated-config flows. Prefer a thin typed Rust helper/binary model first, with stable machine-readable output consumed by Nushell shims. Do not jump straight to replacing the whole CLI with clap. This is post-v14 work and should follow the migration-matrix bead.
acceptance_criteria: There is a concrete recommendation for the incremental Rust integration strategy: where Rust code lives, how Nushell invokes it, what machine-readable contract is used, how errors/logging are surfaced, and how the build/distribution story stays coherent with the current repo. The result is specific enough that later Rust slices can share one integration pattern instead of inventing their own ad hoc shims.

title: Port config contract, parsing, and migration logic behind a Rust shim
description: Plan and later implement a Rust-backed core for the deterministic config logic that currently lives across config_parser.nu, config_contract.nu, config_schema.nu, config_migrations.nu, config_state.nu, and related helpers. The goal is not to rewrite every config-facing command immediately, but to move the most structured, stateful, failure-prone logic into a safer typed core while keeping a thin Nushell compatibility layer during migration.
design: Treat this as the best first real Rust slice once the migration matrix and Rust/Nushell bridge strategy are ready, but only after v14 is shipped. This logic is highly structured, heavily reused, and benefits from strong types, deterministic serialization, and richer tests. Keep the UX and command surface stable by exposing the Rust logic through a narrow helper binary or shim first. Do not mix this with launch orchestration or package-backend work in the same slice.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear migration plan for moving the config contract/parsing/migration/state core behind a Rust shim without breaking the current command surface. The slice defines parity fixtures, migration-rule tests, launch-profile hash fixtures, and which Nushell modules become thin wrappers versus being deleted. The plan is concrete enough to implement incrementally rather than as a big-bang rewrite.
notes:
Primary target modules: config_parser.nu, config_contract.nu, config_schema.nu, config_migrations.nu, config_state.nu, and any tightly coupled helpers they require.

Sequencing note after the 2026-04-05 architecture review:
- Prefer landing `yazelix-uwz5.1` first so the Rust config core inherits a cleaner contract-owned template/default/parity story instead of freezing today's remaining duplication into a Rust shim.

title: Port runtime dependency checking and doctor reasoning into a Rust core
description: Once the runtime dependency contract and shared checker are defined in Nushell, evaluate and stage a Rust-backed implementation for the dependency/preflight reasoning that launch and doctor share. Focus on the classification engine and structured results, not on rendering every doctor message in Rust on day one.
design: This should follow the contract work, not replace it, and it is explicitly post-v14. First prove the dependency model and user-visible semantics in the current codebase. Then move the checker core into Rust if it materially simplifies correctness, structured diagnostics, and future Core-edition support. Keep doctor/launch presentation in Nushell initially if that reduces churn.
acceptance_criteria: There is a concrete plan for moving the shared runtime contract checker into Rust, with a clear boundary between structured result generation and user-facing rendering. The plan identifies parity cases, launch-time performance constraints, expected machine-readable output, and how doctor will consume the same results without reintroducing duplicate logic.
notes: Primary target logic lives around doctor.nu and the future shared runtime checker introduced by yazelix-hv2m. This slice should not start until j4qv and hv2m have clarified the contract in the current codebase.

title: Port launch/bootstrap state and backend adapter logic into Rust after the seam is extracted
description: After the backend adapter seam is clearer, evaluate moving the launch/bootstrap state machine and backend-calling logic out of environment_bootstrap.nu, launch_state.nu, and related yzx env/run/launch/refresh helpers into a Rust core. The target is safer orchestration and clearer state transitions, not a generic backend framework.
design: Do not start here. This is a later post-v14 slice because it mixes process spawning, environment shaping, state reuse, and user-facing behavior. Only move it once the backend contract and command-surface audit have made the boundary explicit and after the earlier Rust slices have proven the integration strategy. A likely good outcome is a Rust core that owns the state machine and structured actions while Nushell stays as a thin CLI/host integration layer.
acceptance_criteria: There is a concrete migration plan for the launch/bootstrap/backend adapter path that respects the extracted backend seam and does not re-entangle product semantics. The plan identifies which Nushell modules become shims or are removed, how parity will be verified for env/run/refresh/launch behavior, and why this slice is worth doing after the earlier ones.
notes:
Primary target modules: environment_bootstrap.nu, launch_state.nu, yzx/env.nu, yzx/run.nu, yzx/launch.nu, and any helper code that still encodes backend-specific state transitions.

Sequencing note after the 2026-04-05 architecture review:
- Treat `yazelix-m5rk.1` as the concrete precondition that makes the runtime state machine explicit before this slice moves into Rust.

title: Decide which config generators should stay Nushell versus move to Rust
description: Evaluate the major generated-config surfaces such as yazi_config_merger.nu, zellij_config_merger.nu, terminal_configs.nu, and related template helpers to decide whether they are good Rust targets or should stay in Nushell. The goal is to avoid rewriting text-heavy, low-risk template glue just because Rust is available, while still identifying generators that would genuinely benefit from stronger modeling.
design: Use the migration matrix to separate structured generator logic from simple template stitching. This is explicitly post-v14 work. Favor keeping straightforward text/template assembly in Nushell unless there is repeated correctness pain, complex state reconciliation, or a clear typed model that would become substantially safer in Rust. Do not let this bead become a license to port all generators indiscriminately.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear keep-versus-port recommendation for the main config generators, with concrete reasons tied to defect history, complexity, and expected payoff. The output identifies any generator slice worth porting and explicitly names the ones that should remain Nushell.
notes:
Likely keep-in-Nushell candidates today: zellij_config_merger.nu, yazi_config_merger.nu, and much of terminal_configs.nu unless a narrower structured core emerges that is worth extracting.

Sequencing note after the 2026-04-05 architecture review:
- `yazelix-jheg` is the likely non-Rust improvement path for `zellij_config_merger.nu`; if that typed render-pipeline work lands first, reevaluate whether any narrower structured subcore is still worth porting instead of assuming the whole generator should move.

title: Investigate how to make session resurrection excellent
description: Investigate what a genuinely strong resurrection story should look like for Yazelix sessions, including Zellij session restore, workspace cwd/state recovery, and any plugin-managed pane context that users would expect to survive restarts.
design: Do not reduce resurrection to merely reopening a Zellij session. Identify what users actually mean by a great restore experience: tabs, cwd, managed panes, sidebar/file-tree state, popup state, editor context, and whether resurrection should be explicit or automatic. Then determine which pieces are already available from Zellij, which belong in Yazelix, and which are too fragile to promise.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear target definition for great resurrection in Yazelix, with supported versus unsupported restored state called out explicitly. The bead leaves behind narrower follow-up beads for the gaps that matter instead of keeping resurrection as a vague aspiration.

title: evaluate splitting some yazelix builting zellij plugins to standalone plugins
description: Imported GitHub issue #485.
close_reason: Superseded by child issues yazelix-l968.1 / #503 and yazelix-l968.2 / #504

title: Extract `zellij_popup_runner` into a standalone managed popup plugin
description: Internal-first roadmap for eventually extracting the managed popup flow into a standalone Zellij plugin. Today the popup command and geometry are already config-driven inside Yazelix, but popup identity and selector assumptions are still hardwired to Yazelix names and wrapper flow.
design: Grounded in current code: `yzx popup` already owns argv command resolution, workspace-root cwd selection, and popup geometry through config parsing and Home Manager parity. The remaining coupling lives in the popup identity contract: `yzx_popup`, `yzx_popup_program.nu`, and the popup-runner selector logic. Do not jump straight to a standalone plugin. First make popup identity explicit and configurable inside Yazelix, formalize the adapter contract around argv/cwd/geometry, and harden the managed single-instance tests. Extraction only makes sense if those steps leave behind a clean generic contract with little or no Yazelix-specific shell glue.
acceptance_criteria: Yazelix first has an explicit internal popup contract covering managed popup identity, argv command execution, cwd policy, and geometry. Popup identity is no longer implicitly tied to hardcoded wrapper names alone. Regression coverage defends the single-instance managed popup behavior. After those steps, there is enough evidence to decide whether standalone extraction is justified.

title: Popup: make managed popup identity explicit and configurable inside Yazelix
description: Replace the remaining hardcoded managed popup identity assumptions with an explicit internal contract so popup selection is not implicitly coupled only to `yzx_popup` and `yzx_popup_program.nu`.
design: Today the popup identity contract is split across `yzx popup`, `yzx_popup_program.nu`, and the popup-runner selector. Make that identity explicit: pane title, wrapper marker, and any downstream selector inputs should be treated as a contract rather than incidental matching. Keep this internal to Yazelix first.
acceptance_criteria: Managed popup identity is represented explicitly in code rather than only as scattered hardcoded string assumptions. The popup-runner selector and wrapper flow agree on one clear identity contract. Existing popup behavior still works.

title: Popup: formalize the argv/cwd/geometry adapter contract
description: Turn the existing popup command, cwd, and geometry behavior into an explicit internal adapter contract that downstream extraction could reuse later.
design: The code already resolves popup argv, workspace-root cwd, and popup width/height through config parsing and Home Manager parity. Make that shape explicit as the internal adapter contract before any extraction discussion. Prefer documenting and defending the existing contract over inventing broader popup abstractions.
acceptance_criteria: The popup argv command, cwd policy, and geometry are treated as an explicit internal contract. The current config path and Home Manager path remain in sync. The behavior is documented or defended clearly enough that later extraction would not need to rediscover the contract.

title: Popup: harden managed single-instance regression coverage
description: Add or strengthen focused tests around the managed popup contract so later refactors or extraction attempts do not regress single-instance behavior.
design: Keep this focused on the contracts that matter: one managed popup instance, configured argv command, workspace-root cwd preference, and configured geometry. Avoid generic command-discovery tests.
acceptance_criteria: Focused regression coverage defends the managed popup identity and single-instance behavior, the popup argv command contract, the cwd policy, and popup geometry behavior.

title: Extract a standalone sidebar plugin with sidebar-aware motions
description: Internal-first roadmap for eventually extracting a generic sidebar plugin with sidebar-aware motions. The reusable motion logic is already partially isolated, but the sidebar launcher and generated layouts are still tightly coupled to Yazelix-managed Yazi behavior.
design: Grounded in current code: sidebar width is already config-driven and the closed-sidebar-aware motion logic already lives in reusable Rust contracts. The main blocker is launcher and layout coupling: generated layouts still hardwire `launch_sidebar_yazi.nu` and a managed `sidebar` pane running Yazi. Do not jump straight to a standalone plugin. First make the sidebar launcher configurable inside Yazelix, isolate the reusable sidebar and motion contract from Yazelix-specific Yazi/editor assumptions, and keep plugin commands as the stable contract while treating keybindings as docs/examples only. Extraction only makes sense if those steps leave a generic sidebar contract behind.
acceptance_criteria: Yazelix first has an explicit internal sidebar contract covering configurable sidebar program, sidebar width, toggle/focus commands, and horizontal motions that ignore a closed sidebar. The launcher and layouts are no longer inherently tied to Yazi-only behavior. After those steps, there is enough evidence to decide whether standalone extraction is justified.
notes: Terminology guardrail: keep `sidebar` as the generic side-surface term in architecture and plugin-contract work. Use `file tree` or `Yazi file tree` only when referring specifically to the current Yazi-backed surface in user-facing wording. This matters because future side surfaces may include agents or other tab-local tools, and the sidebar abstraction should stay broader than the current file-tree implementation.

title: Sidebar: make the sidebar launcher configurable inside Yazelix
description: Remove the assumption that the managed sidebar is always launched through `launch_sidebar_yazi.nu` so the sidebar surface can become generic before any extraction attempt.
design: The current layouts and fragments still hardwire a Yazi launcher script for the sidebar. Generalize this inside Yazelix first: the launcher command should become an explicit config/generation concern rather than a fixed Yazi path baked into every layout.
acceptance_criteria: Generated layouts no longer inherently depend on `launch_sidebar_yazi.nu` as the only sidebar launcher path. The sidebar launch surface is configurable inside Yazelix while preserving current behavior by default.

title: Sidebar: isolate the reusable motion and visibility contract
description: Preserve and sharpen the reusable Rust-side sidebar contract so closed-sidebar-aware motions and visibility toggles can stand on their own apart from Yazelix launcher assumptions.
design: The left/right motion and sidebar visibility logic is already partly isolated in pure Rust contracts. Strengthen that boundary and keep it generic. The reusable contract should not depend on Yazi-specific launcher details, and it should minimize editor-specific assumptions where possible.
acceptance_criteria: The reusable sidebar and motion contract is clearly isolated and defended. Horizontal motions ignore the sidebar when closed and treat it as a valid focus target when open. The contract is not entangled with Yazi-only launcher details.

title: Sidebar: separate plugin commands from keybinding policy
description: Treat sidebar commands such as toggle and focus as the stable command surface and keep keybindings as docs/examples rather than plugin semantics.
design: The plugin command surface already wants to be the stable contract, while keybindings belong to generated config and user policy. Make that separation explicit before any extraction attempt so the later standalone story is about commands, not about copying Yazelix default keymaps.
acceptance_criteria: Sidebar command surfaces such as toggle and focus are documented or treated as the stable contract. Keybindings remain configurable policy in Zellij config, with examples rather than hard requirements.

title: Tighten runtime/launch ownership boundaries after the recent state-ownership fixes
description: Carry the post-hardening runtime/launch architecture tightening lane after the recent fixes to runtime identity, launch-state ownership, desktop launch, refresh/build profile recording, and shell-vs-session profile resolution. The goal is not a rewrite. It is to align specs and code around a cleaner ownership model: dynamic user intent, deterministic runtime code, materialized/generated state, and explicit live session activation state, with narrower helpers and less split-brain between maintainer shells, runtime build artifacts, and recorded launch state.
design: Treat this as a delete-first architecture tightening pass, not a new abstraction project. Keep the current direction: split roots, deterministic runtime code, recorded launch state, and installed-runtime support. Tighten the remaining leaks by making runtime resolution fail fast, separating runtime-project materialization from read-only lookup, decomposing smart profile resolution into explicit layers, clarifying which flows own persistent state writes, and retiring generic root aliases where explicit helpers already exist.
acceptance_criteria: The follow-on child beads land a cleaner runtime/launch model without rewriting the product architecture. The specs name live session activation state explicitly, runtime-root resolution no longer falls back to config-root semantics, runtime-project materialization is not hidden behind read-style helpers, launch/profile resolution uses narrower explicit layers, persistent state writes have clearer ownership, and generic get_yazelix_dir or YAZELIX_DIR usage is reduced to intentional compatibility surfaces.
notes: Deeper review conclusion: the current runtime/launch architecture is directionally sound and does not need a rewrite, but it still needs tightening around runtime root resolution, runtime-project materialization, launch-profile resolution, persistent state ownership, and generic root aliases. The most important finding is that the docs should treat live session activation state as distinct from both materialized/generated state and deterministic runtime code. Follow-on work is now split into child beads le0s.1 through le0s.6 rather than leaving those concerns buried inside one review task.

title: Define live session activation state as a first-class runtime layer
description: Extend the current runtime/launch specs beyond the three-layer model of dynamic user intent, deterministic runtime code, and materialized/generated state by naming live session activation state explicitly. Clarify how DEVENV_PROFILE, PATH activation, IN_YAZELIX_SHELL, YAZELIX_TERMINAL, and Zellij session markers relate to launch_state.json and the recorded built profile, so stale maintainer shells and live launched Yazelix sessions stop looking like the same thing in the architecture docs and helper contracts.
design: This is the spec-first slice. Extend the runtime/launch contracts and architecture map so they distinguish four layers instead of collapsing the last two: dynamic user intent, deterministic runtime code, materialized/generated state, and live session activation state. Be explicit that launch_state.json and rebuild_hash live in materialized state, while DEVENV_PROFILE, PATH activation, IN_YAZELIX_SHELL, YAZELIX_TERMINAL, and Zellij markers belong to live session activation. Keep this bead focused on naming and ownership, not broad helper refactors.
acceptance_criteria: The runtime-facing specs and architecture notes name live session activation state explicitly and distinguish it from both materialized/generated state and deterministic runtime code. The docs explain why a stale maintainer shell can coexist with a correct launch_state.json, and they clarify which session markers are activation-only rather than persisted runtime truth. The resulting terminology gives later helper refactors a cleaner contract to target instead of keeping activation state implicit.
close_reason: Completed

title: Fail fast when runtime root resolution cannot find a valid runtime
description: Remove the hidden fallback in get_yazelix_runtime_dir that currently returns the config root when no valid runtime root can be resolved. That fallback keeps config and runtime concerns conflated and violates the runtime-root contract. Replace it with fail-fast runtime resolution, harden the affected callers, and make startup/doctor/launch errors explain that the runtime root is missing or invalid instead of silently treating ~/.config/yazelix as runtime code.
design: Constrain this bead to runtime-root resolution only. Remove the config-root escape hatch from get_yazelix_runtime_dir, preserve explicit YAZELIX_RUNTIME_DIR or legacy YAZELIX_DIR only as runtime aliases, and harden callers that were accidentally surviving on the fallback. Error handling should say that the runtime root is missing or invalid; it should not silently reinterpret ~/.config/yazelix as shipped runtime code. Do not mix this bead with generic alias cleanup beyond what is strictly required for fail-fast behavior.
acceptance_criteria: No canonical runtime-root helper returns the config root as a fallback when runtime resolution fails. Launch, doctor, startup, and other affected user-facing entrypoints fail fast with a clear runtime-root error instead of drifting into config-owned paths. Existing compatibility surfaces for explicit runtime aliases remain intentional and documented rather than being reintroduced as hidden fallback behavior.
close_reason: Completed

title: Split runtime-project lookup from runtime-project materialization
description: Replace ensure_yazelix_runtime_project_dir with a cleaner split between read-only lookup and explicit materialization. The current helper rewrites the symlinked build workspace even when callers only want to inspect build artifacts, which hides write-heavy behavior behind a read-style name. Introduce a read-only get_existing_runtime_project_dir surface and an explicit materialize_runtime_project_dir surface, then move read callers such as launch-state and bootstrap logic onto the non-materializing path unless they really need to regenerate the project tree.
design: This bead is about helper honesty. Split ensure_yazelix_runtime_project_dir into an explicit materializer and a read-only existing-project resolver so callers stop paying write-heavy workspace regeneration when they only want to inspect build artifacts. Classify current callers into writers versus readers first, then move read paths such as launch-state/profile inspection and bootstrap path discovery onto the non-materializing helper unless they truly need to regenerate the symlinked runtime project.
acceptance_criteria: The codebase has separate read-only and materializing runtime-project helpers with names that match their behavior. Read-side callers no longer recreate the runtime project tree as a hidden side effect. The remaining writers are explicit about why they materialize the project workspace, and the repo is easier to reason about because project-workspace writes are no longer hidden behind lookup-style helpers.
close_reason: Completed

title: Decompose resolve_built_profile into explicit profile-resolution layers
description: Break resolve_built_profile into narrower resolvers instead of keeping live-session detection, runtime-project artifacts, recorded launch state, and ambient env fallback inside one smart resolver. The real goal is not arbitrary caller-defined precedence for every call site. It is to separate the two actual policy bundles in the code today: current live-session profile resolution versus runtime-owned reusable profile resolution, so stale maintainer shells stop leaking into external launch and doctor logic.
design: Refactor resolve_built_profile by extracting explicit evidence resolvers for the distinct sources it currently mixes: live launched session activation, runtime-project build artifacts, recorded launch-state, and ambient shell fallback. Then add small policy wrappers for the two real caller families that already exist in code. Current-session flows such as start_yazelix_inner and refresh should prefer live launched-session activation first, then narrower runtime-owned evidence if needed. External/runtime-owned flows such as yzx launch, doctor, and editor launch outside the session should prefer runtime-project build artifacts first, then recorded launch-state, with ambient shell fallback kept only as a narrow compatibility edge. Keep resolve_profile_candidate and shell-script parsing as low-level utilities. It is acceptable to keep resolve_built_profile temporarily as a thin compatibility wrapper, but its precedence must simply delegate to one explicit policy helper rather than remaining the smart policy center.
acceptance_criteria: Profile resolution is decomposed into narrower helpers that each represent one evidence source or one explicit policy bundle. Core callers stop all relying on one monolithic smart resolver for materially different contexts. At minimum, launch and doctor use a runtime-owned reusable-profile policy, while start/refresh use a current-session policy, and editor launch context picks intentionally between them. If a compatibility wrapper remains, it is thin, documented, and no longer the place where most precedence logic hides.
