title: Update Yazelix to the next Zellij major release
description: Update Yazelix to the next Zellij major release and make the supported runtime, generated config, plugins, and documented behavior work cleanly on that release.
close_reason: Completed: upgraded Yazelix to Zellij 0.44, updated the Rust plugins to zellij-tile 0.44, rebuilt and synced the wasm artifacts, refreshed the maintained default-config snapshot, and fixed version reporting so saved status output reflects the current repo-shell runtime.

title: Define the v15 roadmap skeleton around Rust core, cleaner architecture, profiling, and website quality
description: Define a provisional post-v14 roadmap skeleton for Yazelix. The point is not to promise a full v15 plan early, but to capture the main pillars that are already visible once v14 lands: selective Rust rewrites where they buy real safety and clarity, cleaner subsystem boundaries, top-to-bottom profiling to identify the true pain points before deeper rewrites, and a much stronger website/presentation layer.
design: Keep this explicitly post-v14 and intentionally skeletal. Reuse the real existing lanes instead of creating duplicate implementation work: the Rust rewrite epic, the backend/runtime contract work, the profiling bead, and the website issues. The useful output is a coherent public/internal framing for what v15 is likely to care about once v14 is done, without pretending the detailed sequencing is already settled.
acceptance_criteria: There is a durable v15 skeleton that is clearly marked as provisional and post-v14. It identifies the main pillars for that era, points at the existing Rust/architecture/profiling/website lanes that feed them, and is specific enough to support a public discussion without overpromising the detailed implementation order.

title: Evaluate a Nix-only, no-devenv, opinionated flake backend for Yazelix Core
description: Evaluate whether a future Yazelix Core edition could use a Nix-only, no-devenv backend centered on an opinionated flake that ships the default Yazelix stack as one fixed bundle. The question is not whether this should replace full Yazelix. The question is whether keeping Nix while dropping devenv yields a simpler, more honest Core backend by shipping the core default tool/runtime set directly: Zellij, Yazi, Helix, Nushell, Starship, Lazygit, relevant default terminal support, runtime assets, and launch/update entrypoints.
design: Treat this as a post-v14 evaluation bead and as a specific Core-backend candidate, not as a new commitment for main. Reuse the backend contract, runtime dependency contract, command-surface audit, and Core-boundary work first. Then evaluate whether a fixed flake-defined default-stack bundle plus app/shell outputs can support the Core surface cleanly without carrying devenv profile semantics. This is intentionally not a full replacement for the current flexible pack-driven model. The evaluation should explicitly compare this shape against the Pixi experiment lane and explain where a Nix-only Core bundle is simpler, more honest, or more constrained.
acceptance_criteria: There is a concrete recommendation on whether a Nix-only, no-devenv, opinionated flake that ships the default Yazelix stack is a good fit for Yazelix Core. The result identifies which current Yazelix guarantees it could preserve, which ones it would intentionally drop, what install/update/run surface it would expose, how it would treat the current default tool set, and whether it is a better Core candidate than a non-Nix backend experiment. The output is specific enough to guide either a later prototype or an explicit no-go decision.
notes: Reasoning for graph placement: this is downstream of the general backend contract, runtime dependency contract, command-surface audit, and Core-boundary decision. It is intentionally post-v14 and exploratory, and it should not compete with the current packaging/hardening lane. The current best framing is a fixed default-stack flake bundle for Core, not a flexible replacement for full pack-driven Yazelix.

title: Add Alt+<number> keybindings to yzx keys documentation
description: The yzx keys command should document the Alt+1 through Alt+9 keybindings that Yazelix provides for direct tab navigation. These keybindings already exist in the Zellij config (yazelix_overrides.kdl) but are not shown in the yzx keys output.

title: Experiment: evaluate a Pixi-backed Yazelix branch
description: Evaluate whether Yazelix could support a separate Pixi-backed experiment branch without turning main into a multi-backend runtime. Focus on a narrow prototype: Helix, Yazi, Zellij, Nushell, and core CLI flows such as yzx env, yzx run, and launch/bootstrap. The goal is to determine whether Pixi is credible for a lower-support branch, what major gaps remain compared with the current devenv/Nix model, and whether the branch should exist at all.
design: Treat this as an exploration task, not a commitment to multi-backend support in main. Keep the scope narrow: prototype a separate Pixi-backed branch that covers the core Yazelix stack plus the command slice that still matters after the backend-contract and command-surface work lands. The task should identify what maps cleanly from the current runtime model to Pixi, what becomes unsupported or degraded, and whether the resulting branch would be honest enough to maintain as a lower-support option. Avoid solving generic backend abstraction unless the prototype proves the branch is worth pursuing first.
acceptance_criteria: There is a concrete recommendation on whether Yazelix should maintain a Pixi-backed experiment branch. The result includes: a validated package-availability check for the core toolchain; a clear list of current devenv/Nix assumptions that would need replacement; a small prototype or spike proving the core launch/env/run flow is either viable or not; and a documented capability matrix showing which features would work, degrade, or remain unsupported. Leave behind enough notes and logs that the branch decision is reproducible instead of intuition-driven.

title: Home Manager clean-room refresh should materialize generated Zellij layouts before yzx enter
Split yzx menu from config/edit/open surfaces and thin the command palette
description: The current yzx menu module carries too many unrelated command families. It should be thinned so the command palette UI and popup behavior are separated from config/edit/open/reset/migrate implementations.
design: Keep yzx menu as the palette and popup wrapper only, but stop treating palette eligibility as a tiny hand-filtered subset. Prefer a small explicit command-category model that allows most user-facing yzx commands into the palette by default while excluding commands whose semantics are inherently pane-scoped, tab-scoped, or otherwise unsuitable for palette dispatch. Move config/edit/open/reset/migrate implementations out of menu.nu into dedicated leaf modules. Prefer one declarative metadata source for grouping, labels, and palette eligibility instead of mining help output and then layering ad hoc filters.
acceptance_criteria: 1. yzx menu remains the palette surface, but config/edit/open/reset/migrate implementations no longer live in nushell/scripts/yzx/menu.nu. 2. Palette eligibility is driven by explicit command categories or metadata rather than a fragile manual name filter. 3. Most user-facing yzx commands are eligible by default, while pane-scoped and tab-scoped commands are explicitly excluded or handled outside the palette contract. 4. Popup and normal menu flows keep working without regressing the lightweight dispatcher path. 5. Focused regressions defend the moved command surfaces or palette dispatch seam without weak help-output tests. 6. Net result is a meaningfully smaller and less mixed menu.nu.
close_reason: Split config/edit/open ownership out of menu.nu, added explicit palette categories and eligibility, and kept menu as a thinner palette wrapper.

title: Define yzx command categories and palette eligibility
design: Write a small spec that classifies the public yzx command surface into practical UX categories for the command palette. The goal is not to restate backend-coupling buckets; it is to define which commands are palette-eligible by default, which are explicitly excluded, and how categories drive grouping and labels. Prefer broad inclusion of user-facing commands, with explicit exclusions for pane-level and tab-level commands and any commands whose interaction model is not suitable for palette dispatch.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The spec defines concrete command categories for the public yzx surface with palette-eligibility rules. 2. The spec makes explicit that pane-scoped and tab-scoped commands are not normal palette items. 3. The spec explains how categories differ from backend-coupling buckets and how the two models relate. 4. The spec names the current command families or concrete commands that belong in each category. 5. The spec includes traceability back to yazelix-qrnq and validation through the normal spec validator.
close_reason: Specified yzx command categories and palette eligibility in docs/specs/yzx_command_palette_categories.md and validated specs.

title: Define the Yazelix Core edition boundary and support story
description: Define whether a no-package-management Yazelix edition should exist, what it would own, and what it would explicitly stop promising. Treat this as a product-boundary bead, not an implementation promise. The likely shape is a Yazelix Core edition that keeps the workspace UX layer, generated config ownership, and selected CLI flows while dropping environment/package provisioning and managed backend guarantees. The output should be explicit enough to say whether Core should be a branch, package, or later mode at all.
design: Reason from the current architecture and command inventory rather than from branding first. Use the backend contract and command-surface audit to determine whether a Core edition is actually coherent. Be explicit about what Core would keep, what it would drop, what failure modes would become user responsibility, and whether it should be a separate package, branch, or not pursued at all. The name can stay provisional until the product boundary is proven; the important part is the support story, not the label.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear recommendation on whether Yazelix Core should exist and, if so, what it would own and explicitly stop promising. The decision is grounded in the backend contract, runtime dependency contract, and command-surface classification rather than generic appetite for a lighter edition. The result is specific enough to guide later implementation or a deliberate no-go decision.

title: yzx reveal latency is too high
description: Track `yzx reveal` taking around three seconds to reveal the current file in the managed Yazi sidebar. This is a reveal-path performance bug, not a popup bug and not a correctness regression. The work should identify where the cost actually lives: target-path resolution, sidebar state discovery, Yazi `ya emit-to reveal`, pane focus/orchestrator work, or related coordination on the reveal path.
design: Treat this as a measurement-first bug. Reproduce the latency with a concrete reveal action and instrument the reveal path from `yzx reveal` through target resolution, sidebar lookup, Yazi emit, and focus/orchestrator work. Keep the scope on reveal latency only. Prefer deleting unnecessary work or reusing state over adding more coordination layers.
acceptance_criteria: 1. There is a reproducible measurement of reveal latency before and after the fix. 2. The main source of reveal-path cost is identified and documented. 3. `yzx reveal` becomes substantially faster in normal use without regressing correctness or focus behavior. 4. Add one focused regression or benchmark guard at the seam that actually caused the latency.
close_reason: Completed

title: Home Manager clean-room refresh should materialize generated Zellij layouts before yzx enter
description: On a clean-room Home Manager install, the ownership surfaces validate and `yzx refresh` successfully builds a fresh profile, but it still leaves ~/.local/share/yazelix/configs/zellij/layouts/yzx_side.kdl missing. As a result, `yzx enter --skip-refresh` fails immediately with `Missing Yazelix generated Zellij layout` even after a successful refresh. This leaves the first Home Manager startup path under-materialized and makes `yzx doctor` report a real startup blocker instead of a finished first-run state.

title: Audit and prune the default-lane test suite
close_reason: Completed

title: Audit and prune the full Yazelix test surface for signal
design: Treat this as an aggressive pruning program, not a test-preservation exercise. First inventory every test file by lane, contract, score, and likely verdict. Then audit the default lane test-by-test using the new strength rubric and remove or demote weak/default-inappropriate cases. After that, audit nondefault lanes file-by-file to decide whether each file still deserves to exist. Finish by applying deletions, demotions, and runner simplifications so the repo is smaller and the suite stays easier to reason about.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Every test file under nushell/scripts/dev/test_*.nu is inventoried with lane, rough contract, and a keep/demote/remove/rewrite verdict. 2. The default lane is reviewed test-by-test and at least one weak, redundant, or mis-laned test is removed, demoted, or rewritten if such a case exists. 3. Nondefault lanes are reviewed file-by-file and dead-weight files or tests are removed or narrowed where justified. 4. The resulting runner/validator surfaces remain coherent and all affected validation lanes pass. 5. The audit leaves behind a concise written inventory or report so future pruning starts from reality instead of memory.
close_reason: Completed

title: Audit install and desktop refresh flows against the installed runtime
description: Verify the real effects of the flake install and desktop install paths against the installed runtime. Audit which state they are expected to update, then confirm whether runtime/current, ~/.local/bin/yzx, generated configs, shell hooks, and the desktop entry are actually refreshed or left stale.
close_reason: Audit completed: install and desktop install now refresh the installed runtime coherently; stale desktop contract replaced by stable yzx launcher path.

title: yzx onboard
description: Guide first-time users through creating the supported Yazelix config set instead of making them hand-edit configuration from scratch. Keep yazelix.toml as the front door, but allow onboarding to emit optional sidecars such as yazelix_packs.toml when that is the supported architecture.
design: Treat yzx onboard as an interactive first-run config generator, not as a generic tutorial surface. The command should ask the user a focused set of high-value questions and then write a sensible configuration outcome based on the answers. The interaction model should feel like a lightweight terminal questionnaire: arrow-key navigation for single-choice prompts, and explicit multi-select prompts where space toggles choices and enter confirms. Keep the scope tight around the settings that matter most for getting productive quickly rather than exposing every config knob up front. Question areas should likely include terminal preference, shell choice, editor choice, whether the sidebar is enabled, whether sessions are persistent, and a small set of useful packs or defaults. The output should stay aligned with the supported config architecture rather than hardcoding everything into yazelix.toml forever; if pack config lives in yazelix_packs.toml, onboarding should generate that file instead of collapsing pack answers back into the main file.
acceptance_criteria: `yzx onboard` can guide a first-time user through creating a valid supported Yazelix configuration with a small number of high-signal prompts. Single-choice prompts support arrow-key selection, and multi-select prompts support toggling choices before confirmation. The generated output is valid, readable, and aligned with the same supported options as the normal config path, including optional sidecars where the architecture calls for them. Add behavior tests for prompt flow and generated-config output, including at least one multi-select case, one interrupted-or-aborted flow, one case that writes the main yazelix.toml only, and one case that writes a pack sidecar when pack answers require it.

title: Harden flake onboarding for host tool bootstrap
description:
Problem:
The flake install surface still assumes too much about host bootstrap tools. Recent failures showed that host Nushell version drift can break install/setup even when the packaged runtime is correct.

Design:
Track the remaining onboarding hardening work as a small subtree focused on bootstrap-tool assumptions, especially host Nushell dependence and explicit pinned-tool onboarding behavior.

Acceptance Criteria:
- The remaining host-tool assumptions in the flake onboarding path are explicit and tracked.
- Host Nushell dependence is addressed separately from broader pinned-tool update workflow.
- The onboarding story for pinned bootstrap tools is coherent.
close_reason: Completed

title: Make flake install/bootstrap independent of host Nushell version
description:
Problem:
The flake installer and bootstrap path still expose users to host Nushell compatibility issues. Recent install failures on Nushell 0.108 proved that Yazelix bootstrap is not yet sufficiently isolated from the user's ambient Nu version.

Design:
Audit and reduce every bootstrap-sensitive path that still depends on host Nushell parsing or runtime behavior, and ensure the installer uses the packaged Nushell/tooling path consistently enough that host Nu version drift no longer breaks first-run onboarding.

Acceptance Criteria:
- The flake install/bootstrap path no longer fails because of host Nushell version drift in the supported onboarding flow.
- Bootstrap-sensitive scripts either avoid host Nu entirely or are explicitly compatible with the supported host floor.
- The maintained smoke/install lane covers the intended bootstrap contract.
close_reason: Completed

title: Clarify pinned bootstrap-tool onboarding responsibilities
description:
Problem:
Yazelix now pins and manages some bootstrap-adjacent tools, but the onboarding story is still fuzzy about what the installer should install directly versus what it should merely assume or validate.

Design:
Define the onboarding contract for pinned bootstrap tools such as devenv, and clarify what is in scope for the installer versus what must already exist on the host.

Acceptance Criteria:
- The onboarding contract clearly states which bootstrap tools Yazelix installs or refreshes itself.
- The docs/specs distinguish installer-managed bootstrap tools from host prerequisites.
- The result does not blur into unrelated release/update automation work.
close_reason: Completed

title: Investigate remaining duplicate shellhook contexts on cold profiled startup
description: The Yazelix-owned refreshed startup path no longer re-enters `devenv shell` after rebuild, but a truly cold `yzx dev profile --cold --clear-cache` run still shows two shellhook PID contexts. Warm startup does not show this duplication. This follow-up tracks the remaining deeper investigation separately from the completed refreshed-startup cleanup.
design: Treat this as a narrower cold-path investigation, not a redo of the refreshed startup ownership cleanup. The remaining duplicate shellhook contexts survive after the rebuilt-profile activation simplification, so first determine whether they come from upstream `devenv build shell` behavior, a profiling harness interaction, or another cold-only shell activation boundary. Prefer an explicit explanation and the smallest correct fix over more startup-path branching.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The remaining duplicate shellhook contexts on truly cold profiled startup have a concrete root-cause explanation. 2. If Yazelix owns the duplication, remove or narrow it without regressing visible rebuild behavior or startup correctness. 3. If the duplication is upstream or intentionally unavoidable, document that contract clearly in the bead and keep the startup profile output interpretable. 4. Warm startup remains unchanged and the existing refreshed-startup regression continues to pass.
notes: Root cause was not an unavoidable duplicate shellhook entry. The refreshed-startup path was resolving runtime-owned launch state to the .devenv/gc/shell script path itself instead of extracting the embedded DEVENV_PROFILE. Because direct activation was using a devenv-shell script path rather than the real devenv-profile, cold profiling and refreshed startup behavior became misleading. Fix: resolve shell-script candidates through readlink first, extract the embedded profile path robustly, and defend it with a workspace regression that uses a real .devenv/gc/shell fixture. After reinstalling the runtime, cold yzx dev profile --cold --clear-cache no longer records shellhook contexts at all; it goes straight from bootstrap.devenv.build_shell into inner startup. Warm profiling remains clean.
close_reason: Fixed runtime-owned profile resolution; cold startup profiling no longer shows duplicate shellhook contexts

title: Organize the repository root and clarify root-level ownership
description: Audit the repository root and make root-level ownership legible. The goal is not cosmetic churn; it is to decide which files truly belong at the repo root, which should move under docs/ or a more specific subsystem directory, and which root artifacts need explicit documentation because they are part of the product or maintainer contract.
design: Treat the repo root as a high-signal surface. Root-level files should exist only when they are one of: repository entrypoints, packaging/build metadata, maintainer workflow files, user-facing discovery docs, or source-of-truth config/templates that genuinely belong at the top level. Everything else should either move under a more specific directory or be documented as intentionally root-level. Prefer delete-first reasoning over aesthetic reshuffling: remove stale or redundant root artifacts, group related files when a clearer home exists, and avoid broad moves that break discoverability without a real ownership win.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Inventory the current root-level files and classify them into intentional root ownership, should-move, or should-delete/document categories. 2. At least one real root-level ambiguity, redundancy, or stale artifact is either removed or re-homed. 3. The remaining root-level surfaces have a clearer ownership story consistent with current Yazelix architecture and maintainer workflow. 4. Any user-facing or maintainer-facing moves are reflected in docs or workflow references. 5. Leave behind a short summary of what root artifacts are intentionally kept and why.
notes:
Completed with a conservative first slice focused on root discoverability rather than broad reshuffling.

Removed:
- deleted the unreferenced docs_explore/ root directory and its stale exploratory Helix keybinding reference because current maintained guidance already lives under docs/keybindings.md and docs/helix_keybindings.md.

Intentionally kept at root:
- entrypoint/user-facing docs: README.md, CHANGELOG.md, AGENTS.md, LICENSE, CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md
- build and packaging entrypoints: flake.nix, devenv.nix, devenv.yaml, flake.lock, devenv.lock, locked_devenv_package.nix, mk_runtime_tree.nix, mk_yazelix_package.nix, yazelix_package.nix, yazelix_runtime_package.nix
- maintainer workflow/config: .pre-commit-config.yaml, .taplo.toml, .nu-lint.toml, .github/, .beads/
- source-of-truth runtime templates: yazelix_default.toml, yazelix_packs_default.toml

Documentation follow-up:
- added a short root-ownership rule to docs/contributing.md
- repaired nearby stale docs/specs path references so maintainer workflow docs no longer point at a local config path
close_reason: Completed a conservative root-ownership cleanup slice: removed the stale docs_explore/ root subtree and documented what intentionally belongs at the repo root.

title: Extract flake installer shell block into a maintained script or helper
description:
The `install` app in `flake.nix` still embeds a long shell script inline. That block now carries most of the flake-specific install behavior: runtime symlink setup, seeded config creation, shell hook refresh, generated config rebuilds, and user-facing install messaging.

It works, but it is the largest remaining behavior-heavy chunk in the flake and is harder to review, test, and evolve than the surrounding flake output wiring.
design:
Keep the current install contract and app surface, but move the behavior-heavy shell block out of `flake.nix` into one maintained checked-in script or a small helper derivation that generates the script. Prefer a real script file if possible so install behavior can be reviewed and evolved without editing large inline shell strings.

The goal is not to redesign installation. It is to separate flake output plumbing from installer behavior and make future install-path changes less brittle.
acceptance_criteria: 1. `flake.nix` no longer contains the long inline installer shell block. 2. The install behavior remains the same from the user perspective: `nix run .#install` still installs the runtime, seeds config files, refreshes shell hooks, rebuilds generated configs, and prints the same next-step guidance. 3. Flake install smoke validation still passes after the extraction. 4. The extraction does not reintroduce runtime-path staleness or split install logic across multiple ad hoc shell snippets.
close_reason: Completed: moved the flake installer shell logic into a maintained checked-in script template and kept the nix run install contract unchanged.

title: Evaluate integrating sahaj-b/ghostty-cursor-shaders into Yazelix-managed cursor effects
description: Evaluate adding curated support for the MIT-licensed ghostty-cursor-shaders repo to Yazelix so Ghostty cursor effects remain fully Yazelix-managed instead of requiring users to clone and wire shader files manually. The goal is to extend the current Ghostty cursor-trail system with upstream trail and pulse/boom patterns while preserving Yazelix generation, docs, defaults, and random selection.
design: Current state: Yazelix already generates Ghostty shader config, vendors local shader files under configs/terminal_emulators/ghostty/shaders, and defaults terminal.cursor_trail to random. The upstream repo is a good fit because Ghostty supports multiple custom-shader lines and the repo is simple standalone GLSL under MIT. Important caveat: these upstream effects are not only color variants. They include trail shaders like cursor_warp, cursor_sweep, and cursor_tail, plus mode-change shaders like ripple_cursor, sonic_boom_cursor, rectangle_boom_cursor, and ripple_rectangle_cursor. That means this should be treated as a broader cursor-effects surface, not just more colors. Preferred direction: keep Yazelix as the builder and orchestrator, vendor or generate a curated subset of upstream shaders locally, and expose them through a structured selector that supports both existing color presets and new pattern effects. A robust design is to support an ordered list for Ghostty shader selection and composition, with backward-compatible string aliases for existing cursor_trail values. If random remains the default, define a curated random pool explicitly instead of sampling every shader, because pulse and boom effects are behaviorally different from trails and may need separate inclusion rules. Also account for Ghostty-specific caveats such as custom-shader-animation = always for some line-cursor effects, and avoid regressing Kitty fallback behavior that currently maps random to snow.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear implementation decision and scope for integrating ghostty-cursor-shaders into Yazelix. The chosen design keeps shader management inside Yazelix, documents the upstream source and license, and specifies whether the config surface stays terminal.cursor_trail, becomes truly list-capable, or is renamed to a broader cursor-effects concept. The plan identifies which upstream shaders are included, how random selection works, what Ghostty-only caveats must be emitted in generated config, and how existing cursor_trail users remain backward-compatible. Verification includes generating Ghostty config with a fixed preset, a composed or list-based preset if adopted, and the default random path.
close_reason: Implemented Ghostty cursor effect integration with vendored shaders, new Ghostty effect config, generated shader build, and collection/docs updates

title: Move bundled Yazi asset sync out of warm startup
description:
Bundled Yazi plugins, flavors, and the managed sidebar Starship config are currently refreshed during startup-managed Yazi generation. Those files are largely static for a given runtime, so paying that copy/sync cost on the common warm path is likely the wrong boundary.

This follow-up should evaluate moving bundled Yazi asset materialization to install/refresh/runtime-change ownership instead of launch-time ownership.
design:
Reason from first principles. The common warm path should only do work that is truly dynamic for that launch. Runtime-owned Yazi assets are mostly static for a given installed runtime, so they should not be recopied on every startup unless there is a strong correctness reason. Prefer a design that deletes launch-time bundled-asset sync instead of adding more cache metadata or broad invalidation logic.

Preserve fail-fast behavior and clear ownership boundaries between runtime-owned assets and user-owned Yazi config.
acceptance_criteria:
1. The chosen design makes explicit which Yazi files are runtime-owned static assets versus per-launch dynamic generated config.
2. Warm startup no longer recopies unchanged bundled Yazi assets on the common path.
3. Runtime updates, install, or explicit refresh still materialize the needed bundled Yazi assets correctly.
4. A focused regression defends the new ownership boundary without relying on broad cache metadata.

title: Audit rm usage for safety and destructive-command hygiene
description: Audit the codebase for every rm invocation and decide where deletion behavior needs stronger safety checks, clearer scoping, or better diagnostics.
design: Treat this as destructive-command hygiene, not as a mechanical search-and-replace pass. For each rm usage, verify what is being deleted, how paths are resolved, whether the target can escape the intended runtime boundary, and whether errors are visible. The goal is to catch genuinely risky deletion paths without adding unnecessary friction to safe, bounded cleanup.
acceptance_criteria: All rm usage in the repo has been reviewed and any risky or unclear deletion paths have a concrete follow-up fix or direct cleanup. The audit records which rm invocations are already acceptably bounded and which ones need stronger safeguards or diagnostics.
notes: Promote this safety audit. Current repo direction is pre-packaging hardening: reduce LOC, remove dead/redundant code, and audit destructive paths. rm-call hygiene is a high-priority cleanup pass because it can hide unsafe path handling behind routine maintenance code.

title: Replace Bash-heavy terminal wrappers in devenv.nix with Nushell-first helpers
description: Reduce remaining Bash-heavy terminal wrapper logic in devenv.nix only where moving it into shared Nushell helpers meaningfully shrinks duplication without obscuring terminal-specific launch behavior.
design: Treat this as a cleanup follow-up, not an abstraction contest. The goal is to centralize shared resolution logic, error messaging, and config-path handling in Nushell while leaving terminal-specific CLI flags explicit where they differ materially. Do not hide per-terminal launch contracts behind generic wrappers that become harder to reason about than the current code. Any refactor must preserve fail-fast startup, deterministic terminal config resolution, and the current Yazelix versus user mode semantics. Measure the change by whether the wrappers get simpler and the ownership boundary becomes easier to understand, not by whether every line of shell disappears.
acceptance_criteria: Shared wrapper logic that is truly common moves into Nushell, while terminal-specific launch behavior remains readable and correct. Add focused unit tests for the Nushell helpers that replace wrapper logic, especially config resolution, mode validation, and error paths. Add end-to-end wrapper smoke tests with detailed logs for at least Ghostty, WezTerm, Kitty, Alacritty, and Foot, showing the resolved config path, effective mode, and final terminal invocation shape. Verification must demonstrate that cleanup reduced duplication without changing supported behavior.

title: Adopt minimal prek config for fast maintainer checks
description: Follow-up to the maintainer-pack work: clean up stale prek references that still describe an unrelated tool, add a minimal pre-commit-compatible config at the repo root, and keep the hook set intentionally small and cheap: syntax validation plus README version validation. Document the maintainer usage path without turning prek into the main enforcement layer; CI remains authoritative.
close_reason: Cleaned stale prek references, added a minimal .pre-commit-config.yaml with syntax and README version checks, documented the maintainer usage path, and verified the config with prek run --all-files.

title: Add controlled terminal override layers instead of full config ownership modes
description: Replace the coarse terminal.config_mode ownership question with a cleaner terminal-extension contract. Yazelix should keep owning launch and integration-critical terminal behavior, while users get Yazelix-specific per-terminal override files for harmless customization such as theme, fonts, opacity, padding, and similar preferences. Start with ghostty, kitty, and alacritty. Avoid reading the normal terminal config by default. This should simplify the product boundary rather than adding a generic merge engine.
close_reason: Completed: shipped controlled terminal override layers for Ghostty, Kitty, and Alacritty, kept launch-critical behavior under Yazelix ownership, and documented the contract.

title: Implement Ghostty source policy for non-NixOS Linux
description: Apply the Ghostty package-source policy by removing the assumption that nixpkgs Ghostty is the safe default on non-NixOS Linux.
design: Turn the package-source policy into concrete behavior. Keep Homebrew/system Ghostty on macOS, keep nixpkgs Ghostty acceptable on NixOS, and change the non-NixOS Linux story so Yazelix no longer funnels users into nixpkgs Ghostty as the unqualified default. That may mean preferring another default terminal such as WezTerm on non-NixOS Linux, preferring a system Ghostty path when present, or explicitly demoting the Nix-managed Ghostty path behind an opt-in/caveat.
acceptance_criteria: Yazelix no longer treats nixpkgs Ghostty as an unqualified default on non-NixOS Linux. The resulting default and override behavior is explicit in code and docs, and users on non-NixOS Linux are not steered into a Ghostty path that predictably fails without extra OpenGL setup.

title: Investigate whether Yazelix can avoid Zellij plugin permission prompts safely
description: Investigate whether Yazelix can reduce or avoid the recurring Zellij plugin permission popup without undermining the safety model or relying on brittle local hacks.
design: Start from the principle that permission prompts exist for a reason. The task is not to bypass safety blindly; it is to determine whether Yazelix can ship a safer default permission story through stable plugin paths, predictable permission state, or better first-run setup. If Zellij fundamentally requires user approval, prefer an explicit documented flow over unsafe suppression tricks.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear decision on whether Yazelix can safely reduce or avoid Zellij plugin permission prompts. If yes, the bead identifies the legitimate mechanism. If not, it documents the best supported user flow and why forced suppression would be the wrong tradeoff.
close_reason: Superseded by the built-in Yazelix permission-recovery command; separate forced popup suppression is no longer needed as a tracked feature.

title: Temporary br repro
description: Disposable issue for local br FK repro.

title: Replace swap-layout step logic with Zellij 0.44 override-layout transitions
description:
Post-Zellij-0.44 follow-up: evaluate replacing Yazelix's current swap-layout stepping model for layout-family changes and sidebar open/close transitions with Zellij's newer `override-layout` capability.

Why this exists:
- the pane orchestrator currently hardcodes layout family names and drives transitions via `next_swap_layout()` / `previous_swap_layout()` plus timing delays
- Zellij 0.44 introduced `override-layout` and the built-in layout manager as first-class layout transition surfaces

Scope:
- map the current Yazelix layout families and sidebar open/closed variants onto an explicit override-layout model
- determine whether sidebar toggle, family cycling, or both can stop depending on swap-step timing
- preserve managed-pane focus behavior and workspace invariants across transitions
- reduce hardcoded layout-name coupling where possible

Success means Yazelix either adopts a cleaner override-layout-based transition path for these flows, or leaves behind a documented reason why the swap-layout model still must remain.
design: Treat this as the broader override-layout evaluation umbrella. Zellij 0.44 made override-layout more realistic, but the real product question is whether Yazelix can replace swap-layout stepping without losing managed-pane invariants. The live-trial notes matter: runtime-file IO inside the plugin sandbox was one blocker, and explicit pane identity may be the deeper blocker. Use this bead to hold the broad transition question, while narrower child beads capture the concrete contracts discovered along the way.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear keep or replace decision for swap-layout stepping versus override-layout transitions, backed by concrete evidence from trials. If override-layout remains the direction, the bead points to the narrower explicit-run and pane-ownership work needed to make it real. If not, it records why swap-layout remains necessary.
notes:
Live trial status on 2026-03-26:
- pointer-driven layout changes still work
- direct keybind actions `toggle_sidebar` and `previous_family`/`next_family` were failing even though layout detection succeeded
- root cause: the pane-orchestrator plugin could not read generated runtime layout/script files under `~/.local/share/yazelix/...` from inside the Zellij plugin sandbox
- implementation change: replace runtime file IO with embedded tracked layout templates and in-memory Yazelix placeholder expansion inside the plugin
- current state: rebuilt and synced locally; awaiting live retest after `yzx restart`

title: Move managed Zellij config into user_configs/zellij
description: Make Zellij follow the same ownership model as the rest of managed Yazelix config. In managed mode, the canonical user-editable Zellij input should live under ~/.config/yazelix/user_configs/zellij/ (starting with config.kdl), and Yazelix should generate the merged runtime config into ~/.local/share/yazelix/configs/zellij/. Do not keep ~/.config/zellij/config.kdl as the default managed input path; at most, treat it as an import/migration source or explicit escape hatch. This should optimize for system coherence, least surprise, self-contained flake story, and a single mental model: user edits user_configs/*, Yazelix renders runtime state into ~/.local/share/yazelix/. Include deterministic migration/import behavior, updated docs, and focused regression coverage for path ownership and merge precedence.
close_reason: Completed: managed Zellij config now lives under user_configs/zellij with deterministic legacy relocation, fail-fast duplicate detection, docs updates, and regression coverage.

title: Replace repo-centric update commands with a packaged runtime update surface
description:
Yazelix now has a package-ready and flake-installed runtime model, but the user-facing update surface still includes ❌ Failed to check git status: fatal: not a git repository (or any of the parent directories): .git, which only makes sense for clone-based workflows. Replace the repo-centric user update flow with a packaged runtime update command and simplify ℹ️ devenv CLI already matches the Yazelix-pinned version 2.0.7.
❌ Failed to check git status: fatal: not a git repository (or any of the parent directories): .git around real product updates instead of maintainer git operations.
close_reason: Removed yzx update repo, added yzx update runtime, and redefined yzx update all around the packaged runtime plus devenv update flow.

title: Make welcome launch prompt use any key instead of Enter
description: The welcome screen currently says 'Press Enter to launch Zellij and start your session...' and waits for a full line input. Change it to a true single-key continue prompt so the text and behavior match and startup feels snappier.
close_reason: Changed welcome prompt to a true any-key continue

title: replace welcome art with logo. make it work on half screens
description: Imported open GitHub issue #263.
close_reason: Covered by the broader welcome-screen redesign bead yazelix-2k9

title: Audit update devenv and related version-sync flows
description: Verify yzx update devenv and the surrounding version-sync behavior against the current runtime contract. Confirm the command updates to the Yazelix-pinned devenv surface and does not leave version reporting, doctor output, or runtime entrypoints in a contradictory state.
close_reason: Audit completed: yzx update devenv and yzx dev update canaries were verified; canary syntax and temp config writability bugs were fixed.

title: Add Ghostty cursor effects for Zellij pane changes
description: Explore extending the Ghostty cursor-effect system beyond editor mode changes so Yazelix can trigger visual feedback on Zellij pane focus changes, such as ripple/boom effects when switching panes. Cover feasibility, event plumbing, and whether Ghostty shader triggers can be driven cleanly from Zellij/Yazelix without breaking existing cursor behavior.
design: Treat this as an event-plumbing and UX-value question, not just shader enthusiasm. Determine which pane-focus events Yazelix can observe reliably, how those events could trigger Ghostty effects without colliding with editor-mode effects, and whether the result would actually improve navigation clarity rather than becoming distracting visual noise. Prefer a small number of meaningful pane-change effects over an open-ended effect buffet.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear feasibility and UX-value decision for Ghostty cursor effects on Zellij pane changes, including which events can be observed, what effect shapes are worth trying, and whether the feature should move forward, stay experimental, or be dropped.

title: Clarify sidebar vs file tree terminology
description: Clarify Yazelix terminology so `sidebar` remains the generic side-surface term while `file tree` is used for the current Yazi-specific surface where that wording is clearer.
design: Do not perform a literal repo-wide rename from `sidebar` to `filetree`. The current codebase uses `sidebar` as a pane role and workspace contract term, and that should remain the generic abstraction because future side surfaces may not be file trees. The right change is terminology cleanup: preserve `sidebar` for the architectural slot and command surface, while using `file tree` or `Yazi file tree` in user-facing docs or copy when specifically referring to the current Yazi-backed side surface.
acceptance_criteria: The issue is reframed as terminology clarification rather than a mass rename. User-facing wording is clearer about when Yazelix means the generic sidebar surface versus the current Yazi file tree. Internal architectural and plugin-contract language is not renamed in a way that would block future non-Yazi sidebar surfaces.

title: Split restart rebuild requirement from missing launch-profile evidence
description:
`yzx restart` can rebuild the environment twice in one invocation. The first rebuild is the explicit restart-owned `devenv build shell` pass. The second rebuild happens when the subsequent `yzx launch --force-reenter` still sees `needs_refresh = true` only because a verified launch profile has not been recorded yet, even though the environment was already rebuilt moments earlier.

This shows that Yazelix is conflating two different conditions:
- rebuild-required input drift
- missing or unverified reusable launch-profile evidence

Those conditions should not both force a rebuild. A missing verified launch profile should force a slower shell-entry path, not a second `devenv build shell` for the same inputs.
design: Keep this delete-first. Do not paper over the symptom with restart-only flags unless the broader state model remains coherent. The likely direction is to split config-state reporting so callers can distinguish `rebuild required` from `launch profile missing`, then make restart/launch/startup rebuild only on real input drift. Preserve the current ability to fall back from fast profile activation to shell entry when reusable launch evidence is absent.
acceptance_criteria: 1. `yzx restart` performs at most one rebuild for one unchanged config/input state transition. 2. Missing verified launch-profile evidence no longer forces a rebuild by itself after an explicit successful rebuild. 3. Launch and startup paths still fall back cleanly to shell entry when reusable launch-profile evidence is missing. 4. A focused regression test defends the exact double-rebuild failure mode seen in practice.

title: Explain and reduce duplicate shellhook entry during cold startup
description:
The structured cold-start report from yazelix-3n1 shows two distinct shellhook process invocations during shell entry. That may be architecturally justified, but today it is ambiguous and likely contributes avoidable cold-start cost.

Design:
Treat this as a startup-ownership and process-boundary investigation first. Determine why two separate environment.nu shellhook processes run during the cold path, which one is strictly required, and whether one can be deleted or narrowed. Prefer simplifying the flow over adding profiler-only complexity. If both invocations are truly required, document the contract and keep the profiler context clear enough to distinguish them.

Acceptance Criteria:
1. The cold startup path has an explicit explanation for each shellhook process invocation. 2. Unnecessary duplicate shellhook entry is removed if possible; otherwise the surviving duplication is deliberately justified. 3. The startup profile output remains clear about which shellhook process is being timed. 4. Any cleanup does not regress visible rebuild behavior, setup correctness, or noninteractive launch flows.
notes: Completed slice: refreshed startup no longer re-enters `devenv shell` after rebuild. `start_yazelix.nu` now rebuilds once, activates the fresh profile directly, and skips replaying `environment.nu` on that refreshed path. A new default-lane regression defends that ownership boundary. The remaining cold duplicate shellhook contexts were split into follow-up `yazelix-rqz4`, because they survive after this cleanup and appear to come from a deeper cold-path or upstream `devenv` interaction rather than the old Yazelix re-entry path.
close_reason: Completed refreshed-startup cleanup; remaining cold duplicate tracking moved to yazelix-rqz4

title: Expand yzx edit to additional managed surfaces
description: Replace the current narrow yzx edit surface with a picker or fuzzy-query flow for canonical Yazelix-managed config surfaces. Scope for this bead: support config, packs, helix, zellij, and yazi.toml; allow yzx edit with no args to present choices, and yzx edit QUERY to filter or disambiguate managed targets. Keep host shell rc files out of scope. Follow-up bead will cover extra Yazi sub-surfaces like keymap and init.lua.
close_reason: Completed: yzx edit now supports picker or fuzzy-query selection for config, packs, helix, zellij, and yazi.toml managed surfaces.

title: Expand user config control where the merge cost is worth it
description: This is about giving users more control over generated tool configs without turning Yazelix into a fully generic config engine. The clearest remaining gap is terminal config merging: users should be able to layer more of their own terminal-emulator config on top of Yazelix-managed config when the merge model is safe and maintainable.
design: Treat this as an evaluation-first feature, not a promise to toml-ize every config surface. Start by identifying the low-hanging configuration areas where merging is practical and the maintenance cost is acceptable. The current likely target is terminal config merging. Keep the broader idea of a more fully controlled profile as direction, but do not expand this bead to cover `devenv` config control; that should stay in a separate bead. The bar is whether the extra user control is worth the implementation and support complexity.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear decision and scope for which config surfaces should support richer user merging. If terminal config merging is worth it, Yazelix should support it in a maintainable way. If not, the bead should still produce a documented rationale and narrower control model rather than vague ambition about controlling everything. Any implementation path should include unit tests for merge semantics, end-to-end generation checks for the affected config surfaces, and detailed logs showing the user input, managed defaults, and final merged output.
close_reason: Not planned: first-class Ghostty/WezTerm support should expand through explicit Yazelix-owned options and the existing terminal.config_mode ownership boundary, not through a generic terminal-config merge engine.

title: Add Game of Life welcome style
design: Add a Game of Life welcome style seeded or shaped so it feels intentional rather than like a generic programmer screensaver. The motion should be short, bounded, and end in a readable final frame. The implementation may be deterministic live generation or pre-rendered playback, whichever best fits startup and width constraints.
acceptance_criteria: Selecting welcome_style = life renders the Life sequence within the startup budget, preserves narrow-layout readability, and is defended by tests for deterministic generation, frame bounds, and final-frame fit.
close_reason: Completed

title: Fix Alt y failing to reopen sidebar after opening an extra pane in a fresh tab
description: After opening a fresh tab where Yazi starts in the sidebar, opening one extra pane, closing the sidebar with Alt y, and then pressing Alt y again, the sidebar does not reopen. The pane-frame regression is fixed separately; this bead tracks the narrower reopen failure only.
close_reason: The override-layout path now reads the generated runtime layouts directly; this removed the stale widget injection and also fixed the sidebar reopen no-op regression.

title: Continue centralizing Yazelix declarative config semantics onto the canonical contract
description: Yazelix already has a canonical machine-readable config contract in config_metadata/main_config_contract.toml, but some real consumers still duplicate semantics across the default template, Home Manager, parser-adjacent logic, schema/diagnostics-adjacent logic, and rebuild-sensitive ownership sites. Treat this as a delete-first centralization bead: continue moving real consumers onto the canonical contract, delete duplicated semantic ownership that is no longer justified, and make the remaining duplication explicit where it must stay. Build on the already-completed 0oj1 centralization work instead of reopening the problem from scratch.
design: Treat this as a delete-first follow-up to the already completed 0oj1 centralization work, not as a fresh architecture spike. The canonical in-repo TOML contract already exists and several consumers already moved onto it; the remaining job is to identify which real semantic ownership sites still duplicate that contract, migrate at least one meaningful remaining consumer, and delete semantic duplication that is no longer justified. Keep the protocol question pragmatic: only adopt an external protocol if it clearly beats the current in-repo TOML contract for Yazelix's actual consumers and maintenance burden. Related open beads in this lane include b16x.1 for doctor freshness reporting and 53zx.* for staged migration semantics across managed surfaces; this bead should complement those, not reopen their contracts.
acceptance_criteria: The bead records a clear statement of which config-semantic ownership sites are still duplicated after the completed 0oj1 work. At least one meaningful remaining consumer is migrated further onto the canonical contract, reducing real semantic duplication instead of adding abstraction. The design makes an explicit keep-vs-adopt decision on the in-repo TOML contract versus any external protocol, with concrete reasoning tied to Yazelix's real consumers. The resulting repo shape is simpler than before: fewer duplicated semantic ownership sites, not more generation or indirection.
notes: Imported from GitHub issue #516. This should be treated as an umbrella cleanup/centralization bead for the remaining live work after the closed 0oj1.* sequence, not as evidence that the earlier centralization work failed. Likely remaining hotspots to inspect first: home_manager/module.nix, yazelix_default.toml, and any residual hand-maintained semantic lists that still sit outside config_metadata/main_config_contract.toml.

title: Generate the TOML template and Home Manager defaults from the canonical config contract
description: Finish the contract-centralization work by making the canonical machine-readable config contract drive more of the emitted/default surfaces directly. Focus on the remaining duplication across yazelix_default.toml, Home Manager defaults, parser-facing metadata, and similar drift-prone surfaces.
design: The repo now has a real canonical config contract, but too much maintained behavior is still duplicated around it. Treat the contract as the semantic source of truth and push more downstream surfaces to derive from it mechanically. Prefer deleting handwritten parallel default tables over adding more validation glue. Keep human-facing prose and long comments handwritten only where that actually adds value; the maintained semantics should stop living in several files at once.
acceptance_criteria: 1. At least the shipped main config template defaults and Home Manager defaults are generated from, or mechanically derived from, the canonical config contract. 2. Drift-prone duplicated default tables are removed or reduced to thin adapters. 3. Remaining handwritten config surfaces are explicitly justified as prose/presentation rather than semantic source of truth. 4. Follow-up work becomes narrower because the contract now owns more of the maintained semantics.

title: Fix Ghostty shader generation and packaging
description: Ghostty shader assets and generated shader config are still fragile in packaged/runtime installs. Recent failures showed that shader generation, copying, or cleanup can leave Ghostty without the expected shader files even when Yazelix otherwise launches.
design: Treat this as a real generated-asset contract bug. Identify which shader artifacts Ghostty actually needs at runtime, where Yazelix generates or copies them, and why installed-runtime launches can end up with missing, unreadable, or stale shader trees. Fix the asset pipeline at the source instead of relying on local cleanup or manual re-generation.
acceptance_criteria: Installed and clone-based Yazelix launches both materialize the expected Ghostty shader tree. Repeated config generation does not leave broken or empty shader directories. The maintained Ghostty config points only at shader assets that actually exist after generation/install.

title: Repo pass after the Zellij major update for simplifications and new opportunities
description: After the Zellij major update lands, do a repository-wide pass looking for simplifications, workaround removals, and new feature opportunities made practical by the newer Zellij surface.
notes:
Audit snapshot after the Zellij 0.44 upgrade:

Ranked follow-up list:
1. `yazelix-tuj` — replace swap-layout stepping/delay logic with an `override-layout` transition model. This is the clearest architectural simplification because the pane orchestrator still hardcodes layout families and depends on `next_swap_layout()` / `previous_swap_layout()` timing.
2. `yazelix-anc` — evaluate replacing the custom popup-runner plugin with Zellij 0.44 CLI pane control (`list-panes --json`, pane IDs from creation commands, targeted actions). This could remove one Rust/WASM plugin if active-tab popup semantics remain reliable without plugin state.
3. `yazelix-6zx` — document and validate modern Zellij sharing flows for Yazelix, now that the built-in share UI and read-only tokens are first-class in the newer release.

No smaller shipped simplification looked clearly worth landing immediately without broadening scope or adding noisy docs that Zellij already covers in-session.
close_reason: Completed

title: Implement a shared runtime contract checker and reuse it across launch and doctor
description: Implement a shared checker for the runtime dependency contract instead of scattering availability probes and stale assumptions across launch, doctor, and one-off helpers. The checker should be lightweight enough for normal preflight use, classify failures clearly, and expose the same underlying reasoning to yzx doctor. Avoid turning launch into a giant slow audit; the value is one authoritative dependency story with clear required/optional classifications and actionable errors.
close_reason: Duplicate of yazelix-hv2m created during parallel bead creation.

title: investigate FlakeHub Cache and magic-nix-cache
description: Investigate whether FlakeHub Cache or magic-nix-cache would materially benefit Yazelix CI or installs.
close_reason: Not planned after investigation: Yazelix already uses magic-nix-cache in CI, and FlakeHub Cache is not justified right now.

title: Make validate_default_test_budget finish reliably without live Zellij hang paths
description: `nu nushell/scripts/dev/validate_default_test_budget.nu` can hang instead of completing its default-suite budget check. In the observed failure, the validator spawned `test_yzx_commands.nu`, which in turn left a live `zellij action pipe --plugin yazelix_pane_orchestrator` subprocess running, so the budget validator never returned a pass/fail result. This makes the validator unreliable as a maintainer/CI signal: instead of reporting a budget violation or success, it can block indefinitely on a leaked interactive path.
design: Keep this delete-first. Do not just add bigger timeouts around the current validator. First identify whether the validator truly needs to execute the full live path it currently reaches, or whether one default-suite test is accidentally leaking a Zellij/plugin subprocess during profiling. Prefer deleting the interactive dependency from the budget-validation path, or constraining the suite/profile runner so it terminates deterministically even when Zellij-related tests are present. The end state should be a budget validator that either passes or fails clearly; it should not hang waiting on live session plumbing.
acceptance_criteria: 1. `validate_default_test_budget.nu` completes deterministically with a pass/fail result instead of hanging indefinitely. 2. The validator no longer leaves or waits on stray live Zellij `action pipe` or equivalent plugin subprocesses. 3. If the default suite exceeds the allowed budget, the validator fails with a clear budget message rather than stalling. 4. A focused regression or harness-level check defends the specific hang path so it does not silently return via a larger timeout alone.

title: Fix skip_welcome_screen=false being ignored during interactive launch
description: The core.skip_welcome_screen setting is currently being ignored for interactive Yazelix launch flows. Users with skip_welcome_screen = false still do not see the welcome UI.
design: Trace so far: the interactive launch path still calls show_welcome from nushell/scripts/core/start_yazelix_inner.nu, so the setting is not being ignored because the UI code was removed. The likely regression is environment leakage: launch wrappers pass --skip-welcome into the devenv/bootstrap layer, which sets YAZELIX_SKIP_WELCOME=true, and show_welcome treats that as test_mode. The most suspicious paths are nushell/scripts/core/start_yazelix.nu, nushell/scripts/utils/environment_bootstrap.nu, and nushell/scripts/yzx/launch.nu. The likely fix is to keep bootstrap welcome suppression for environment setup while clearing or scoping YAZELIX_SKIP_WELCOME before the real interactive inner session starts.
acceptance_criteria: With core.skip_welcome_screen = false, interactive Yazelix launch shows the welcome screen again. With core.skip_welcome_screen = true, the welcome screen is skipped and the info is logged as before. The fix must not regress env-only, one-shot command, or sweep/test flows that intentionally use YAZELIX_SKIP_WELCOME.
close_reason: Cannot reproduce

title: Auto-assign new GitHub issues to the maintainer
description: Automatically assign new GitHub issues to the maintainer instead of leaving assignment as a manual follow-up step.
design: Treat this as repository workflow automation, not product runtime work. Decide whether GitHub Actions, issue templates, or another automation path is the right place to assign newly opened issues to the maintainer, and verify the permission model before committing to an approach. The solution should be reliable without creating noisy side effects on transferred, reopened, or bot-created issues unless that behavior is explicitly desired.
acceptance_criteria: New issues are assigned to the intended maintainer automatically, or the repo has a documented explanation of why that automation is not supportable. Include validation for the chosen workflow path, with logs or dry-run evidence showing the assignment behavior for representative issue-opening scenarios.

title: verify how to re-enable pane frames without re-introducing the zellij flickering problem
description: Imported GitHub issue #508.
close_reason: Closed on GitHub

title: Fix Ghostty dead keys regression
description: Ghostty dead keys stopped working again in the Yazelix-provided Ghostty path. Restore dead-key composition without changing the default terminal contract or relying on host-specific local workarounds.
design: Treat this as a real input regression in the default Ghostty path, not a cosmetic character-rendering bug. Reproduce the dead-key failure in the Yazelix-provided Ghostty integration, identify whether the breakage comes from wrapper/env handling, config generation, input-method propagation, or another launch-layer change, and restore dead-key composition without falling back to a different terminal or shipping a local-machine workaround.
acceptance_criteria: Dead-key composition works again in the Yazelix-provided Ghostty path. The fix lives in Yazelix itself, preserves Ghostty as the intended default experience, and is verified through the real Ghostty launch path rather than a substitute terminal. The bead records the actual cause so the regression is less likely to recur.
close_reason: Fixed by restoring the managed Ghostty path and moving launch to the current runtime-local Ghostty script. Dead keys now work again in the Yazelix-provided Ghostty path.

title: https://asciimagic.vercel.app
description: Imported GitHub issue #502.
close_reason: Closed on GitHub

title: Spec persistent-session behavior across multiple Yazelix windows
description: Specify how multiple windows behave when `zellij.persistent_sessions = true` and Yazelix reuses one named Zellij session.
design:
Persistent sessions are not just the default behavior with more reuse. They are a different session model: multiple windows become clients of one named logical Zellij session.

This task should define the consequences of that model explicitly:
- once the named session exists, later windows reattach instead of creating fresh live sessions
- `--path` or other fresh-bootstrap intent cannot silently override the already-running session root
- last-client behavior may intentionally differ from non-persistent mode because the point of persistence is to keep the session alive
- restart/update semantics may be session-scoped rather than client-scoped

The goal is to make persistent-session surprises legible and intentional instead of accidental side effects of current Zellij attach behavior.
acceptance_criteria:
1. The docs state that persistent mode means multiple windows attach to one named logical session rather than creating independent sessions.
2. The docs state what happens when a user provides `--path` or similar fresh-start intent after the named persistent session already exists.
3. The docs state whether the last-client lifecycle for persistent sessions intentionally differs from the default non-persistent mode.
4. The docs state that persistent-session restart/update behavior is evaluated at the logical-session scope, not by pretending each attached window is independent.
5. The contract is specific enough that later lifecycle bugs can distinguish an intentional persistent detached session from a default-mode leak.
notes:
This task stays explicit about where persistent behavior intentionally diverges from the default non-persistent contract instead of restating the same rules twice.

Spec delivered:
- `docs/specs/persistent_window_session_contract.md`

Current defense:
- `nu nushell/scripts/dev/validate_specs.nu`
- `nu -c 'source nushell/scripts/dev/test_yzx_workspace_commands.nu; test_launch_here_path_warns_when_existing_persistent_session_ignores_it'`
close_reason: Completed: documented the persistent-session multi-window contract.

title: Audit the full GitHub/Beads backlog contract and define which beads deserve public issues
description: Audit the current backlog to ensure every in-scope GitHub issue maps to exactly one bead, lifecycle state is consistent in both directions, canonical automated comments exist where required, and the repo has an explicit policy for which internal beads should or should not become public GitHub issues. The goal is to clean up the contract and publication boundary for the backlog without imposing a blanket one-to-one rule from beads to GitHub.
design: Treat this as backlog-governance work, not as a mass issue-creation task. Start from the existing GitHub/Beads contract in AGENTS.md and the current imported backlog, then classify beads into public-worthy versus internal-planning-only categories. Repair missing GitHub→Beads mappings and lifecycle mismatches first. Only create new GitHub issues for beads that are genuinely user-visible, externally discussable, or worth tracking publicly. Avoid exporting low-level decomposition, dependency scaffolding, or purely internal planning slices just to satisfy symmetry.
acceptance_criteria: There is a documented and applied policy for which beads should have GitHub issues. Every in-scope GitHub issue maps cleanly to exactly one bead with matching open/closed state and the canonical automated comment. The backlog also has a reviewed list of open beads that intentionally remain internal and therefore do not need public GitHub issues.

title: Define the v14 release gate around packaging completion and repo hardening
description: Capture when Yazelix should actually jump to v14. The major bump should happen only once packaging/distribution feels canonical rather than transitional, and only after the repo/runtime surface has been hardened enough that the packaged story is trustworthy.
design: Treat v14 as a product-era decision, not a cosmetic version bump. The gate should explicitly answer: which distribution surfaces must be finished, whether the flake/Home Manager story is now canonical, and what repo/runtime hardening must be complete before calling Yazelix a packaged product rather than merely package-ready. Keep this bead planning-focused; concrete implementation work should stay in child beads or existing packaging issues.
acceptance_criteria: There is an explicit durable definition of what must be true before v14: the required packaging/distribution milestones, the required repo-hardening expectations, and the concrete remaining beads/issues that satisfy that gate. The decision is specific enough that future release planning can say whether v14 is actually earned.
notes: Initial framing: v14 should mean packaged Yazelix, not just package-ready Yazelix. At minimum, the nixpkgs packaging track and a repo-hardening track should be resolved before the major bump.

title: Add yzx uninstall for installer-owned Yazelix artifacts
description: Add a supported uninstall command for Yazelix. The default path should remove only installer-owned artifacts such as the stable yzx shim, runtime/current, and generated desktop integration, while preserving user-managed config by default. The command should detect Home Manager ownership and refuse or warn instead of deleting paths managed by Home Manager.

title: Add yzx uninstall --all for explicit full cleanup
description: Extend the uninstall surface with an explicit yzx uninstall --all path that removes installer-owned artifacts plus Yazelix state and managed config. Keep this as an intentional destructive path, separate from the safe default uninstall behavior. Document exactly what yzx uninstall --all deletes and keep Home Manager ownership checks in place so Yazelix does not silently fight declarative ownership.

title: Add a supported migration helper for manual-install to Home Manager takeover
description: Provide an explicit supported migration helper for users moving from manual flake install to Home Manager, so they do not have to guess which installer-owned artifacts to move or delete by hand. Reuse installer ownership detection from the uninstall work instead of inventing a second cleanup model.
design: Prefer one ownership engine. If the right UX is a dedicated yzx migrate-to-home-manager command, make it a thin flow over the same installer-owned artifact detection used by yzx uninstall. If a narrower uninstall submode is better, keep the command surface explicit and document exactly which paths it cleans up before Home Manager takes over. Non-goal: deleting Home Manager-managed paths or silently touching user-owned config.
acceptance_criteria: There is a supported command-driven migration path from manual install to Home Manager takeover that removes or hands off installer-owned artifacts blocking Home Manager, especially ~/.local/bin/yzx, without asking the user to guess cleanup steps. The implementation shares ownership logic with the uninstall surface instead of duplicating path rules.

title: Add CI check for README version drift
description: Protect the public README version marker from drifting away from YAZELIX_VERSION. Add one focused check that compares the README title/version line against the version constant and run it in CI.
close_reason: Completed: added README version drift validator to CI and taught yzx dev update to auto-sync the README title/version marker from YAZELIX_VERSION

title: investigate strong flicker when opening a new tab
description:
Imported GitHub issue #468.

### Details

investigate strong flicker when opening a new tab
notes:
Resolved on 2026-03-29:
- Root cause was plugin-driven pane-frame toggling in zjstatus, not tab creation itself.
- Yazelix already enforced `pane_frames false` in the merged Zellij config, while the zjstatus tab template also enabled `hide_frame_for_single_pane "true"`.
- On Zellij 0.44.0 this interaction caused strong flicker/glitching when opening a new tab.
- Fix: remove `hide_frame_for_single_pane` from the managed zjstatus template and from the sweep-test layout fixture so frame ownership stays with the merged Zellij config.
- Verified locally by opening a new tab after the change and by rerunning focused merged-Zellij generation tests.
close_reason: Completed: removed zjstatus frame toggling from the managed template after confirming it was the root cause of the new-tab flicker on Zellij 0.44.0.

title: Add maintainer pack for local maintainer tooling
description: Imported GitHub issue #453 after deciding that maintainer tooling should be an opt-in pack rather than a special yzx dev mode. Add a top-level maintainer pack for repo maintenance tools that are meaningfully tied to maintaining Yazelix itself, starting with gh, prek, tru, beads-rust, and beads-viewer. Keep runtime behavior unchanged, sync the default config and Home Manager module, document the pack in the collection/docs, and add a focused regression test that verifies the pack stays aligned across configuration paths.
close_reason: Added an opt-in maintainer pack with gh, prek, tru, beads-rust, and beads-viewer; synced the default config, Home Manager module, and collection docs; and added a regression test that keeps the pack aligned across both configuration paths.

title: Extract a backend adapter boundary around env, run, refresh, and launch bootstrap
description: Refactor the command/bootstrap layer so the current devenv integration sits behind a clearer backend adapter boundary instead of bleeding directly into user-facing command semantics. Focus on the shared paths now rooted in environment_bootstrap.nu and the yzx env/run/refresh/launch surface. The immediate goal is not to support a second backend in main; it is to make current devenv-specific assumptions explicit, shrink accidental coupling, and leave behind a clearer seam for later experiments or a Core edition.
close_reason: Duplicate of yazelix-m5rk created during parallel bead creation.

title: Fix missing Yazelix plugin permission popup on fresh Zellij startup
description: On fresh Yazelix launches under Zellij 0.44, the pane orchestrator/popup runner permission popup may never appear even after clearing Zellij runtime/cache/share state. Only zjstatus gets a persisted permission block, while Yazelix plugins remain ungranted until permissions are seeded manually in permissions.kdl.
notes: Added repo-level recovery path on 2026-03-27: `yzx repair zellij-permissions` now seeds the tracked/runtime permission blocks for the pane orchestrator and popup runner into ~/.cache/zellij/permissions.kdl and tells the user to restart Yazelix. This is an explicit recovery/workaround path, not the root-cause fix for the missing popup.
close_reason: Superseded by the startup-handoff hardening and permission recovery work on 2026-03-27. The missing Yazelix plugin permission popup was a downstream symptom of the broken Zellij startup path; commit 5043aeb removed the boot-time plugin IPC and duplicate launch-tree hazard, and commit 142e4c8 added an explicit recovery path for stale permission state.

title: Make zjstatus permission state survive runtime updates and relaunches
description: zjstatus can forget its granted permission state after a Yazelix runtime update and then prompt again on the next new window/relaunch. This is not just a desktop-entry bug. Track the broader permission-persistence problem across runtime updates, normal relaunches, and desktop launches so users do not end up with a broken transparent top bar or the Alt+Arrow + y recovery dance.
design: Treat this as a plugin-identity/permission-cache bug, not a prompt-UX quirk. Current evidence: Yazelix already preserves permission blocks for the pane orchestrator and popup runner in ~/.cache/zellij/permissions.kdl, but zjstatus is still loaded from a runtime-scoped wasm path and does not get the same preservation/seed treatment. A runtime update can therefore make Zellij see zjstatus as a new plugin and forget the prior grant. Fix the source of truth so zjstatus permission survives normal runtime updates and relaunches, and only prompt when the user truly has not granted it before. Record whether the fix is path stability, permission-cache migration, explicit seeding, or a combination.

The transparent top bar no longer breaks after runtime updates because zjstatus permission state is preserved. 4. The bead records the exact mechanism that now preserves the permission state.
